A common error is using the wrong day-to-season mapping. > 5. **Truncation in $\cos^{-1}$** — Always state the value in degrees, not radians, and round only at the *final* step. > 6. **Declination on leap year** — If $n$ is given for Feb 29, the formula remains valid but day numbers after Feb shift by 1. Most KTU problems assume a non-leap year ($n$ up to 365). > 7. **Equation of Time in minutes vs. hours** — Convert minutes to hours (÷ 60) before adding to standard time to compute the hour angle. Many students forget this conversion and get the hour angle wrong by 1°–4°. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Revision (Pin this on your wall before the exam):** - ✅ **Solar constant** is always **1367 W/m²** (KTU value), representing extraterrestrial irradiance at 1 AU. - ✅ **Declination** uses Cooper's formula with argument $\dfrac{360(284+n)}{365}$ — peak on **June 21**, zero at equinoxes, trough on **Dec 21**. - ✅ **Hour angle** = $15°$ per hour, **negative in morning**, **zero at noon**, **positive in afternoon**. - ✅ **Altitude** is the Sun's height above the horizon; **zenith** is its complement; $\alpha + \theta_z = 90°$. - ✅ **Azimuth** measured from North; in the **Northern Hemisphere, the Sun is in the southern sky** ($\gamma_s$ between 90° and 270°). - ✅ **Air mass** = $1/\cos\theta_z$ — never less than 1, peaks at sunrise/sunset (theoretically $\infty$). - ✅ **Day length** = $\dfrac{2}{15}\cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$ — longest at summer solstice, shortest at winter solstice. - ✅ **Equation of time** ranges from about **$-14$ minutes to $+16$ minutes** throughout the year. - ✅ **Total solar radiation** $G = I_b + I_d + I_r$ (beam + diffuse + reflected). - ✅ **Optimal annual tilt** of a PV module ≈ **latitude** of the location (a useful quick-estimate for Kerala ≈ 10°). - ✅ **For solar noon calculations**: $\omega = 0$, $\theta_z = \phi - \delta$, so $\cos\theta_z$ is maximum when $\phi \approx \delta$. - ✅ **Units to memorize**: W/m² for irradiance, MJ/m²/day for daily irradiation, μm for wavelength, degrees for angles. - ✅ **Diagrams expected**: A neat celestial-sphere triangle (P, Z, S) fetches full marks on the geometry sub-questions. - ✅ **Just remember**: $\sin\alpha$, $\cos\theta_z$, $\cos\omega_s$ — the trig trio behind 80% of KTU Module 2 problems. ---

topics.title: Solar Radiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET412 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solar Radiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ☀️ SOLAR RADIATION — Section 1: Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Solar Radiation** is the electromagnetic energy emitted by the Sun, propagated through space in the form of electromagnetic waves, and received at the Earth's surface. It is the fundamental primary energy input for almost all renewable energy systems — photovoltaic (PV), solar thermal, solar architecture, biomass, wind, hydro, and ocean thermal.

---

## 1.1 The Sun as an Energy Source — A Physical Snapshot

The Sun is a **G2V-type Yellow Dwarf Star** located at an average distance of **1 AU ≈ 1.496 × 10⁸ km** from Earth. Its core sustains a continuous **thermonuclear fusion** reaction:

$$4 \, ^{1}_{1}\text{H} \;\longrightarrow\; ^{4}_{2}\text{He} \;+\; 2\,e^{+} \;+\; 2\,\nu_{e} \;+\; 26.7 \text{ MeV}$$

Through **mass–energy equivalence** ($E = mc^{2}$), roughly **4.26 × 10⁹ kg of mass is converted to pure energy every second**, yet the Sun has enough hydrogen fuel for another ~5 billion years.

| Solar Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Radius of Sun | **6.96 × 10⁵ km** |
| Surface Temperature | **~5778 K** (effective blackbody) |
| Core Temperature | **~1.5 × 10⁷ K** |
| Total Power Output | **3.846 × 10²⁶ W** |

---

## 1.2 Solar Constant ( $G_{sc}$ )

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOLAR CONSTANT ($G_{sc}$)** is the rate at which solar energy is received **per unit area** on a surface **perpendicular to the Sun's rays**, at the **mean Sun–Earth distance**, **outside the Earth's atmosphere** (i.e., at the outer edge of the atmosphere / AM 0).
> **Accepted Value: $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^{2}$** (WMO 1981 standard) — sometimes given as **1361 W/m²** per newer NASA SORCE measurements.

### 🔆 Intuitive Analogy: "The Fire Hydrant of the Universe"

Imagine the Sun as a giant cosmic *fire hydrant* spewing out light particles (photons) in **every direction**. Earth is like a small ball placed about **150 million km away** from that hydrant. Even at that huge distance, the splashing energy is so intense that, on every square metre of a sun-facing surface at the top of the atmosphere, the falling photons deliver an average power equivalent to **about 14 × 100-watt incandescent bulbs**. This "per-square-metre rate" is what we call the **Solar Constant**.

---

## 1.3 Why the Solar Constant is *Not* Constant

Although the name says "constant", the actual extraterrestrial irradiance received at Earth's orbital distance varies by about **±3.3%** throughout the year because of the elliptical nature of Earth's orbit.

$$\text{Eccentricity of Earth's orbit: } e = 0.0167$$

$$E_{0} = 1 + 0.033 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{360\,n}{365}\right)$$

where $n$ = day of the year and $E_{0}$ = **Eccentricity correction factor (dimensionless)**.

---

## 1.4 Spectrum of Solar Radiation

The Sun's radiation approximates a **blackbody at 5778 K** following Planck's law. The spectrum spans:

- **Ultraviolet (UV):** $\lambda < 0.4 \text{ \mu m}$ — ~8.7% of total energy
- **Visible Light:** $0.4 \text{ \mu m} \le \lambda \le 0.7 \text{ \mu m}$ — ~43% of total energy
- **Infrared (IR):** $\lambda > 0.7 \text{ \mu m}$ — ~48.3% of total energy

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners love asking *"Why is the peak solar emission in the visible region?"* The answer: by **Wien's Displacement Law**, $\lambda_{\text{max}} = 2898/T$ μm = 2898/5778 ≈ **0.501 μm**, which lies in the green–blue region. This is also why plant chlorophyll evolved to absorb in the **PAR (Photosynthetically Active Radiation, 0.4–0.7 μm)** band!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📐 SOLAR RADIATION — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Solar Geometry — Coordinate System of the Sun

To compute the energy falling on a collector at any place and time, we must know **where the Sun is in the sky** relative to the observer. This is described using angles.

### 2.1.1 Solar Declination Angle ($\delta$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Declination angle ($\delta$)** is the angle between the **line joining the Earth's centre to the Sun** and the **equatorial plane**. It varies from **+23.45°** (summer solstice, June 21) to **−23.45°** (winter solstice, Dec 21) to **0°** (equinoxes).

**Cooper's Equation (most commonly used in KTU problems):**

$$\delta = 23.45^{\circ}\,\sin\!\left[\dfrac{360}{365}\,(284 + n)\right]$$

where $n$ = day number (1 = Jan 1, 365 = Dec 31).

### 2.1.2 Hour Angle ($\omega$)

The **hour angle** converts solar time into angular displacement of the Sun across the sky.

$$\omega = 15^{\circ}\,(T_{\text{solar}} - 12) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \omega = (T_{\text{solar}} - 12)\times 15^{\circ}/\text{hr}$$

- At **solar noon**, $\omega = 0^{\circ}$
- Each hour corresponds to **15°** of Earth's rotation
- **Morning:** $\omega < 0$ (negative), **Afternoon:** $\omega > 0$ (positive)

### 2.1.3 Solar Altitude / Elevation Angle ($\alpha$ or $\beta$)

$$\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\,\sin\delta \;+\; \cos\phi\,\cos\delta\,\cos\omega$$

where $\phi$ = **latitude** of the location (positive for Northern hemisphere).

### 2.1.4 Solar Azimuth Angle ($\gamma_{s}$)

$$\sin\gamma_{s} = \dfrac{\cos\delta\,\sin\omega}{\cos\alpha}$$

$$\cos\gamma_{s} = \dfrac{\sin\alpha\,\sin\phi \;-\; \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha\,\cos\phi}$$

### 2.1.5 Zenith Angle ($\theta_{z}$)

The complement of the altitude angle:

$$\theta_{z} = 90^{\circ} - \alpha$$

### 2.1.6 Solar Time ($T_{s}$) — Equation of Time Correction

Civil clock time is **not** solar time. The corrections are:

$$T_{s} = T_{\text{standard}} \;+\; E_{t} \;+\; 4\,(L_{st} - L_{\text{loc}}) \;-\; \text{DST}$$

**Equation of Time ($E_{t}$) in minutes (Spencer's formula):**

$$E_{t} = 229.18\,\bigl[0.000075 + 0.001868\cos B - 0.032077\sin B - 0.014615\cos 2B - 0.04089\sin 2B\bigr]$$

$$B = \dfrac{360\,(n-1)}{365} \quad (\text{in degrees})$$

---

## 2.2 Components of Terrestrial Solar Radiation

Once solar radiation passes through the atmosphere, it is split into three parts:

| Component | Symbol | Cause | Approx. Share (clear sky) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct / Beam radiation** | $I_{b}$ | Travels straight from Sun's disk | ~85% |
| **Diffuse radiation** | $I_{d}$ | Scattered by air molecules, dust, clouds | ~15% |
| **Reflected radiation** | $I_{r}$ | Bounced from ground, water, snow | 0–5% |

**Global Horizontal Irradiance (GHI):**

$$\text{GHI} = I_{b}\cos\theta_{z} + I_{d}$$

**Total Radiation on a Tilted Surface:**

$$I_{T} = I_{b}\cos\theta + I_{d}\!\left(\dfrac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right) + \rho\,I\!\left(\dfrac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}\right)$$

where:
- $\theta$ = **angle of incidence** on the tilted surface
- $\beta$ = **tilt angle** of the surface from horizontal
- $\rho$ = **ground albedo** (0.2 for grass, 0.8 for fresh snow)

---

## 2.3 Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface ($H_{0}$)

$$H_{0} = \dfrac{24 \times 3600}{\pi}\;G_{sc}\,E_{0}\!\left[\omega_{s}\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_{s}\right]$$

(Units: $\text{J/m}^{2}\cdot\text{day}$) where $\omega_{s}$ = **sunset hour angle**.

$$\omega_{s} = \cos^{-1}\!\left(-\tan\phi\,\tan\delta\right)$$

---

## 2.4 Atmospheric Effects — Air Mass (AM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Mass (AM)** is defined as the ratio of the path length of solar radiation through the atmosphere to the path length when the Sun is at the zenith.
> **AM 0** = outside the atmosphere, **AM 1** = Sun directly overhead, **AM 1.5** = standard test condition for PVs.

$$\text{AM} = \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta_{z}} \quad \text{(for } \theta_{z} \le 90^{\circ}\text{)}$$

For an altitude $\alpha$:

$$\text{AM} = \dfrac{1}{\sin\alpha} \quad \text{(approx., valid for } \alpha > 30^{\circ}\text{)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Fact:** Standard PV test conditions are **STC = 1000 W/m², AM 1.5, 25 °C cell temperature** — these are the bench-test numbers stamped on every solar panel datasheet.

---

|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solar Constant | $G_{sc} = 1367$ | W/m² |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar PV System Design:** Predicts power output for any geographic location and any day.
- **Solar Thermal Collectors:** Determines optimal tilt angle and orientation to maximize annual yield.
- **Building Integrated PV (BIPV):** Calculates shading losses from surrounding structures.
- **Agriculture & Crop Modelling:** Estimates PAR for photosynthesis in greenhouses.
- **Climate Modelling & Meteorology:** Inputs for atmospheric heat-balance models.

# 🧮 SOLAR RADIATION — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive-Content Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is shown line-by-line. No steps are skipped. Each derivation block uses `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` formatting.

---

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Solar Altitude Angle ($\alpha$) from the Spherical Triangle

### Statement to Prove
$$\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$$

### Step-by-Step Geometric Derivation

**Step 1 — Set up the Celestial Sphere:**

Consider the Earth at the centre of a celestial sphere of infinite radius. The observer is at latitude $\phi$ (North). The Sun, at a given instant, is at declination $\delta$ and hour angle $\omega$ (measured westward from the meridian).

**Step 2 — Apply the Spherical Cosine Rule:**

In the navigational spherical triangle PZS (Pole–Zenith–Sun), the three sides are:
- Zenith distance: $z = 90^{\circ} - \alpha$ (co-altitude)
- Co-declination: $p = 90^{\circ} - \delta$ (polar distance)
- Co-latitude: $c = 90^{\circ} - \phi$

The included angle at the pole is the **hour angle $\omega$**.

Applying the **cosine rule for a spherical triangle**:

$$\cos z = \cos c \cos p + \sin c \sin p \cos\omega$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the co-angles:**

$$\cos(90^{\circ} - \alpha) = \cos(90^{\circ} - \phi)\cos(90^{\circ} - \delta) + \sin(90^{\circ} - \phi)\sin(90^{\circ} - \delta)\cos\omega$$

**Step 4 — Apply the co-function identity $\cos(90^{\circ} - x) = \sin x$:**

$$\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$$

**Hence Proved.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface ($H_{0}$)

### Statement to Prove
$$H_{0} = \dfrac{24 \times 3600}{\pi}\;G_{sc}\,E_{0}\bigl[\omega_{s}\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_{s}\bigr]$$

### Step-by-Step Integration

**Step 1 — Instantaneous Extraterrestrial Beam Normal Irradiance:**

At the top of the atmosphere, on a surface **normal** to the Sun's rays, the irradiance is:

$$G_{on} = G_{sc}\,E_{0}$$

**Step 2 — Projection onto a Horizontal Surface:**

A horizontal surface receives the beam at angle $\theta_{z}$ to its normal, so the horizontal component is:

$$G_{oh} = G_{on}\cos\theta_{z} = G_{sc}\,E_{0}\cos\theta_{z}$$

**Step 3 — Express $\cos\theta_{z}$ in Terms of $\phi$, $\delta$, $\omega$:**

Since $\theta_{z} = 90^{\circ} - \alpha$, we have $\cos\theta_{z} = \sin\alpha$. Substituting the result of §3.1:

$$G_{oh} = G_{sc}\,E_{0}\bigl[\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega\bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Integrate Over the Day from Sunrise ($\omega = -\omega_{s}$) to Sunset ($\omega = +\omega_{s}$):**

$$H_{0} = \int_{-\omega_{s}}^{+\omega_{s}} G_{oh}\;dt$$

Convert the integration variable from $dt$ to $d\omega$. Since $\omega = 15^{\circ}\cdot(t - 12)$ in degrees, then in **radians**:

$$d\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{24}\;dt \quad\Rightarrow\quad dt = \dfrac{24}{2\pi}\,d\omega = \dfrac{12}{\pi}\,d\omega$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and Integrate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{0} &= \int_{-\omega_{s}}^{+\omega_{s}} G_{sc}\,E_{0}\,\bigl[\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega\bigr]\,\dfrac{12}{\pi}\,d\omega \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{12\,G_{sc}\,E_{0}}{\pi}\int_{-\omega_{s}}^{+\omega_{s}}\bigl[\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega\bigr]\,d\omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Perform the Two Integrals Separately:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{-\omega_{s}}^{+\omega_{s}}\sin\phi\sin\delta\,d\omega &= \sin\phi\sin\delta\,(2\omega_{s}) \\[6pt]
\int_{-\omega_{s}}^{+\omega_{s}}\cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega\,d\omega &= \cos\phi\cos\delta\,[\sin\omega]_{-\omega_{s}}^{+\omega_{s}} \\
&= \cos\phi\cos\delta\,(2\sin\omega_{s})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Multiply by the 12/π Coefficient and Convert Hours to Seconds:**

Total hours in a day = 24 h = **24 × 3600 s**. The constant 12 × 2 / π = 24/π, and multiplying by 3600 to convert radians of time to seconds:

$$H_{0} = \dfrac{24 \times 3600}{\pi}\;G_{sc}\,E_{0}\,\bigl[\omega_{s}\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_{s}\bigr]$$

**Hence Proved.** $\blacksquare$

> [!TIP]
> **Note:** In the formula, $\omega_{s}$ must be expressed in **radians** to get $H_{0}$ in J/m². If you want kWh/m²·day, divide by $3.6 \times 10^{6}$ afterwards.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Angle of Incidence on a Tilted Surface ($\theta$)

### Statement to Prove
$$\begin{aligned}
\cos\theta = &\;\sin\delta\,\sin\phi\,\cos\beta \\
&-\,\sin\delta\,\cos\phi\,\sin\beta\,\cos\gamma \\
&+\,\cos\delta\,\cos\phi\,\cos\beta\,\cos\omega \\
&+\,\cos\delta\,\sin\phi\,\sin\beta\,\cos\gamma\,\cos\omega \\
&+\,\cos\delta\,\sin\beta\,\sin\gamma\,\sin\omega
\end{aligned}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the surface normal vector in the observer's coordinate frame.**

For a tilted surface inclined at angle $\beta$ from the horizontal, oriented at surface azimuth $\gamma$ (measured from South, positive to West):

$$\hat{n} = \bigl[\sin\beta\cos\gamma,\;\sin\beta\sin\gamma,\;\cos\beta\bigr]$$

**Step 2 — Define the Sun's direction unit vector in the same frame:**

$$\hat{s} = \bigl[\cos\delta\cos\omega\cos\phi - \sin\delta\sin\phi,\;\cos\delta\sin\omega,\;\cos\delta\cos\omega\sin\phi + \sin\delta\cos\phi\bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Cosine of Incidence = Dot Product of Normals:**

$$\cos\theta = \hat{n}\cdot\hat{s}$$

Multiplying out the 3 components and collecting like terms in $\sin\delta$ and $\cos\delta$:

$$\cos\theta = \sin\delta\,(\sin\phi\cos\beta - \cos\phi\sin\beta\cos\gamma) + \cos\delta\,(\cos\phi\cos\beta\cos\omega + \sin\phi\sin\beta\cos\gamma\cos\omega + \sin\beta\sin\gamma\sin\omega)$$

This is identical to the form stated. $\blacksquare$

**Special Case Check — Horizontal surface ($\beta = 0^{\circ}$):**

$$\cos\theta = \sin\delta\sin\phi\cos 0 + \cos\delta\cos\phi\cos\omega = \sin\delta\sin\phi + \cos\delta\cos\phi\cos\omega = \cos\theta_{z}$$

Correct! ✓

---

## 3.4 ANALYTICAL PROBLEM-MAPPING MATRIX (For Exam Practice)

| Type of Problem | Key Inputs | Key Formulas Used | Common Trap |
|---|---|---|---|
| Find $\delta$ for a date | Day number $n$ | $\delta = 23.45^{\circ}\sin[360(284+n)/365]$ | Forgetting $n$ in degrees, not radians |
| Find $\omega$ at a clock time | Solar time $T_{s}$ | $\omega = 15(T_{s}-12)$ | Confusing AM (morning negative) and PM (afternoon positive) |
| Find $\omega_{s}$ (sunset hour) | Latitude $\phi$, declination $\delta$ | $\omega_{s} = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$ | Output must be in **radians** for $H_{0}$ |
| Find extraterrestrial $H_{0}$ | $\phi$, $\delta$, $E_{0}$ | Eq. 10 of Formula Sheet | Mixing up radian/degree in $\omega_{s}$ |
| Find incidence on tilted panel | $\phi,\delta,\omega,\beta,\gamma$ | Eq. 9 (the 5-term formula) | Sign convention of $\gamma$ (S=0, W=+, E=-) |
| Compute AM number | Zenith angle $\theta_{z}$ | $\text{AM} = 1/\cos\theta_{z}$ | Thresholding: $\text{AM} = \infty$ at sunrise |

# 🎯 SOLAR RADIATION — Section 5: KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ✏️ Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> Define the following terms with one example each:
> (i) Solar Constant, (ii) Air Mass, (iii) Declination Angle.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Solar Constant ($G_{sc}$):**
The Solar Constant is the rate at which solar energy is received per unit area on a surface **perpendicular** to the Sun's rays, at the **mean Sun–Earth distance**, **outside the Earth's atmosphere**. Its accepted value is **$G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^{2}$**. *Example:* Used as the reference value in satellite solar cell calibration.

**(ii) Air Mass (AM):**
Air Mass is defined as the ratio of the actual path length of sunlight through the atmosphere to the path length when the Sun is at the zenith.
**AM = 1** at zenith, **AM 1.5** is the standard terrestrial test condition for PV modules (corresponds to a zenith angle of ~48.19°). *Example:* AM 1.5 spectrum (ASTM G173) is the standard for rating solar panels.

**(iii) Declination Angle ($\delta$):**
The declination angle is the angular position of the Sun at solar noon with respect to the equatorial plane. It varies from **+23.45°** (Summer Solstice) to **−23.45°** (Winter Solstice).
**Cooper's equation:** $\delta = 23.45^{\circ}\sin[360(284+n)/365]$. *Example:* $\delta = 0°$ on equinox days (March 21, September 23).

---

### ✏️ Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> Differentiate between **beam radiation** and **diffuse radiation**. State the instrument used to measure each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Beam (Direct) Radiation | Diffuse Radiation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Solar radiation travelling in a straight line from the Sun's disk to the Earth's surface | Solar radiation scattered by atmospheric molecules, aerosols, and clouds |
| **Direction** | Collimated, parallel rays | Random, isotropic from the sky hemisphere |
| **Measurement** | **Pyrheliometer** (with a narrow opening tracking the Sun) | **Pyranometer with a shadow band** (to block the direct beam) |
| **Dominant on** | Clear, cloudless days | Cloudy, hazy days |
| **Fraction in clear sky** | ~85% | ~15% |

The **Global** radiation measured by an unshaded pyranometer is the sum: $I_{\text{global}} = I_{b} + I_{d}$.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### 📌 Question B-A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2 Choice A]`

> **B-A (a)** [7 Marks] Derive an expression for the **angle of incidence of solar radiation on a tilted surface** in terms of declination $\delta$, latitude $\phi$, hour angle $\omega$, surface tilt $\beta$, and surface azimuth $\gamma$.
>
> **B-A (b)** [7 Marks] A solar collector is located at **Trivandrum (latitude $\phi = 8.5°$ N)**. On **March 21** ($n = 80$), at **10:00 AM solar time**, compute:
> (i) Declination angle, (ii) Hour angle, (iii) Altitude angle, (iv) Azimuth angle, and (v) Angle of incidence if the panel is tilted at $\beta = 15°$ and oriented with surface azimuth $\gamma = 0°$ (due South).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — B-A (a) — Angle of Incidence Derivation

*(This was fully derived in §3.3. The final expression is reproduced as the answer.)*

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos\theta = &\;\sin\delta\sin\phi\cos\beta \\
&-\sin\delta\cos\phi\sin\beta\cos\gamma \\
&+\cos\delta\cos\phi\cos\beta\cos\omega \\
&+\cos\delta\sin\phi\sin\beta\cos\gamma\cos\omega \\
&+\cos\delta\sin\beta\sin\gamma\sin\omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Derivation Highlights (as in §3.3):**
- Surface normal $\hat{n} = (\sin\beta\cos\gamma,\;\sin\beta\sin\gamma,\;\cos\beta)$
- Sun direction $\hat{s} = (\cos\delta\cos\omega\cos\phi - \sin\delta\sin\phi,\;\cos\delta\sin\omega,\;\cos\delta\cos\omega\sin\phi + \sin\delta\cos\phi)$
- $\cos\theta = \hat{n}\cdot\hat{s}$, then group terms.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — B-A (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** $\phi = 8.5°$, $n = 80$, $T_{s} = 10$ AM, $\beta = 15°$, $\gamma = 0°$.

**(i) Declination Angle:**

$$\delta = 23.45^{\circ}\sin\!\left[\dfrac{360(284 + 80)}{365}\right] = 23.45^{\circ}\sin\!\left[\dfrac{360 \times 364}{365}\right]$$

$$\delta = 23.45^{\circ}\sin(358.85^{\circ}) = 23.45^{\circ}\times(-0.0247) \approx -0.58^{\circ}$$

(Equinox date: very close to zero, as expected!)

**(ii) Hour Angle:**

$$\omega = 15^{\circ}(T_{s} - 12) = 15^{\circ}(10 - 12) = -30^{\circ}$$

(AM → negative value ✓)

**(iii) Altitude Angle:**

$$\sin\alpha = \sin(8.5°)\sin(-0.58°) + \cos(8.5°)\cos(-0.58°)\cos(-30°)$$

$$\sin\alpha = (0.1478)(-0.01012) + (0.9890)(0.99995)(0.8660)$$

$$\sin\alpha = -0.00150 + 0.8564 = 0.8549$$

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.8549) = 58.78^{\circ}$$

**(iv) Azimuth Angle ($\gamma_{s}$):**

$$\cos\gamma_{s} = \dfrac{\sin\alpha\sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha\cos\phi}$$

$$\cos\gamma_{s} = \dfrac{(0.8549)(0.1478) - (-0.01012)}{(0.5187)(0.9890)} = \dfrac{0.1264 + 0.01012}{0.5130} = \dfrac{0.1365}{0.5130} = 0.2661$$

$$\gamma_{s} = \cos^{-1}(0.2661) = 74.57° \text{ (East of South)}$$

**(v) Angle of Incidence on Tilted Surface:**

Using the 5-term formula with $\gamma = 0°$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos\theta &= \sin\delta\sin\phi\cos\beta + \cos\delta\cos\phi\cos\beta\cos\omega \\
&\quad + \cos\delta\sin\phi\sin\beta\cos\gamma\cos\omega \\
&\quad - \sin\delta\cos\phi\sin\beta\cos\gamma
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting $\gamma = 0°$ (so $\cos\gamma = 1$, and the $\sin\gamma$ terms vanish):

$$\cos\theta = \cos\beta(\sin\delta\sin\phi + \cos\delta\cos\phi\cos\omega) + \sin\beta(\cos\delta\sin\phi\cos\omega - \sin\delta\cos\phi)$$

Plugging in numbers:

$$\cos\theta = \cos 15°\,[\underbrace{(-0.01012)(0.1478) + (0.99995)(0.9890)(0.8660)}_{=0.8549 = \sin\alpha}] + \sin 15°\,[\underbrace{(0.99995)(0.1478)(0.8660) - (-0.01012)(0.9890)}_{=0.1281 + 0.0100 = 0.1381}]$$

$$\cos\theta = 0.9659 \times 0.8549 + 0.2588 \times 0.1381$$

$$\cos\theta = 0.8257 + 0.0357 = 0.8614$$

$$\boxed{\theta = \cos^{-1}(0.8614) \approx 30.59^{\circ}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B-A):**
> - Many students **forget to convert** $E_{t}$ from minutes to hours when computing solar time → mark loss.
> - The sign convention of $\omega$ is **NOT optional**: AM is negative, PM is positive. Flipping this gives wrong altitude/incidence.
> - When $\gamma = 0°$ (panel facing South), students often forget to **drop the $\sin\gamma$ terms** (which are zero) and keep the $\cos\gamma$ terms.
> - The hour angle in Cooper's declination formula is in **degrees**; in $H_{0}$ integration, it must be in **radians**.

---

### 📌 Question B-B — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2 Choice B]`

> **B-B (a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **different components of solar radiation reaching the Earth's surface**. Derive the expression for **total radiation on a tilted surface** for an isotropic sky.
>
> **B-B (b)** [7 Marks] For a location at latitude $\phi = 28.6°$ N (Delhi), the **mean daily extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface** is to be calculated. On the **autumnal equinox** ($n = 266$, $\delta = 0°$), the **eccentricity correction factor** is $E_{0} = 0.985$. Compute:
> (i) Sunset hour angle, (ii) Day length, (iii) Daily extraterrestrial radiation $H_{0}$, in **kWh/m²·day**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — B-B (a)

**Components of Solar Radiation at Earth's Surface:**

1. **Direct (Beam) radiation ($I_{b}$):** Radiation received directly from the Sun's disk without being scattered. Travels in a straight line.
2. **Diffuse radiation ($I_{d}$):** Solar radiation scattered by air molecules, dust, water vapour, and clouds. Comes from the entire sky dome.
3. **Reflected radiation ($I_{r}$):** Solar radiation reflected from the ground, water bodies, or snow into the collector surface.

**Total Radiation on a Tilted Surface — Isotropic Sky Derivation:**

For a tilted surface making angle $\beta$ with the horizontal:

**Step 1 — Direct beam component:**
The beam irradiance on a tilted surface = beam on horizontal × geometric factor:
$$I_{b,T} = I_{b,n}\cos\theta = I_{b,h}\dfrac{\cos\theta}{\cos\theta_{z}}$$

**Step 2 — Diffuse component on a tilted surface (isotropic sky):**

Assume the sky is a uniform **Lambertian source** of diffuse radiation $I_{d,h}$ on the horizontal. The fraction of sky hemisphere "seen" by a tilted surface is the **view factor** $(1 + \cos\beta)/2$. Hence:

$$I_{d,T} = I_{d,h}\,\dfrac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Ground-reflected component:**

The horizontal surface receives total $I_{h} = I_{b,h} + I_{d,h}$. Of this, a fraction $\rho$ (albedo) is reflected. The tilted surface sees the ground at view factor $(1 - \cos\beta)/2$:

$$I_{r,T} = \rho\,I_{h}\,\dfrac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Total radiation on the tilted surface:**

$$I_{T} = I_{b,n}\cos\theta + I_{d,h}\!\left(\dfrac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right) + \rho I_{h}\!\left(\dfrac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}\right)$$

**Hence Proved.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** *Isotropic sky* means the diffuse radiance is the same in all directions of the sky hemisphere. This is the **Liu & Jordan model (1963)**. It overestimates in the circumsolar region; for higher accuracy, **Hay–Davies** or **Perez** anisotropic models are used.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — B-B (b) — Numerical Computation at Delhi

**Given:** $\phi = 28.6°$, $n = 266$, $\delta = 0°$, $E_{0} = 0.985$, $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^{2}$.

**(i) Sunset Hour Angle:**

$$\omega_{s} = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta) = \cos^{-1}(-\tan 28.6° \times \tan 0°) = \cos^{-1}(0) = 90^{\circ} = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad}$$

**(ii) Day Length:**

$$\text{Day length} = \dfrac{2\,\omega_{s}}{15°/\text{hr}} = \dfrac{2 \times 90°}{15°/\text{hr}} = 12 \text{ hours}$$

(Equinox → day and night are equal everywhere on Earth ✓)

**(iii) Daily Extraterrestrial Radiation $H_{0}$:**

Using the formula derived in §3.2:

$$H_{0} = \dfrac{24 \times 3600}{\pi}\;G_{sc}\,E_{0}\bigl[\omega_{s}\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_{s}\bigr]$$

With $\delta = 0°$: $\sin\delta = 0$, $\cos\delta = 1$, $\sin\omega_{s} = 1$, $\omega_{s} = \pi/2$.

$$H_{0} = \dfrac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} \times 1367 \times 0.985 \times \bigl[0 + \cos(28.6°) \times 1\bigr]$$

$$H_{0} = \dfrac{86400}{\pi} \times 1346.4 \times \cos(28.6°)$$

$$H_{0} = 27501.6 \times 1346.4 \times 0.8779$$

$$H_{0} = 32{,}524{,}680 \text{ J/m}^{2}\cdot\text{day}$$

Converting to **kWh/m²·day** (divide by $3.6 \times 10^6$):

$$\boxed{H_{0} \approx 9.03 \text{ kWh/m}^{2}\cdot\text{day}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B-B):**
> - Forgetting to convert **radians ↔ degrees** in $\omega_{s}$: a 57.3× error is **very common**.
> - On equinox ($\delta = 0°$), students sometimes compute $\omega_{s}$ incorrectly because $\tan 0° = 0$ — they get $\cos^{-1}(0) = 90°$ ✓ — but carelessly write it as "$\pi$" instead of "$\pi/2$".
> - The unit-conversion factor $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$ is **almost always asked explicitly**; skipping it costs 1 mark.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Memorise Sheet for the Night Before the Exam:**

- ☑ **Solar Constant** = 1367 W/m²; WMO 1981 standard.
- ☑ **Eccentricity factor** $E_{0} = 1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365)$; accounts for Earth's elliptical orbit.
- ☑ **Declination** by **Cooper's equation**: $\delta = 23.45°\sin[360(284+n)/365]$. Range = ±23.45°.
- ☑ **Hour angle** $\omega = 15°(T_{s} - 12)$; **−ve in AM, +ve in PM**.
- ☑ **Sunset hour angle** $\omega_{s} = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$; gives **half-day-length × 15°**.
- ☑ **Altitude** $\alpha$, **Zenith** $\theta_{z} = 90° - \alpha$, **Azimuth** $\gamma_{s}$ — all in **degrees**.
- ☑ **Air Mass** $\text{AM} = 1/\cos\theta_{z}$; **AM 1.5** is the universal PV test condition.
- ☑ **3 Components of terrestrial solar radiation:** **Beam + Diffuse + Reflected**.
- ☑ **Total on tilted surface:** $I_{T} = I_{b}\cos\theta + I_{d}(1+\cos\beta)/2 + \rho I (1-\cos\beta)/2$.
- ☑ **Extraterrestrial daily radiation** $H_{0}$ is a definite integral from $-\omega_{s}$ to $+\omega_{s}$.
- ☑ **Instruments:** **Pyranometer** for global & diffuse; **Pyrheliometer** for direct beam.
- ☑ **Wien's Law:** $\lambda_{\text{max}} = 2898/T(\text{K})$ → 0.501 μm (green) for the Sun.
- ☑ **STC for PV:** 1000 W/m², AM 1.5, 25 °C.
- ☑ **Remember to convert** $\omega$ between **radians (for integration)** and **degrees (for trig table lookup)**.

> 🏁 **End of Topic — Solar Radiation. All the best for your KTU 2024 ESE!**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Solar Radiation on Horizontal and Inclined Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET412 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Solar Radiation on Horizontal and Inclined Surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Solar Radiation — The Engine of Every Photovoltaic and Thermal System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Solar radiation reaching the Earth's surface is the fundamental primary energy input for all solar energy conversion systems. Its accurate estimation on horizontal and inclined (tilted) surfaces is the first and most critical step in the design of PV arrays, solar thermal collectors, and concentrator systems.*

In the context of **PEEET412 — Renewable Energy Sources**, "estimation of solar radiation" refers to the mathematical and empirical procedures used to **predict or calculate the amount of solar irradiance (W/m²)** and **insolation (Wh/m² or kWh/m² per day)** available on a given surface — either **horizontal** (flat on the ground) or **inclined** (tilted toward the sun) — at a specific location, time, and orientation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Moving Spotlight"

Imagine you are inside a dark room holding a piece of paper. Someone outside is shining a flashlight (the Sun) through a small window. The brightness (intensity) on your paper depends on:

1. **How powerful the flashlight is** → corresponds to the **solar constant** ($G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$).
2. **How far the paper is from the window** → corresponds to the **Earth–Sun distance** variation through the year.
3. **The angle at which the light strikes the paper** → corresponds to the **angle of incidence** ($\theta$). When the light hits the paper *perpendicularly*, the energy is concentrated on a small area (high intensity). When the light hits at a *slant*, the same energy is spread over a larger area (low intensity). This is governed by the **cosine law**: $G = G_{direct} \cos\theta$.
4. **Whether clouds or dust are in the way** → corresponds to **atmospheric attenuation**, giving rise to **direct** and **diffuse** components.

Now, if you **tilt the paper** (install a solar panel at an angle), the area that the light "sees" changes — and the total light energy collected can either *increase* (if tilted toward the sun) or *decrease* (if tilted away). This is exactly what we calculate when we estimate radiation on an **inclined surface**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Numerical Values (Memorize for KTU Exam):**
> - **Solar Constant:** $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$ (often rounded to 1361 or 1366 in newer references).
> - **Mean Earth–Sun Distance:** $d_m = 1.496 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}$ (1 AU).
> - **Earth's Axial Tilt (Obliquity):** $\varepsilon = 23.45°$.
> - **Standard Air Mass:** AM1.5 = 1.5 (spectral reference for PV rating).
> - **Albedo of Ground (typical grass/snow):** $\rho_g = 0.2 \text{ (grass)}, 0.8 \text{ (fresh snow)}$.
> - **Tropical Latitude Range (Kerala):** $\phi \approx 8°\text{ N} - 12.5°\text{ N}$.

### 1.4 The Three Components of Terrestrial Solar Radiation

| Component | Symbol | Cause | Typical Share (Clear Sky, Tropical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct (Beam) Radiation** | $G_b$ | Rays travelling straight from the Sun's disk | 70 – 85 % |
| **Diffuse (Sky) Radiation** | $G_d$ | Scattered by molecules, aerosols, clouds | 15 – 30 % |
| **Ground-Reflected Radiation** | $G_r$ | Reflected from ground onto tilted surface | 0 – 5 % (tilt) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Radiation on a Tilted Surface:**
> $$G_T = G_{b,T} + G_{d,T} + G_{r,T}$$
> This deceptively simple equation is the **central equation** of Module 2. Every formula, model, and derivation in the rest of this section is built to evaluate each of these three terms.

### 1.5 Why Inclined Surfaces Matter

A horizontal surface is convenient for measurement but **not optimal** for collection. By tilting a surface:

- The **angle of incidence** with the sun's rays is reduced (approaches $0°$).
- The **cosine loss** is minimized.
- For a location in the northern hemisphere, the **optimum tilt** roughly equals the **latitude angle** (annual optimum) or $\phi - 15°$ in summer and $\phi + 15°$ in winter.
- For Kerala ($\phi \approx 10°\text{N}$), the optimum annual tilt is small ($10° - 12°$), but monthly optima vary between $0°$ (June) and $25°$ (December).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:**
> Students frequently forget that the **total radiation on a tilted surface is NOT simply the horizontal radiation multiplied by $\cos\theta$**. That relation only applies to the *direct beam* component. The diffuse and ground-reflected components require separate (and more complex) models. This is one of the most common places students lose marks in KTU ESE papers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Solar Geometry — The Foundation

Before estimating any radiation, we must first define the **position of the Sun** relative to an observer on Earth. This is described by several angles, all functions of the **day of the year** ($n$) and the **solar time** (or hour angle, $\omega$).

### 2.1.1 Declination Angle ($\delta$)

The declination is the angular position of the Sun north or south of the equator. It varies from $-23.45°$ (winter solstice) to $+23.45°$ (summer solstice).

$$\delta = 23.45° \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right]$$

where $n$ is the day number (Jan 1 = 1, Dec 31 = 365).

### 2.1.2 Hour Angle ($\omega$)

The hour angle measures time relative to solar noon. It is **zero at solar noon**, **negative in the morning**, and **positive in the afternoon**.

$$\omega = 15° \times (\text{solar time in hours} - 12)$$

So, at 9 AM solar time, $\omega = -45°$; at 3 PM solar time, $\omega = +45°$.

### 2.1.3 Solar Time and the Equation of Time

A clock measures **standard (mean) time**, but the Sun moves slightly non-uniformly due to Earth's elliptical orbit and axial tilt. The correction is given by the **Equation of Time (EoT)**:

$$EoT = 9.87 \sin(2B) - 7.53 \cos(B) - 1.5 \sin(B)$$

where $B = \frac{360}{365}(n - 81)$ in degrees.

The **Solar Time** is then:

$$\text{Solar Time} = \text{Standard Time} + EoT + 4(\text{Longitude}_{std} - \text{Longitude}_{loc}) - \text{DST}$$

### 2.1.4 Zenith, Altitude, and Azimuth Angles

- **Zenith Angle** $\theta_z$ — angle between the sun's rays and the vertical (local zenith).
- **Altitude Angle** $\alpha$ — angle between the sun's rays and the horizontal.
- **Azimuth Angle** $\gamma_s$ — compass direction of the sun's projection on the horizontal plane (measured from North, clockwise).

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

$$\alpha = 90° - \theta_z$$

$$\cos\gamma_s = \frac{\sin\alpha \sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha \cos\phi} \quad (\text{sign convention varies})$$

### 2.2 Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface

The radiation incident on a surface **outside the Earth's atmosphere** on a plane perpendicular to the sun's rays is:

$$G_{on} = G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360n}{365}\right)$$

The factor $1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365)$ is the **eccentricity correction factor** ($E_0$), accounting for the variation in Earth–Sun distance.

The **extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface** is then:

$$G_{0} = G_{on} \cos\theta_z = G_{sc}\,E_0\,(\sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega)$$

### 2.3 Hourly, Daily, and Monthly Insolation

The **hourly extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface** $I_0$ (Wh/m²) is obtained by integrating $G_0$ over one hour centered on solar time $t$:

$$I_0 = \frac{12 \times 3600}{\pi}\, G_{sc}\,E_0\, \left[ \cos\phi \cos\delta (\sin\omega_2 - \sin\omega_1) + \frac{\pi}{180}(\omega_2 - \omega_1)\sin\phi \sin\delta \right]$$

The **daily extraterrestrial radiation** $H_0$ (MJ/m²·day) is:

$$H_0 = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi}\, G_{sc}\,E_0\, \left[ \cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi}{180}\omega_s \sin\phi \sin\delta \right]$$

where $\omega_s$ is the **sunset hour angle**:

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$$

### 2.4 Clearness Index and Diffuse Fraction

Two dimensionless indices are critical for estimating terrestrial radiation from extraterrestrial values:

**Atmospheric (or Clearness) Index:**
$$k_T = \frac{H}{H_0}$$
where $H$ is the measured daily global radiation on a horizontal surface.

**Diffuse Fraction ($k_d$):**
$$k_d = \frac{H_d}{H}$$
where $H_d$ is the diffuse component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these indices matter:** Because we rarely have direct measurements of diffuse radiation at a given site, we use **empirical correlations** (e.g., Erbs, Liu & Jordan, Page) that relate $k_d$ to $k_T$. Once $k_d$ is known, we can separate $H$ into $H_b = H - H_d$ and $H_d$.

### 2.5 Angle of Incidence on a Tilted Surface

For a surface tilted at angle $\beta$ from horizontal, with surface azimuth $\gamma$ (measured from South, positive to West), the **angle of incidence** is:

$$\cos\theta = \sin\delta \sin\phi \cos\beta - \sin\delta \cos\phi \sin\beta \cos\gamma + \cos\delta \cos\phi \cos\beta \cos\omega + \cos\delta \sin\phi \sin\beta \cos\gamma \cos\omega + \cos\delta \sin\beta \sin\gamma \sin\omega$$

A useful **simplified form** (for south-facing surface in northern hemisphere, $\gamma = 0$):

$$\cos\theta = \sin\delta \sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos\delta \cos(\phi - \beta) \cos\omega$$

For a **horizontal surface** ($\beta = 0$), this reduces to:

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

For a surface **tilted at latitude** ($\beta = \phi$) and facing the equator ($\gamma = 0$):

$$\cos\theta = \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

This is the famous "**$\cos\delta \cos\omega$**" — meaning the cosine loss depends only on the hour angle and declination, and the annual average is maximized.

### 2.6 The Three Components on a Tilted Surface

#### 2.6.1 Direct (Beam) Component on a Tilted Surface
$$G_{b,T} = G_b \cdot R_b = G_b \frac{\cos\theta}{\cos\theta_z}$$

where $R_b$ is the **tilt factor** (or geometric ratio) for beam radiation.

#### 2.6.2 Diffuse Component — Three Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favourite Topic:** The diffuse radiation model is the most frequently asked sub-question in KTU ESE Module 2. Know all three models below.

**Model 1: Isotropic Sky (Liu & Jordan, 1963)**
Assumes the sky radiates uniformly. Ground reflection is also isotropic.

$$G_{d,T} = G_d \frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}$$

$$G_{r,T} = G \cdot \rho_g \frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}$$

**Total tilted radiation (Liu & Jordan):**
$$G_T = G_b R_b + G_d \frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2} + G \rho_g \frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}$$

**Model 2: Hay-Davies (1978) — Anisotropic**
Accounts for the **forward scattering** (circumsolar) component.

$$G_{d,T} = G_d \left[ A_i \frac{G_b}{G_{on}} + (1 - A_i)\frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2} \right]$$

where the **anisotropy index** is:

$$A_i = \frac{G_b}{G_{on}} = \frac{G_b}{G_{sc} E_0}$$

**Model 3: Perez (1990) — Most Accurate**
Adds horizon brightening correction coefficients; too complex for KTU derivations, but **name the model** for marks.

#### 2.6.3 Ground-Reflected Component
$$G_{r,T} = G \cdot \rho_g \cdot \frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}$$

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **This table is your exam-day lifeline. Memorize the units, boundary conditions, and sign conventions.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Declination | $\delta = 23.45° \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right]$ | $-23.45° \le \delta \le +23.45°$ |
| 2 | Hour Angle | $\omega = 15°(t_{solar} - 12)$ | $-180° \le \omega \le +180°$ |
| 3 | Equation of Time | $EoT = 9.87 \sin 2B - 7.53 \cos B - 1.5 \sin B$ | minutes, $B = \frac{360}{365}(n-81)$ |
| 4 | Eccentricity Factor | $E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cos(360n/365)$ | $0.967 \le E_0 \le 1.033$ |
| 5 | Extraterrestrial (on surface normal) | $G_{on} = G_{sc} E_0$ | W/m², $G_{sc} = 1367$ |
| 6 | Extraterrestrial (horizontal) | $G_0 = G_{on} \cos\theta_z$ | W/m² |
| 7 | Zenith Angle | $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$ | $0° \le \theta_z \le 90°$ |
| 8 | Sunset Hour Angle | $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$ | degrees |
| 9 | Daily Extraterrestrial | $H_0 = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} G_{sc} E_0 [\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi}{180}\omega_s \sin\phi \sin\delta]$ | J/m²·day |
| 10 | Clearness Index | $k_T = H / H_0$ | $0 \le k_T \le 0.75$ (typical) |
| 11 | Diffuse Fraction | $k_d = H_d / H$ | $0.1 \le k_d \le 0.9$ |
| 12 | Angle of Incidence (general) | See Section 2.5 (full expression) | degrees |
| 13 | Angle of Incidence (south-facing) | $\cos\theta = \sin\delta \sin(\phi-\beta) + \cos\delta \cos(\phi-\beta)\cos\omega$ | degrees |
| 14 | Beam Tilt Factor | $R_b = \cos\theta / \cos\theta_z$ | dimensionless |
| 15 | Tilted Beam | $G_{b,T} = G_b R_b$ | W/m² |
| 16 | Tilted Diffuse (Liu & Jordan) | $G_{d,T} = G_d (1 + \cos\beta)/2$ | W/m² |
| 17 | Tilted Ground-Reflected | $G_{r,T} = G \rho_g (1 - \cos\beta)/2$ | W/m² |
| 18 | Total Tilted (Liu & Jordan) | $G_T = G_b R_b + G_d \frac{1+\cos\beta}{2} + G \rho_g \frac{1-\cos\beta}{2}$ | W/m² |
| 19 | Hay-Davies Anisotropy Index | $A_i = G_b / G_{on}$ | $0 \le A_i \le 1$ |
| 20 | Optimum Tilt (annual) | $\beta_{opt} \approx \phi$ (for tropical) | degrees |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PV System Design (KTU CO1, CO2):** The tilted radiation $G_T$ directly determines the kWh output of a PV array. The performance ratio of a 1 MW plant in Kerala is computed from monthly $H_T$ values.
- **Solar Water Heater Sizing (FPC, ETC):** Thermal gain $Q_u = A_c F_R [S - U_L(T_{in} - T_a)]$ — where $S$ is the absorbed tilted radiation.
- **Building-Integrated PV (BIPV):** Vertical walls and roof slopes require $G_{d,T}$ estimation with anisotropic models for accurate yield prediction.
- **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP):** Requires direct normal irradiance (DNI) — only the beam component is focused; diffuse radiation is lost from the optical system.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Zenith Angle from Basic Trigonometry

We start with the **celestial triangle** connecting the **North Celestial Pole (P)**, the **Observer's Zenith (Z)**, and the **Sun (S)**. In this spherical triangle, the sides are:
- $Z-S = 90° - \alpha = \theta_z$ (co-altitude)
- $Z-P = 90° - \phi$ (co-latitude)
- $P-S = 90° - \delta$ (co-declination)
- The angle at $P$ is the hour angle $\omega$.

Applying the **spherical law of cosines** (analogous to the planar law of cosines but for spherical surfaces):

$$\cos(P-S) = \cos(Z-S)\cos(Z-P) + \sin(Z-S)\sin(Z-P)\cos(\text{angle at } Z)$$

But the angle at $Z$ is the azimuth $\gamma_s$, not zero. Instead, apply the law at the **Pole** vertex. The standard form used in solar geometry is:

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

**Step-by-step expansion:**

Start with the **cosine rule for the zenith angle**:

$$\cos\theta_z = \cos(90° - \phi)\cos(90° - \delta) + \sin(90° - \phi)\sin(90° - \delta)\cos\omega$$

Apply the identity $\cos(90° - x) = \sin x$ and $\sin(90° - x) = \cos x$:

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

This is the **fundamental zenith-angle equation**. **Q.E.D.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sunset Hour Angle

At sunset, the **altitude angle $\alpha = 0$**, which means $\theta_z = 90°$, i.e., $\cos\theta_z = 0$.

Starting from the zenith angle equation:

$$0 = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega_s$$

Solve for $\cos\omega_s$:

$$\cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega_s = -\sin\phi \sin\delta$$

$$\cos\omega_s = -\frac{\sin\phi \sin\delta}{\cos\phi \cos\delta} = -\tan\phi \tan\delta$$

Therefore:

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$$

**Validity check:**
- At equator ($\phi = 0$): $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(0) = 90°$ → 6 AM to 6 PM, **12-hour day** always. ✓
- At Tropic of Cancer, summer solstice ($\phi = 23.45°$, $\delta = 23.45°$): $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-1) = 180°$ → 24-hour daylight. ✓
- At polar circle, winter solstice ($\phi = 66.5°$, $\delta = -23.45°$): $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(+1) = 0°$ → 0 hours of daylight (polar night). ✓

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Daily Extraterrestrial Radiation $H_0$

The **instantaneous extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface** is:

$$G_0 = G_{sc} E_0 (\sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega)$$

To get the **daily** total, integrate from sunrise ($-\omega_s$) to sunset ($+\omega_s$):

$$H_0 = \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} G_0 \, dt$$

Since $\omega$ is in radians and $dt$ relates to $d\omega$ by $d\omega = \frac{2\pi}{24} \, dt = \frac{\pi}{12} \, dt$ (i.e., 15°/hour), we have $dt = \frac{12}{\pi} d\omega$.

Substituting:

$$H_0 = \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} G_{sc} E_0 (\sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega) \cdot \frac{12}{\pi}\, d\omega$$

The first term $\sin\phi \sin\delta$ is constant in $\omega$:

$$\int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} \sin\phi \sin\delta \cdot \frac{12}{\pi}\, d\omega = \frac{12}{\pi} \sin\phi \sin\delta \cdot (2\omega_s) = \frac{24\omega_s}{\pi} \sin\phi \sin\delta$$

The second term:

$$\int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega \cdot \frac{12}{\pi}\, d\omega = \frac{12}{\pi} \cos\phi \cos\delta \cdot [\sin\omega]_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} = \frac{24}{\pi} \cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s$$

Combining (and converting $H_0$ to standard units of J/m²·day by multiplying by 3600 seconds/hour × 24 hours is implicit, OR keeping it in Wh/m²·day):

$$H_0 = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} \, G_{sc} E_0 \left[ \cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi}{180} \omega_s \sin\phi \sin\delta \right]$$

where the $\frac{\pi}{180}$ converts $\omega_s$ from degrees to radians (only if $\omega_s$ is in degrees in your calculation).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Beam Tilt Factor $R_b$

The **hourly beam radiation on a horizontal surface** is:

$$G_b = G_{b,n} \cos\theta_z$$

where $G_{b,n}$ is the **direct normal irradiance** (DNI). On a tilted surface:

$$G_{b,T} = G_{b,n} \cos\theta$$

Dividing:

$$\frac{G_{b,T}}{G_b} = \frac{G_{b,n} \cos\theta}{G_{b,n} \cos\theta_z} = \frac{\cos\theta}{\cos\theta_z} \equiv R_b$$

For **daily averages**, the same ratio holds for the **daily beam radiation on tilted vs horizontal surfaces** when both are integrated over the day (assuming a constant $G_{b,n}$ throughout the day):

$$R_b = \frac{H_{b,T}}{H_b} = \frac{\cos\theta}{\cos\theta_z} \bigg|_{avg}$$

A more precise daily version involves **integrating the cosine terms**:

$$R_b = \frac{\int \cos\theta \, d\omega}{\int \cos\theta_z \, d\omega} = \frac{\cos(\phi-\beta)\cos\delta \sin\omega_{s,T} + \frac{\pi}{180}\omega_{s,T}\sin(\phi-\beta)\sin\delta}{\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi}{180}\omega_s \sin\phi \sin\delta}$$

where $\omega_{s,T}$ is the sunset hour angle for the tilted surface.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Total Tilted Radiation (Liu & Jordan Model)

We start with the **three additive components**:

$$G_T = G_{b,T} + G_{d,T} + G_{r,T}$$

**Step 1 — Beam component:**

$$G_{b,T} = G_b R_b = G_b \frac{\cos\theta}{\cos\theta_z}$$

**Step 2 — Diffuse component (isotropic sky assumption):**

The diffuse radiation on a horizontal surface is $G_d$. On a tilted surface, the **view factor** of the sky from the tilted plane is $(1 + \cos\beta)/2$. So:

$$G_{d,T} = G_d \cdot \frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Ground-reflected component:**

The **view factor** of the ground as seen from the tilted surface is $(1 - \cos\beta)/2$. The radiation incident on the ground is the global horizontal radiation $G$, of which a fraction $\rho_g$ is reflected. So:

$$G_{r,T} = G \cdot \rho_g \cdot \frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}$$

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{G_T = G_b R_b + G_d \frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2} + G \rho_g \frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}}$$

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a model KTU numerical. Practice until you can reproduce it in 6 minutes.**

**Problem:** A location has latitude $\phi = 10°\text{N}$, day number $n = 172$ (June 21). The clearness index $k_T = 0.65$, and the diffuse fraction $k_d = 0.25$. Find the **total radiation on a south-facing surface tilted at $\beta = 10°$** at solar noon. Use $\rho_g = 0.2$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Declination:**
$$\delta = 23.45° \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 172)\right] = 23.45° \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(456)\right]$$
$$= 23.45° \sin(449.75°) = 23.45° \sin(89.75°) \approx 23.45° \times 0.99999 \approx 23.45°$$

**Step 2 — At solar noon, $\omega = 0$:**

For the horizontal surface:
$$\cos\theta_z = \sin(10°)\sin(23.45°) + \cos(10°)\cos(23.45°)\cos(0°)$$
$$= 0.1736 \times 0.3979 + 0.9848 \times 0.9174 \times 1 = 0.0691 + 0.9034 = 0.9725$$
$$\theta_z = 13.50°$$

For the tilted surface (south-facing, $\gamma = 0$, $\beta = 10°$):
$$\cos\theta = \sin(23.45°)\sin(10° - 10°) + \cos(23.45°)\cos(10° - 10°)\cos(0)$$
$$= \sin(23.45°)\sin(0°) + \cos(23.45°)\cos(0°) \times 1$$
$$= 0 + 0.9174 = 0.9174$$
$$\theta = 23.45°$$

**Step 3 — Beam tilt factor:**
$$R_b = \frac{\cos\theta}{\cos\theta_z} = \frac{0.9174}{0.9725} = 0.9434$$

**Step 4 — Components of global horizontal radiation** (normalized to $G = 1$ kW/m² for the example):

- $G = 1.0$
- $G_d = k_d \cdot G = 0.25 \times 1.0 = 0.25$ kW/m²
- $G_b = G - G_d = 0.75$ kW/m²

**Step 5 — Total tilted radiation (Liu & Jordan):**
$$G_T = G_b R_b + G_d \frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2} + G \rho_g \frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}$$

Compute each term:
- $G_b R_b = 0.75 \times 0.9434 = 0.7076$
- $G_d \frac{1 + \cos(10°)}{2} = 0.25 \times \frac{1 + 0.9848}{2} = 0.25 \times 0.9924 = 0.2481$
- $G \rho_g \frac{1 - \cos(10°)}{2} = 1.0 \times 0.2 \times \frac{1 - 0.9848}{2} = 0.2 \times 0.0076 = 0.0015$

**Sum:**
$$G_T = 0.7076 + 0.2481 + 0.0015 = 0.9572 \text{ kW/m}^2$$

**Interpretation:** A 10° tilt at solar noon in June at 10°N **reduces** the collected radiation slightly compared to a horizontal surface (which receives 1.0 kW/m²). This is because at this latitude/season, the sun is nearly overhead and the horizontal surface is already nearly optimal.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Model

| Condition | Recommended Model | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Clear sky, tropical, low latitude (Kerala) | **Liu & Jordan** (isotropic) | Diffuse fraction is small; isotropic assumption holds adequately |
| Partly cloudy, mid-latitudes | **Hay-Davies** | Captures circumsolar brightening; $A_i$ is significant |
| Overcast, high-latitudes | **Isotropic + larger $k_d$** | Sky is nearly uniform; no circumsolar peak |
| Concentrated PV / CSP | **Direct normal only** | Only the beam component is usable; estimate DNI from $G_{b,n}$ measurements |
| Building-integrated (vertical walls) | **Perez (1990)** | Horizon brightening matters; vertical surface sees much horizon |

```mermaid
    B1 --> C
    B2 --> C

    C1 --> D[RADIATION COMPONENTS]
    C2 --> D
    D --> D1[Direct beam on tilted surface G_bT]
    D --> D2[Diffuse on tilted surface G_dT]

    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E

```

## 4.2 Radiation Component Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Extraterrestrial Radiation G_on] --> B[Eccentricity correction E0]
    B --> C[G_sc times E0 equals 1367 times factor]
    C --> D[Atmospheric attenuation]
    D --> E[Direct beam G_b]
    D --> F[Diffuse sky G_d]
    D --> G[Global on horizontal G equals G_b plus G_d]
    G --> H[Tilt conversion step]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    H --> H1[Beam on tilted G_bT equals G_b R_b]
    H --> H2[Diffuse on tilted G_dT equals G_d times 1 plus cos beta over 2]
    H --> H3[Ground reflected G_rT equals G rho_g times 1 minus cos beta over 2]
    H1 --> I[TOTAL TILTED R_T]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    Q2 -->|Maximum accuracy needed| M4[Perez 1990]

```
