    CO2A[Atmospheric CO2] --> PhotoA
    H2OA[Soil Water] --> PhotoA
    PhotoA --> CarbA[Carbohydrates Glucose Starch Cellulose]
    CarbA --> BioA[Biomass Feedstock]

    BioA --> ConA3[Physicochemical Conversion]
    ConA1 --> OutA1[Heat Char Gas Liquid Fuel]
    ConA2 --> OutA2[Biogas Ethanol Methanol]
    ConA3 --> OutA3[Biodiesel Pellets Briquettes]

```

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Question A1:** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
> **Define biomass. List any four categories of biomass with one example each.**

### Model Answer:
Biomass is the biodegradable organic matter derived from plants, animals, and microorganisms, which can be converted into useful forms of energy through thermochemical, biochemical, or mechanical routes.

Four categories:
1. **Forest biomass** – e.g., firewood, sawdust.
2. **Agricultural biomass / residues** – e.g., rice straw, sugarcane bagasse.
3. **Animal waste** – e.g., cattle dung.
4. **Aquatic biomass** – e.g., water hyacinth, microalgae.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Question A2:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
> **Distinguish between Higher Calorific Value (HCV) and Lower Calorific Value (LCV) of a biomass fuel. Which one is used in practice and why?**

### Model Answer:

| Parameter | HCV | LCV |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Definition | Heat released with water vapour fully condensed | Heat released with water vapour escaping as flue gas |
| Magnitude | Higher | Lower (HCV − latent heat) |
| Use | Theoretical / lab measurement | Practical engineering applications |

In practice, **LCV is used** because in real boilers and engines, the flue gases exit at temperatures > 100 °C, and the latent heat of water vapour cannot be recovered economically.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students often *forget to subtract O/8 from H in Dulong's formula* — losing 4 marks. Also, mixing HCV and LCV values in heat balance problems is the most common error. Always state whether your fuel composition is *air-dried* or *oven-dried* (dry basis). Examiners award **methodology marks** — show every step, even for stoichiometric ratios.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Jul 2023, Model Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **classification of biomass resources**. Discuss the role of biomass in India's renewable energy mix. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A biomass sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass:
C = 42 %, H = 5 %, O = 38 %, N = 0.5 %, S = 0.5 %, Ash = 14 %.
Calculate its **HCV and LCV** using Dulong's formula. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Classification of Biomass Resources**

Biomass resources are broadly classified into five categories, as illustrated below.

| Sl. No. | Category | Source / Examples | Energy Use |
|---------|----------|-------------------|------------|
| 1 | Forest biomass | Firewood, sawdust, bark | Direct combustion, gasification |
| 2 | Agricultural residues | Rice husk, sugarcane bagasse | Cogeneration, briquettes |
| 3 | Energy crops (dedicated) | Jatropha, switchgrass | Biodiesel, power generation |
| 4 | Animal waste | Cattle dung, poultry waste | Biogas via anaerobic digestion |
| 5 | Municipal / Industrial waste | MSW, sewage sludge | Incineration, landfill gas |

**Role of Biomass in India's Energy Mix:**

- India produces approximately **500 million tonnes** of crop residues annually, of which ≈ 140 million tonnes are surplus.
- Installed biomass power capacity: ≈ **10,200 MW** (MNRE 2024).
- Estimated potential: ≈ **25,000 MW** from biomass alone.
- Supports **Clean Development Mechanism (CDM)** and rural employment.
- Enables **decentralized generation**, reducing T&D losses.

**(b) HCV and LCV Calculation**

**Given:**
C = 42 %, H = 5 %, O = 38 %, N = 0.5 %, S = 0.5 %, Ash = 14 %.

**Step 1: Apply Dulong's formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HCV &= 0.338 \, C + 1.428 \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 0.095 \, S \\
HCV &= 0.338 \times 42 + 1.428 \left(5 - \dfrac{38}{8}\right) + 0.095 \times 0.5 \\
HCV &= 14.196 + 1.428 \times (5 - 4.75) + 0.0475 \\
HCV &= 14.196 + 1.428 \times 0.25 + 0.0475 \\
HCV &= 14.196 + 0.357 + 0.0475 \\
HCV &= 14.601 \; \text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate LCV.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LCV &= HCV - 0.09 \, H \\
LCV &= 14.601 - 0.09 \times 5 \\
LCV &= 14.601 - 0.45 \\
LCV &= 14.151 \; \text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{HCV = 14.601 \; \text{MJ/kg}, \quad LCV = 14.151 \; \text{MJ/kg}}$$

The relatively low LCV is explained by the high ash content (14 %) and the partial oxidation of hydrogen by oxygen within the fuel.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Pattern]**

**(a)** What is biomass? Explain the **process of photosynthesis** with the governing chemical equation. Comment on the **carbon-neutral** nature of biomass. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The ultimate analysis of a bagasse sample yields:
C = 45 %, H = 6 %, O = 43 %, N = 0.2 %, S = 0.1 %, Ash = 5.7 %.
If the moisture content is 10 %, determine the **dry-basis and as-fired calorific value**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Biomass, Photosynthesis, and Carbon Neutrality**

**Definition:**
Biomass is the biodegradable organic matter from biological origin that can be converted into bioenergy via thermochemical, biochemical, or physicochemical routes.

**Photosynthesis Equation:**

$$6CO_2 + 6H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Sunlight, Chlorophyll}} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2$$

The chlorophyll pigment in leaves absorbs photons (typically in the red and blue wavelengths), enabling plants to combine CO₂ and H₂O into glucose. The free energy change is **ΔG° = +2870 kJ/mol**.

**Carbon Neutrality:**
- During growth, the plant absorbs **CO₂** from the atmosphere.
- During combustion or bio-conversion, **CO₂** is released back.
- Over one full growth–combustion cycle, the net CO₂ added to the atmosphere is **zero**.
- Hence biomass is termed *carbon neutral* (modulo small processing losses).

**(b) Dry-basis and As-fired Calorific Value**

**Given (dry basis):** C = 45 %, H = 6 %, O = 43 %, N = 0.2 %, S = 0.1 %, Ash = 5.7 %.

**Step 1: HCV on dry basis using Dulong's formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HCV_{\text{dry}} &= 0.338 \times 45 + 1.428 \left(6 - \dfrac{43}{8}\right) + 0.095 \times 0.1 \\
HCV_{\text{dry}} &= 15.21 + 1.428 \times (6 - 5.375) + 0.0095 \\
HCV_{\text{dry}} &= 15.21 + 1.428 \times 0.625 + 0.0095 \\
HCV_{\text{dry}} &= 15.21 + 0.8925 + 0.0095 \\
HCV_{\text{dry}} &= 16.112 \; \text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: LCV on dry basis.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LCV_{\text{dry}} &= 16.112 - 0.09 \times 6 \\
LCV_{\text{dry}} &= 16.112 - 0.54 \\
LCV_{\text{dry}} &= 15.572 \; \text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: As-fired calorific value (10 % moisture, M = 0.10).**

The as-fired (or wet-basis) calorific value is reduced by the latent heat carried away by the moisture in the fuel, plus the moisture formed from H combustion. A simplified but KTU-acceptable correction is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
LCV_{\text{wet}} &= LCV_{\text{dry}} \times (1 - M) - 2.442 \times M \\
LCV_{\text{wet}} &= 15.572 \times 0.90 - 2.442 \times 0.10 \\
LCV_{\text{wet}} &= 14.015 - 0.244 \\
LCV_{\text{wet}} &= 13.771 \; \text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where 2.442 MJ/kg is the latent heat of vaporization of water at 25 °C.

**Result:**

$$\boxed{LCV_{\text{dry}} = 15.572 \; \text{MJ/kg}, \quad LCV_{\text{as-fired}} \approx 13.77 \; \text{MJ/kg}}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Biomass Resources**

- ☐ **Biomass = biodegradable organic matter** — plant/animal origin, renewable, non-fossil.
- ☐ **Photosynthesis** traps ≈ 0.1 – 1 % of incident solar energy at the field level.
- ☐ **5 main categories**: Forest, Agricultural, Animal, Aquatic, Industrial/Municipal.
- ☐ **Proximate analysis** tells *how it burns*; **Ultimate analysis** tells *what elements are present*.
- ☐ **HCV** includes latent heat of water; **LCV** does not. Use LCV for practical calculations.
- ☐ **Dulong's formula** (Board-must):

$$\boxed{HCV = 0.338 \, C + 1.428 \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 0.095 \, S}$$

- ☐ Conversion shortcut: **LCV ≈ HCV − 0.09 H** (use dry-basis H).
- ☐ **High ash & moisture** = poor biomass; **higher lignin + cellulose** = better for combustion, **higher sugar/starch** = better for fermentation.
- ☐ **Woody biomass** has LCV ≈ 15 – 17 MJ/kg; **Agro-residues** ≈ 12 – 15 MJ/kg; **Cattle dung** ≈ 13 – 16 MJ/kg.
- ☐ **Carbon-neutral cycle**: CO₂ absorbed during growth ≈ CO₂ released during combustion.
- ☐ India's biomass potential ≈ **25,000 MW**; installed ≈ **10,000+ MW** (MNRE 2024).
- ☐ Always convert to **dry basis** before applying Dulong's formula unless explicitly asked.
- ☐ Photosynthesis equation must be memorized in the standard form with stoichiometric coefficients.
- ☐ Sugar-based crops → ethanol pathway; oil-based crops → biodiesel pathway; lignocellulosic → gasification / pyrolysis pathway.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biomass Conversion Technologies - Fixed Dome Type Biogas Plant
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET412 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Biomass Conversion Technologies - Fixed Dome Type Biogas Plant

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fixed Dome Type Biogas Plant** (also known as the **Chinese Biogas Plant** or **Constant Volume Biogas Plant**) is a *masonry/cast-in-situ underground anaerobic digestion unit* in which the digester chamber, the gas collection dome, and the displacement chamber are permanently fixed in volume. Biogas generated by microbial action accumulates in the sealed upper dome and exerts pressure on the slurry, forcing the digested effluent upwards into a **compensation/expansion tank**. The plant has *no moving metal parts*, depends on the hydraulic displacement principle, and is typically constructed from brick, stone, or reinforced concrete.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Fixed Dome plant is a **mandatory topic** under Module 3 (Biomass Conversion Technologies). Students must memorize the labelled diagram, the function of each component, and the standard sizing formulae (Retention Time, Gas Yield, Daily Feed).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine blowing air into a **rigid, sealed water tank** that has a small overflow pipe. The air pressure inside pushes the water level down a little, and the displaced water rises into a side-cup connected to the tank. Now replace *air* with **biogas** produced by rotting cow-dung, and replace the *side-cup* with the **expansion/compensation chamber**. The dome is your sealed tank, the slurry is your water, and the biogas is the air. The higher the gas pressure, the more slurry is pushed up. **Gas pressure is therefore NOT constant — it varies with the amount of gas produced and consumed.** This is the essence of the fixed dome plant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike the **Floating Drum (KVIC) plant**, the gas pressure here is *variable* (typically 0.7–1.2 m water column). The plant works purely on **hydraulic displacement** — no metal gasholder, no corrosion, lifetime often exceeds **20 years**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Memorize)

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Plant Capacity (household range) | **2 m³ to 20 m³** |
| Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) | **30 – 50 days** |
| Gas Yield (cow dung, TS ≈ 8 %) | **0.036 m³/kg of wet dung** ≈ 0.20–0.50 m³/kg VS |
| Operating Temperature | **Mesophilic: 25 – 40 °C** (Optimum **35 °C**) |
| Gas Pressure (variable) | **0.7 – 1.2 m H₂O column** |
| Methane (CH₄) content | **55 – 65 %** |
| CO₂ content | **30 – 40 %** |
| Design life | **20 – 25 years** |
| pH range | **6.8 – 7.5** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Components & Function

A fixed dome plant consists of **eight primary components**, each performing a specific biochemical or hydraulic role:

1. **Mixing/Feeding Tank (Inlet)** — Fresh dung slurry (cow-dung : water = **1 : 1** by weight) is mixed here and fed into the digester.
2. **Inlet Pipe (250 mm dia.)** — Delivers slurry from the mixing tank into the digester chamber.
3. **Digester Chamber (Main Reactor)** — The airtight masonry/RC chamber where **anaerobic fermentation** occurs in four stages:
   - **Hydrolysis** (complex organics → monomers)
   - **Acidogenesis** (monomers → volatile fatty acids)
   - **Acetogenesis** (VFA → acetic acid + H₂ + CO₂)
   - **Methanogenesis** (CH₃COOH → CH₄ + CO₂)
4. **Fixed Gas Dome (Storage/Collection)** — The upper curved portion acts as the gas holder with **variable pressure**.
5. **Displacement Chamber / Expansion Chamber** — The slurry pushed up by accumulated gas collects here temporarily.
6. **Outlet Pipe** — Connects the displacement chamber back to the main digester for overflow of digested slurry.
7. **Compensation Tank (Outlet/Overflow)** — The digested slurry overflows out of the plant from this tank. It is **also the gas-pressure-indicator** — its height varies with gas production.
8. **Gas Outlet Pipe with Valve** — Takes the biogas to the point of use (stove, lamp, engine).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The compensation tank is the *visual pressure gauge*. If the slurry level in it rises abnormally high → **gas is being produced but not used** (over-pressure risk). If it stays low → **leakage or low gas production**.

## 2.2 Working Principle (Why & How)

- **Why does gas accumulate at the top?** The digester is sealed; biogas (CH₄ + CO₂), being lighter and insoluble, rises and collects in the fixed dome.
- **How is displacement achieved?** Accumulating gas exerts hydrostatic pressure on the slurry surface in the digester → pushes slurry down → displaces an equal volume of slurry up through the central dividing wall into the expansion chamber.
- **Why is pressure variable?** Volume of gas storage is fixed (rigid dome), so as gas production varies through the day (higher in afternoon due to temperature), pressure fluctuates.

## 2.3 Construction & Material Specification

- **Dome geometry:** Spherical or elliptical; built using **brick masonry + cement mortar (1:3)** with inner plaster.
- **Excavation depth:** Below ground level to maintain **mesophilic temperature** (~constant 25–30 °C year-round in tropical Kerala).
- **Waterproofing:** 2 coats of bitumen or waterproof cement coating.
- **Manhole cover:** Steel-reinforced concrete cover for periodic cleaning.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{d} = Q \times t$ | Digester volume = daily slurry feed × hydraulic retention time | m³ |
| 2 | $Q = \dfrac{N \times M}{D}$ | Daily feed = (No. of animals × dung/animal/day) / dilution | m³/day |
| 3 | $G = M_{d} \times G_{c}$ | Daily gas production = dry matter input × specific gas yield | m³/day |
| 4 | $V_{s} = G \times T_{s}$ | Gas storage volume = daily gas × hours of storage (≈ 12 h) | m³ |
| 5 | $V_{total} = V_{d} + V_{g}$ | Total plant volume (digester + gas space) | m³ |
| 6 | $\eta_{CH_4} = \dfrac{V_{CH_4}}{V_{total\,gas}}$ | Methane fraction of total gas | dimensionless |
| 7 | $CV_{biogas} = 0.55 \times 35.8 + 0.45 \times 0$ | Calorific value (CH₄ 55 %, CO₂ 45 %) ≈ **19.7 MJ/m³** | MJ/m³ |
| 8 | $\rho_{slurry} \approx 1000 - 1090$ | Slurry density (kg/m³) | kg/m³ |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Fixed dome plants are widely deployed in **rural electrification (biogas-based generators), institutional cooking, and rural sanitation (linked to household toilets in India's "Swachh Bharat" initiative)**. In Kerala, they are popular in dairy farms, hostels, and as decentralized waste-to-energy units. Engineers design them using **Sinha & Annapurna's standard designs** (2 m³ to 60 m³ family/community models).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Sizing a Household Fixed Dome Plant

**Statement:** A family of 6 owns 4 adult cows. Each cow produces **25 kg/day** of dung. Cow dung is mixed with water in **1:1 ratio** (by weight). The plant is to operate at a **retention time of 40 days** and must store **12 hours** of gas production. The gas yield is **0.036 m³ per kg of wet dung**. Determine:
- (a) Daily slurry feed $Q$
- (b) Digester volume $V_d$
- (c) Daily gas production $G$
- (d) Gas storage volume $V_s$
- (e) Total plant volume $V_{total}$

### (a) Daily Slurry Feed $Q$

Total dung produced per day:
$$M_{wet} = 4 \times 25 = 100 \ \text{kg/day}$$

After mixing with water 1:1, total slurry mass = 100 + 100 = 200 kg. Using $\rho_{slurry} \approx 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ (dilute slurry):

$$Q = \dfrac{M_{slurry}}{\rho_{slurry}} = \dfrac{200}{1000} = 0.20 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

### (b) Digester Volume $V_d$

Using retention time $t = 40$ days:
$$V_d = Q \times t$$
$$V_d = 0.20 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day} \times 40 \ \text{days} = 8.0 \ \text{m}^3$$

### (c) Daily Gas Production $G$

$$G = M_{wet} \times G_c = 100 \ \text{kg/day} \times 0.036 \ \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$
$$G = 3.6 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

### (d) Gas Storage Volume $V_s$

For $T_s = 12$ hours = 0.5 day:
$$V_s = G \times T_s = 3.6 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day} \times 0.5 \ \text{day} = 1.8 \ \text{m}^3$$

### (e) Total Plant Volume $V_{total}$

$$V_{total} = V_d + V_s = 8.0 + 1.8 = 9.8 \ \text{m}^3 \approx 10 \ \text{m}^3$$

> **Engineering Decision:** A standard **10 m³ Sinha-model fixed dome plant** is recommended. The dome occupies ~20–25 % of the digester volume for gas storage, hence $V_s$ (1.8 m³) is consistent with this proportion.

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Verification of Gas Storage Proportion

**Statement:** Show that the gas storage volume in a fixed dome plant typically occupies **20–25 %** of the total digester volume.

### Derivation

By convention, gas storage is sized for **half a day's** production:
$$T_s = 0.5 \ \text{day}$$

Daily gas production from $V_d$ cubic metres of slurry is:
$$G = V_d \times \rho_{slurry} \times Y \times \eta$$

where $Y$ = gas yield (m³/kg dry matter) and $\eta$ ≈ volatile solid conversion.

Simplified empirical relation from field data:
$$\dfrac{V_s}{V_d} = 0.20 \ \text{to} \ 0.25$$

Plugging in for our Problem 1:
$$\dfrac{V_s}{V_d} = \dfrac{1.8}{8.0} = 0.225 = 22.5\ \% \ \ \checkmark$$

> This validates the standard design rule. If the ratio falls below 0.20, gas will be **wasted through the expansion chamber overflow**; if above 0.25, the dome is *under-utilized* and construction cost is unnecessarily high.

## 3.3 Problem 3 — Energy Output Estimation

**Statement:** From Problem 1, calculate the daily useful thermal energy available from the biogas, given calorific value = 19.7 MJ/m³ and stove thermal efficiency = 55 %.

### Derivation

Daily chemical energy in gas:
$$E_{chem} = G \times CV = 3.6 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day} \times 19.7 \ \text{MJ/m}^3$$
$$E_{chem} = 70.92 \ \text{MJ/day}$$

Useful thermal energy:
$$E_{useful} = \eta_{stove} \times E_{chem} = 0.55 \times 70.92$$
$$E_{useful} = 39.0 \ \text{MJ/day}$$

Converting to kWh ($1 \ \text{kWh} = 3.6 \ \text{MJ}$):
$$E_{useful} = \dfrac{39.0}{3.6} \approx 10.83 \ \text{kWh/day}$$

**Equivalent:** Enough to cook **3 meals/day for a family of 6** (typical requirement: 2.5–3.0 m³ of biogas per meal).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of a Fixed Dome Biogas Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mixing Tank - Cow Dung and Water 1:1] --> B[Inlet Pipe 250 mm dia]
    B --> C[Digester Chamber - Anaerobic Reactor]
    C --> D[Hydrolysis and Acidogenesis]
    D --> E[Acetogenesis and Methanogenesis]
    E --> F[Fixed Gas Dome - Variable Pressure Storage]
    F --> G[Gas Outlet Pipe with Valve]
    G --> H[End Use: Stove / Lamp / Engine]
    F --> I[Displacement Chamber - Slurry Rises]
    I --> J[Compensation Tank - Pressure Indicator]
    J --> K[Overflow Digestate - Used as Bio-manure]
    C --> L[Outlet Pipe]
    L --> J
```

## 4.2 Component-Level Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputStage
        A1[Animal Dung] --> A2[Water 1:1]
        A2 --> A3[Slurry]
    end
    subgraph ReactorStage
        A3 --> B1[Inlet]
        B1 --> B2[Main Digester]
        B2 --> B3[Fixed Dome]
    end
    subgraph OutputStage
        B3 --> C1[Gas Pipe]
        C1 --> C2[Utilization]
        B2 --> D1[Displacement]
        D1 --> D2[Compensation Tank]
        D2 --> D3[Overflow Digestate]
    end
```

## 4.3 Sectional Schematic (Layout Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Underground
        U1[Digester Chamber - Masonry RC] 
        U2[Fixed Gas Dome - Top Curved]
        U3[Central Dividing Wall]
        U4[Displacement Chamber]
        U5[Compensation Tank]
    end
    IN[Inlet Pipe] --> U1
    U1 -->|biogas rises| U2
    U2 -->|gas out| GAS[Gas Outlet Valve]
    U1 -->|slurry pushed| U3
    U3 --> U4
    U4 --> U5
    U5 -->|overflow| OUT[Digestate to Field]
    GAS --> UTIL[Stove / Lamp / Genset]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram (for KTU 14-mark answers):**
> - The **central dividing wall** is critical — it separates the *active* digester zone (where fresh slurry enters) from the *displacement* zone (where used slurry is pushed up). The two chambers are hydraulically connected only at the **bottom**, ensuring that fermentation and gas collection are not disturbed by fresh feed.
> - The **compensation tank** is *physically outside* the underground dome, typically at ground level, and its slurry level serves as a **manometer** for gas pressure.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] *List any three advantages and two disadvantages of a fixed dome biogas plant.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Advantages:**
1. Long service life — masonry/RC construction lasts **20–25 years** with no corrosion.
2. No moving parts → low maintenance, no need for metal gasholder replacement.
3. Underground construction → stable mesophilic temperature, aesthetic appeal, structurally stable in soil.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Gas pressure is **variable**, requiring a **pressure regulator** for appliances.
2. Skilled masonry labour is required for construction; leak detection in masonry domes is difficult.

*(1 mark per advantage/point + 0.5 mark each for disadvantages = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] *What is the role of the compensation tank in a fixed dome biogas plant?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The compensation tank (or expansion chamber) serves three functions:
1. **Acts as a hydraulic pressure indicator** — the height of the slurry in this tank reflects the current gas pressure inside the dome. (1 mark)
2. **Stores displaced slurry** — when gas accumulates, the slurry is pushed up into this chamber, providing space for the gas. (1 mark)
3. **Allows overflow of digested slurry** (digestate) to the outside, which is then collected as bio-manure. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]

**Q. A. (a)** With a neat labelled schematic, explain the **construction and working principle** of a **Fixed Dome Type Biogas Plant**. List its major components. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A fixed dome biogas plant is constructed entirely underground using **brick masonry or reinforced concrete**. The plant comprises:
- A **mixing tank** at ground level for preparing the 1:1 (dung:water) slurry.
- An **inlet pipe** (typically 250 mm diameter) feeding the digester.
- The **digester chamber** — the main fermentation vessel, built spherical or cylindrical.
- The **fixed gas dome** — the upper hemispherical portion, sealed airtight and acting as a permanent gas holder.
- A **central dividing wall** separating the digester into two hydraulically connected halves.
- A **displacement chamber** connected to the digester through the dividing wall at the bottom.
- A **compensation tank** at ground level.
- A **gas outlet pipe with valve** leading to the point of use.
- An **outlet pipe** that allows digested slurry to flow out.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
- Cow dung slurry is fed into the digester through the inlet pipe (1).
- Inside, **anaerobic bacteria** carry out fermentation in four biochemical stages, producing **biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)**.
- Biogas, being lighter and insoluble, rises and accumulates in the fixed dome.
- As gas volume increases, it exerts **hydrostatic pressure** on the slurry, pushing the slurry down on one side of the central wall and **up into the displacement chamber** on the other side.
- The displaced slurry rises into the **compensation tank**; when the tank overflows, **digestate** exits the plant.
- Biogas is drawn through the outlet pipe to the point of use.
- Pressure fluctuates with gas production and consumption.

**Labelling (1 mark):** All eight components are correctly labelled on the schematic.

---

**Q. A. (b)** A household has **6 cattle** producing **20 kg dung/day each**. The dung is mixed with water in **1:1 ratio by weight**. Hydraulic retention time is **50 days**; gas yield is **0.04 m³/kg of wet dung**. Gas is stored for **10 hours**. Calculate: *(i) daily slurry volume, (ii) digester volume, (iii) daily gas production, and (iv) total plant volume.* *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** N = 6 cattle; dung = 20 kg/day each; Dung:Water = 1:1; HRT = 50 d; $G_c$ = 0.04 m³/kg; $T_s$ = 10 h.

**(i) Daily Slurry Volume (2 marks):**
$$M_{wet} = 6 \times 20 = 120 \ \text{kg/day}$$
$$M_{slurry} = 120 + 120 = 240 \ \text{kg/day}$$
$$Q = \dfrac{240}{1000} = 0.24 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**(ii) Digester Volume (2 marks):**
$$V_d = Q \times t = 0.24 \times 50 = 12.0 \ \text{m}^3$$

**(iii) Daily Gas Production (2 marks):**
$$G = M_{wet} \times G_c = 120 \times 0.04 = 4.8 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**(iv) Total Plant Volume (1 mark):**
$$V_s = G \times T_s = 4.8 \times \dfrac{10}{24} = 2.0 \ \text{m}^3$$
$$V_{total} = V_d + V_s = 12.0 + 2.0 = 14.0 \ \text{m}^3$$

**Recommended plant size: 14 m³** (closest standard size in Sinha designs).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative Choice]*

**Q. B. (a)** Compare the **Fixed Dome plant** and the **Floating Drum (KVIC) plant** under the following heads: principle, gas pressure, life, cost, maintenance, and suitability. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Fixed Dome Plant | Floating Drum (KVIC) Plant |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Hydraulic displacement, constant volume | Gas collected in a floating metal drum |
| **Gas Pressure** | **Variable** (0.7–1.2 m H₂O) | **Constant** (drum weight = pressure) |
| **Life** | **20 – 25 years** | **10 – 15 years** (drum corrodes) |
| **Initial Cost** | Higher (skilled masonry) | Moderate (prefabricated metal) |
| **Maintenance** | Low (no moving parts) | Moderate (painting drum, greasing) |
| **Gas Yield Indication** | Indirect (slurry level in comp. tank) | Direct (drum height) |
| **Suitability** | Long-term rural/hot climates | Coastal, short-term deployments |
| **Construction Skill** | Skilled mason required | Semi-skilled, factory components |

*(Distribute 1 mark per correctly compared head × 6 = 6 marks; 1 mark for overall conclusion/justification.)*

---

**Q. B. (b)** Discuss the **four biochemical stages** of anaerobic digestion inside the fixed dome reactor. Indicate the **optimum pH** and **temperature** for each stage. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Stage 1 — Hydrolysis (2 marks):**
Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are broken down by extracellular enzymes into soluble monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids). **Optimum pH = 6.0–6.5**; mesophilic conditions (30–40 °C). Performed by *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*.

**Stage 2 — Acidogenesis (2 marks):**
Monomers are converted by **acidogenic bacteria** into volatile fatty acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂, and CO₂. **Optimum pH = 5.5–6.5**. This stage produces acidity; if the system is overloaded, pH drops sharply and inhibits methanogens.

**Stage 3 — Acetogenesis (1 mark):**
VFAs and alcohols are converted by **acetogenic bacteria** into **acetic acid, H₂, and CO₂**. This stage requires very low hydrogen partial pressure. **Optimum pH ≈ 7.0**.

**Stage 4 — Methanogenesis (2 marks):**
**Methanogenic archaea** (e.g., *Methanobacterium*, *Methanococcus*) convert acetate and H₂/CO₂ into **CH₄ + CO₂**:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2$$
$$\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
**Optimum pH = 7.0–7.5**; **optimum temperature = 35 °C (mesophilic)** or 55 °C (thermophilic). Methanogens are very slow-growing and pH-sensitive; the system must remain in this pH range.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing retention time and digestion time** — *HRT* = total slurry residence time; *digestion time* refers to the biochemical conversion time. They are not always equal.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1:1 dilution** — Many students apply the dung mass directly into $Q = M/\rho$, forgetting water addition. **Always add water mass first.**
> 3. **Mixing up $V_d$ and $V_{total}$** — The digester volume excludes the gas space; the total plant volume includes it. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 4. **Wrong units in $T_s$** — Gas storage is computed in *days* (or fractional days), not hours. Convert $T_s$ before substituting.
> 5. **Calling the gas dome a "gasholder"** — In fixed dome plants, it is a *storage dome*, not a movable holder; the word *gasholder* is reserved for KVIC/floating drum plants.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Fixed Dome Biogas Plant**

- ⭐ **Also called:** Chinese Biogas Plant; Constant Volume Plant; Sinha's Model.
- ⭐ **Key principle:** Hydraulic displacement, **no moving parts**, **variable gas pressure**.
- ⭐ **Eight components to memorize:** Mixing tank, inlet pipe, digester, fixed dome, central dividing wall, displacement chamber, compensation tank, gas outlet.
- ⭐ **Standard sizes:** 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 60 m³ (Sinha designs).
- ⭐ **Mixing ratio:** Cow dung : Water = **1 : 1 by weight**.
- ⭐ **HRT formula:** $V_d = Q \times t$ (HRT typically **30–50 days**).
- ⭐ **Daily gas:** $G = M_{wet} \times G_c$; typical $G_c$ = **0.036–0.040 m³/kg wet dung**.
- ⭐ **Gas storage time:** $T_s$ = **10–12 hours** (half a day).
- ⭐ **Optimum temperature:** **35 °C (mesophilic)**.
- ⭐ **Optimum pH for methanogenesis:** **7.0 – 7.5**.
- ⭐ **Biogas composition:** CH₄ = **55–65 %**, CO₂ = **30–40 %**.
- ⭐ **Calorific value:** ≈ **19.7 – 23.5 MJ/m³**.
- ⭐ **Plant life:** **20–25 years** (vs 10–15 years for KVIC).
- ⭐ **Compensation tank = pressure indicator** (slurry height ∝ gas pressure).
- ⭐ **Biogas uses:** Cooking, lighting, genset (dual-fuel mode), refrigeration.
- ⭐ **By-product:** Digestate slurry = high-quality **organic bio-manure** (N-P-K enriched).
- ⭐ **Top disadvantage:** **Variable gas pressure** (needs a regulator for stoves).

> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "MAD COGS-DC":** *Mixing tank → Inlet → Digester → Central wall → Outlet → Gas dome → Slurry-displacement → Compensation-tank.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Urban Waste to Energy Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET412 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Urban Waste to Energy Conversion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Urban Waste to Energy Conversion (UWtE)** refers to the systematic recovery of usable thermal, electrical, or chemical energy from **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** streams generated by urban populations, using a family of thermochemical, biochemical, and physico-chemical conversion technologies. The discipline sits at the intersection of **waste management hierarchy** (the 5R framework: Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover) and **distributed renewable power generation**.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, MSW is classified as a **biomass-derived heterogeneous solid fuel** with a typical composition by mass of: **paper/cardboard (30–40%)**, **food/green waste (30–45%)**, **plastics (10–15%)**, **glass/metals (10–15%)**, and **textiles/others (5–10%)**. The energy recovery potential is governed by the **moisture content (M)**, **ash content (A)**, **volatile matter (VM)**, **fixed carbon (FC)**, and the **calorific value (CV)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme Module 3 explicitly focuses on **four primary routes**: (1) Incineration with heat recovery, (2) Anaerobic Digestion (AD) producing biogas, (3) Landfill Gas (LFG) recovery, and (4) Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF) production. Pyrolysis and gasification appear as advanced variants.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Kitchen Leftover Analogy:** Think of urban waste as a "messy kitchen pantry" — it contains burnt toast (carbon), wet vegetable peels (biodegradable organics), plastic wrappers (hydrocarbons), and metal foil. You cannot feed this mess directly to a sensitive engine. Instead, you must **sort, dry, and convert** it. *Incineration* is like burning the dry fraction in a furnace to boil water. *Anaerobic digestion* is like sealing wet food scraps in an airtight jar where invisible microbes "eat" the waste and exhale methane, which you collect and burn. The key insight: **the wetter the waste, the less suitable it is for burning but the better it is for digestion**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of typical MSW:** **8 MJ/kg to 12 MJ/kg** (as-received basis)
- **Methane (CH₄) calorific value:** **55.5 MJ/kg** (pure)
- **Standard temperature and pressure (STP):** **273.15 K, 101.325 kPa**
- **Molar volume at STP:** **22.414 m³/kmol**
- **Density of methane at STP:** **0.716 kg/m³**
- **Carbon content of biodegradable fraction:** approx. **40–50%** by dry mass

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** Do NOT confuse the **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** with the **Lower Heating Value (LHV)**. HHV includes the latent heat of water vapour condensation; LHV assumes water leaves as vapour. For MSW with high moisture, the difference can exceed **2 MJ/kg** and examiners award separate marks for correctly choosing LHV in energy yield calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Routes — The Four Pillars

#### **Pillar 1: Incineration (Direct Combustion with Energy Recovery)**

- MSW is burned at **850 °C to 1100 °C** in a controlled furnace (typically a **mass-burn grate** or **fluidised bed combustor**).
- Flue gases pass over a **water-tube boiler**, generating **steam at 40–60 bar and 400–450 °C**.
- Steam drives a **Rankine-cycle turbine** coupled to a generator, producing **electricity at 18–28% net efficiency**.
- **Combined Heat and Power (CHP)** boosts total energy utilisation to **60–80%**.

**Why this route?** Simplest, fastest volume reduction (90% by volume, 70% by mass), and suitable for waste with **LHV > 6 MJ/kg**.

**How it works — Step Logic:**

1. Waste receipt and tipping floor storage (2–5 day buffer).
2. Crane grabs feed the combustion grate.
3. Primary air from below (40–60% stoichiometric) sustains combustion; secondary air above completes burnout of CO and VOCs.
4. Combustion heat is transferred to boiler tubes.
5. Flue gas treatment removes particulates (electrostatic precipitator), acid gases (wet scrubber), NOₓ (SNCR), and dioxins (activated carbon + bag filter).
6. Bottom ash is quenched and sent for metal recovery; fly ash is vitrified or landfilled as hazardous waste.

#### **Pillar 2: Anaerobic Digestion (AD)**

- Biochemical breakdown of organic matter by **four microbial consortia**: hydrolytic, acidogenic, acetogenic, and methanogenic bacteria.
- Operates in the **mesophilic range (35–40 °C)** or **thermophilic range (50–55 °C)**.
- Residence time: **15–30 days (mesophilic)** or **10–20 days (thermophilic)**.
- Outputs: **biogas (50–65% CH₄, 30–45% CO₂, trace H₂S)** and **digestate (liquid/solid fertiliser)**.

**Why this route?** Handles wet organic waste (>50% moisture), generates two revenue streams (electricity + fertiliser), and operates at near-atmospheric pressure with low emissions.

**How it works — Step Logic:**

1. Pre-treatment: removal of plastics, metals, glass (recycling stream).
2. Size reduction and slurry formation (total solids **TS = 10–15%**).
3. Sealed airtight digester with controlled pH (**6.8–7.5**) and alkalinity.
4. Continuous or batch feeding.
5. Biogas collection in a **gas holder**; H₂S scrubbing with iron oxide or activated carbon.
6. Biogas utilisation in **CHP engine (electrical efficiency 35–42%)** or upgraded to **biomethane** (CH₄ > 95%) for grid injection.

#### **Pillar 3: Landfill Gas (LFG) Recovery**

- Anaerobic decomposition of waste *in situ* within a sanitary landfill.
- Gas generation follows a **four-phase model**: aerobic (Phase I), transition (Phase II), acidogenic (Phase III — peak CH₄), methanogenic steady (Phase IV), and decline (Phase V).
- **LFG collection system**: vertical extraction wells + horizontal trenches connected to a **blower/flare station**.

**Why this route?** Passive energy recovery from legacy landfills, with global warming mitigation potential of **21× CO₂ equivalent** (methane GWP₁₀₀ = 28–34).

#### **Pillar 4: Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF) and Solid Recovered Fuel (SRF)**

- **Mechanical Biological Treatment (MBT)** of MSW to produce a homogenised, dried, high-CV fuel pellet.
- Process: shredding → magnetic separation (ferrous) → eddy current (non-ferrous) → ballistic/air classifier (organic/inorganic) → drying to moisture **<15%** → pelletisation.
- **RDF-CV** typically **15–20 MJ/kg**, suitable for co-firing in **cement kilns**, **coal-fired power plants**, and **dedicated fluidised bed combustors**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Symbol / Definition** | **Units** | **Applicable Route** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Q = m \times \text{CV}_{\text{LHV}} \times \eta_{\text{thermal}} \times \eta_{\text{electrical}}$ | Mass-based power output | kWh/day | Incineration |
| $\text{CV}_{\text{Dulong}} = 0.338C + 1.428\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 0.095S$ | Dulong's empirical CV | MJ/kg | RDF, MSW |
| $G_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.4 \times B_o \times M \times e^{-k(t-t_c)}$ | First-order decay methane yield (IPCC) | m³ | Landfill |
| $V_{\text{CH}_4} = \dfrac{B_o \cdot M_{\text{degradable}}}{1000}$ | Buswell stoichiometric yield | m³ | AD, LFG |
| $C_n H_a O_b N_c + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2} + \dfrac{3c}{4}\right)H_2O \rightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} + \dfrac{3c}{8}\right)CO_2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{3c}{8}\right)CH_4 + c\,NH_3$ | Buswell generalised equation | mol | AD |
| $\eta_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{electrical,grid}}}{m_{\text{waste}} \times \text{CV}_{\text{LHV}}}$ | Net electrical efficiency | dimensionless | All routes |
| $E_{\text{daily}} = \dfrac{W \times \text{CV}_{\text{LHV}} \times \eta_{\text{overall}}}{365}$ | Annual energy potential | kWh/yr | Planning |
| $\text{Stoichiometric air} = \dfrac{11.6C + 34.8(H_2 - O_2/8) + 4.35S}{100}$ | Theoretical O₂ demand | kg air/kg fuel | Combustion |
| $H_{\text{CH}_4, \text{LHV}} = 50.0 \text{ MJ/kg}$ (pure); biogas 50–65% CH₄ | Methane energy density | MJ/kg | AD, LFG |
| $Y_{\text{biogas}} = 0.4 \text{ to } 0.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg VS}_{\text{added}}$ | Volatile solids yield | m³/kg VS | AD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Table Symbols:** All absolute values (such as $\vert C \vert$) are rendered using `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. In your exam answer scripts, simply write the absolute value bars normally.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban planning:** Estimating power generation potential of a city (e.g., 1000 tonnes/day MSW ≈ 8–14 MW electrical from incineration).
- **Carbon credit accounting:** Each MWh of landfill gas-derived electricity displaces approximately **0.8–1.0 tonnes of CO₂** compared to grid coal.
- **Cement industry co-processing:** RDF replaces fossil coal at substitution rates of **20–60%** by thermal input.
- **Smart grid integration:** Anaerobic digestion provides **dispatchable renewable baseload** (unlike solar/wind), giving 7000+ hours/yr capacity factor.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Biogas Yield from Buswell's Generalised Stoichiometric Equation

**Statement:** A model organic substrate with empirical formula $C_n H_a O_b N_c$ undergoes complete anaerobic digestion. Derive the theoretical methane yield per kilogram of substrate.

**Starting Reaction (Buswell Equation):**

$$
C_n H_a O_b N_c + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2} + \dfrac{3c}{4}\right)H_2O \longrightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} + \dfrac{3c}{8}\right)CO_2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{3c}{8}\right)CH_4 + c\,NH_3
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the substrate.** For a generic carbohydrate-lipid-protein blend approximating urban food waste, take the empirical formula as **$C_{10}H_{19}O_3N$** (representing an average organic constituent with $n=10$, $a=19$, $b=3$, $c=1$).

**Step 2 — Compute stoichiometric coefficients.**

Moles of water required:
$$
n_{H_2O} = n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2} + \dfrac{3c}{4} = 10 - \dfrac{19}{4} - \dfrac{3}{2} + \dfrac{3}{4} = 10 - 4.75 - 1.5 + 0.75 = 4.5
$$

Moles of CO₂ produced:
$$
n_{CO_2} = \dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} + \dfrac{3c}{8} = 5 - \dfrac{19}{8} + \dfrac{3}{4} + \dfrac{3}{8} = 5 - 2.375 + 0.75 + 0.375 = 3.75
$$

Moles of CH₄ produced:
$$
n_{CH_4} = \dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{3c}{8} = 5 + 2.375 - 0.75 - 0.375 = 6.25
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of substrate per mole.**

Molecular weight of $C_{10}H_{19}O_3N$:
$$
M = 10(12) + 19(1) + 3(16) + 1(14) = 120 + 19 + 48 + 14 = 201 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric methane yield at STP.**

Volume per mole of substrate:
$$
V_{CH_4} = 6.25 \times 22.414 \text{ L} = 140.09 \text{ L}
$$

Yield per kg:
$$
Y_{CH_4} = \dfrac{140.09 \text{ L}}{0.201 \text{ kg}} = 696.96 \text{ L/kg substrate} \approx 0.697 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Energy content of methane produced.**

Mass of CH₄ per kg substrate:
$$
m_{CH_4} = 6.25 \times 16 \text{ g/mol} = 100 \text{ g} = 0.1 \text{ kg}
$$

Energy content (using LHV = 50 MJ/kg):
$$
E_{CH_4} = 0.1 \times 50 = 5.0 \text{ MJ/kg substrate}
$$

**Step 6 — Final balanced equation for this specific case:**

$$
C_{10}H_{19}O_3N + 4.5\,H_2O \longrightarrow 3.75\,CO_2 + 6.25\,CH_4 + NH_3
$$

**Result:** The theoretical maximum methane yield is **0.697 m³ CH₄ per kg of substrate**, equivalent to **5.0 MJ/kg** of substrate as chemical energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Yield Correction:** In real digesters, only **60–85%** of this theoretical maximum is achieved due to incomplete hydrolysis, microbial cell synthesis (≈5% of carbon retained as biomass), and process losses. The KTU examiner expects you to state a **realistic operating yield of 0.25–0.45 m³ CH₄/kg VS** for food waste and **0.15–0.25 m³ CH₄/kg VS** for mixed MSW.

### 3.2 Derivation: Power Generation from MSW Incineration

**Given:**

- Daily MSW generation: $W = 1000$ tonnes/day
- LHV of as-received MSW: $\text{CV}_{\text{LHV}} = 9.5$ MJ/kg
- Boiler thermal efficiency: $\eta_{\text{boiler}} = 0.82$
- Turbine-generator electrical efficiency: $\eta_{\text{turbine}} = 0.32$
- Auxiliary consumption (parasitic load): $\eta_{\text{aux}} = 0.10$ of gross power
- Plant availability: $A = 0.85$

**Step 1 — Daily thermal energy released in furnace.**

$$
Q_{\text{daily}} = W \times \text{CV}_{\text{LHV}} = 1000 \times 10^3 \text{ kg/day} \times 9.5 \text{ MJ/kg} = 9.5 \times 10^6 \text{ MJ/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Gross electrical energy per day (before auxiliaries).**

$$
E_{\text{gross}} = Q_{\text{daily}} \times \eta_{\text{boiler}} \times \eta_{\text{turbine}} = 9.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.82 \times 0.32 = 2.4928 \times 10^6 \text{ MJ/day}
$$

Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ):
$$
E_{\text{gross}} = \dfrac{2.4928 \times 10^6}{3.6} = 6.924 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Net electrical energy after auxiliary consumption.**

$$
E_{\text{net}} = E_{\text{gross}} \times (1 - \eta_{\text{aux}}) = 6.924 \times 10^5 \times 0.90 = 6.232 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Net electrical power output.**

$$
P_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{net}}}{24 \text{ h}} = \dfrac{6.232 \times 10^5}{24} = 25{,}966 \text{ kW} \approx 26 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual energy generation considering plant availability.**

$$
E_{\text{annual}} = E_{\text{net}} \times 365 \times A = 6.232 \times 10^5 \times 365 \times 0.85 = 1.933 \times 10^8 \text{ kWh/yr} \approx 193.3 \text{ GWh/yr}
$$

**Step 6 — Overall net electrical efficiency.**

$$
\eta_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{net, kWh/day}} \times 3.6}{Q_{\text{daily, MJ/day}}} = \dfrac{6.232 \times 10^5 \times 3.6}{9.5 \times 10^6} = 0.2361 \approx 23.6\%
$$

**Result Summary:**

| **Output Metric** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| Net electrical power | **26 MW** |
| Annual energy generation | **193.3 GWh** |
| Overall net efficiency | **23.6%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to convert MJ to kWh, OR they apply auxiliary consumption *after* annualising the result instead of *before*. The correct order is: **daily thermal → gross electrical (daily) → net electrical (daily) → annualise with availability**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Landfill Gas Generation Using First-Order Decay Model (IPCC FOD)

**Statement:** Estimate methane generation from a landfill receiving $M = 500{,}000$ tonnes of waste over 10 years ($t_c = 10$ yr), with degradable organic carbon (DOC) content $DOC = 0.15$ (fraction), fraction of DOC that decomposes $DOC_f = 0.5$, methane fraction in LFG $F = 0.5$, and decay rate $k = 0.05$ /yr. The landfill is currently at $t = 15$ years after closure.

**Step 1 — Methane generation potential $L_0$ (per kg of waste).**

$$
L_0 = \text{DOC} \times \text{DOC}_f \times F \times \dfrac{16}{12} = 0.15 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 \times \dfrac{16}{12}
$$

$$
L_0 = 0.0375 \times 1.3333 = 0.050 \text{ kg CH}_4/\text{kg waste}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual waste deposition.**

$$
\dot{M} = \dfrac{500{,}000}{10} = 50{,}000 \text{ tonnes/yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Methane generated in year $t$ (since deposit).**

For waste deposited in year $j$ and now at age $\tau = t - j$:

$$
G_{\tau} = \dot{M} \times L_0 \times e^{-k\tau}
$$

**Step 4 — Total methane generation at $t = 15$ yr (sum over deposits from year 5 to year 15, since waste at $t=15$ is 5–15 years old).**

$$
G_{\text{total}} = \sum_{j=5}^{15} 50{,}000 \times 0.050 \times e^{-0.05(15-j)}
$$

In tonnes/year:
$$
G_{\text{total}} = 2500 \sum_{\tau=0}^{10} e^{-0.05\tau}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the geometric series.**

Sum of geometric series:
$$
S = \sum_{\tau=0}^{10} e^{-0.05\tau} = \dfrac{1 - e^{-0.05 \times 11}}{1 - e^{-0.05}} = \dfrac{1 - e^{-0.55}}{1 - e^{-0.55}}
$$

Wait — re-evaluate: numerator $= 1 - e^{-0.55} = 1 - 0.5769 = 0.4231$; denominator $= 1 - 0.9512 = 0.0488$.

$$
S = \dfrac{0.4231}{0.0488} = 8.670
$$

**Step 6 — Compute total methane generation.**

$$
G_{\text{total}} = 2500 \times 8.670 = 21{,}675 \text{ tonnes CH}_4/\text{yr}
$$

Convert to volume at STP:
$$
V_{\text{CH}_4} = 21{,}675 \times 10^3 \text{ kg} \times \dfrac{22.414 \text{ m}^3/\text{kmol}}{16 \text{ kg/kmol}} = 21{,}675 \times 10^3 \times 1.4009 = 3.036 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}
$$

Convert to energy (LHV of CH₄ = 50 MJ/kg):
$$
E_{\text{annual}} = 21{,}675 \times 10^3 \times 50 = 1.084 \times 10^9 \text{ MJ/yr} = 301{,}000 \text{ MWh/yr}
$$

With 35% electrical conversion efficiency:
$$
P_{\text{electric}} = \dfrac{301{,}000 \text{ MWh}}{8760 \text{ h}} \approx 34.4 \text{ MW}_{\text{electric}} \text{ (continuous equivalent)}
$$

**Result:** This landfill can support a **~30 MW electrical** LFG-to-power plant (with capacity factor 0.85).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define Municipal Solid Waste (MSW). List any four major components of MSW and state the typical range of its calorific value (as-received basis).

**Model Answer:**

**Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** is defined as the everyday items and materials that are collected by or on behalf of the local municipal authority and disposed of through waste management systems. It includes household refuse, commercial waste, institutional waste, street sweepings, and construction debris from small sources (as per **KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus**).

**Four major components of MSW:**

1. **Organic / biodegradable fraction** (food waste, garden waste, paper) — 30–45% by mass
2. **Paper and cardboard** — 30–40% by mass
3. **Plastics** (PE, PP, PET, PVC) — 10–15% by mass
4. **Glass, metals, and inert materials** — 10–15% by mass
5. **Textiles, leather, rubber, and others** — 5–10% by mass

**Calorific value (as-received basis):** LHV ranges from **8 MJ/kg to 12 MJ/kg** for typical Indian urban MSW. With sorting and drying, RDF achieves **15–20 MJ/kg**. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Distinguish between **Incineration** and **Anaerobic Digestion** as routes for urban waste to energy conversion. Mention the energy carrier produced in each.

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Incineration** | **Anaerobic Digestion** |
|---|---|---|
| Process type | Thermochemical (combustion) | Biochemical (microbial) |
| Operating temperature | 850–1100 °C | 35–55 °C |
| Energy carrier produced | **High-pressure steam** → electricity | **Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)** → electricity/heat or biomethane |
| Suitable moisture range | **Moisture < 50%** preferred | **Moisture > 50%** suitable |
| Volume reduction | 90% | 30–50% (rest is digestate) |
| By-product | Bottom ash, fly ash | Digestate (biofertiliser) |
| Air emission risk | Higher (NOₓ, dioxins, particulates) | Very low (sealed system) |
| Plant size economics | Large scale (>500 TPD) | Small to medium (10–500 TPD) |
| Net electrical efficiency | 18–28% | 30–42% (gas engine CHP) |

(3 marks — full tabular comparison is acceptable)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the working of a **mass-burn incineration plant** with energy recovery for Municipal Solid Waste. Clearly state the function of each major component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A city generates **1500 tonnes per day** of MSW with a moisture content of **45%** (wet basis) and an LHV of **7.5 MJ/kg (as-received)**. The incineration plant operates with a boiler efficiency of **80%**, turbine-generator efficiency of **30%**, and auxiliary consumption of **8%** of gross power. The plant availability is **330 days/year**. Calculate:

   (i) Daily thermal energy released.
   (ii) Daily gross electrical energy.
   (iii) Net electrical power output (MW).
   (iv) Annual net energy generation (MWh/yr).
   (v) Overall net electrical efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Flowchart — Mass-Burn Incineration Plant:** (Refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid diagram)

**Functions of Major Components:**

1. **Tipping Floor & Waste Crane** — Temporary storage (2–5 days) and homogenisation; crane feeds hopper.
2. **Feed Hopper with Live-Bottom** — Continuous gravity feeding of waste onto grate.
3. **Reciprocating Grate** — Primary combustion surface; waste dries, volatilises, and burns progressively.
4. **Primary & Secondary Combustion Chambers** — Complete burnout; secondary air ensures ≥2 sec residence at 850 °C for dioxin destruction.
5. **Water-Tube Boiler** — Heat recovery; converts water to steam at 40–60 bar, 400–450 °C.
6. **Superheater & Economiser** — Final steam temperature boost and feedwater preheating.
7. **Steam Turbine-Generator** — Converts thermal energy of steam to electrical energy (Rankine cycle).
8. **Flue Gas Treatment Train (ESP + Scrubber + SNCR + Bag Filter)** — Removes particulates, acid gases, NOₓ, dioxins.
9. **Air Pollution Control Residue Handling** — Bottom ash to recovery; fly ash to secure landfill.
10. **Condenser & Cooling Tower** — Steam condensation and heat rejection to atmosphere.

**(7 marks — full marks for component-by-component explanation tied to flowchart)**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- $W = 1500$ TPD $= 1.5 \times 10^6$ kg/day
- $\text{CV}_{\text{LHV}} = 7.5$ MJ/kg
- $\eta_{\text{boiler}} = 0.80$
- $\eta_{\text{turbine}} = 0.30$
- $\eta_{\text{aux}} = 0.08$
- $A = 330/365 = 0.904$

**(i) Daily thermal energy released:**

$$
Q_{\text{daily}} = W \times \text{CV}_{\text{LHV}} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \times 7.5 = 1.125 \times 10^7 \text{ MJ/day}
$$

**(ii) Daily gross electrical energy:**

$$
E_{\text{gross}} = Q_{\text{daily}} \times \eta_{\text{boiler}} \times \eta_{\text{turbine}} = 1.125 \times 10^7 \times 0.80 \times 0.30 = 2.7 \times 10^6 \text{ MJ/day}
$$

Convert to kWh:
$$
E_{\text{gross}} = \dfrac{2.7 \times 10^6}{3.6} = 7.5 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**(iii) Net electrical power output:**

Net daily energy:
$$
E_{\text{net, daily}} = E_{\text{gross}} \times (1 - 0.08) = 7.5 \times 10^5 \times 0.92 = 6.9 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

Net power:
$$
P_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{6.9 \times 10^5}{24} = 28{,}750 \text{ kW} = 28.75 \text{ MW}
$$

**(iv) Annual net energy generation:**

$$
E_{\text{annual}} = E_{\text{net, daily}} \times 330 = 6.9 \times 10^5 \times 330 = 2.277 \times 10^8 \text{ kWh/yr} = 227{,}700 \text{ MWh/yr}
$$

**(v) Overall net electrical efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{net, daily, kWh}} \times 3.6}{Q_{\text{daily, MJ}}} = \dfrac{6.9 \times 10^5 \times 3.6}{1.125 \times 10^7} = \dfrac{2.484 \times 10^6}{1.125 \times 10^7} = 0.2208 \approx 22.08\%
$$

**Tabulated Result:**

| **Quantity** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| Daily thermal energy | **11,250,000 MJ/day** (11.25 TJ/day) |
| Daily gross electrical energy | **750,000 kWh/day** |
| Net electrical power output | **28.75 MW** |
| Annual net energy | **227,700 MWh/yr** |
| Overall net efficiency | **22.08%** |

(7 marks — full marks for stepwise computation)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** What is **Landfill Gas (LFG)**? Describe the four phases of landfill decomposition with a graph of methane generation vs. time. List the typical composition of LFG. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sanitary landfill received **200,000 tonnes of MSW over 8 years (uniform rate)**. The DOC = 0.20, DOC_f = 0.5, fraction of DOC that converts to CH₄ is F = 0.5, and first-order decay rate $k = 0.04$/yr. Estimate the methane generation rate (m³/yr) **at the end of year 12** (i.e., 4 years after closure). Assume landfill operation years 1 to 8; thus, at end of year 12, waste ages range from 4 to 12 years. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Landfill Gas — Theory:**

**Definition:** *Landfill Gas (LFG)* is the anaerobic decomposition byproduct of organic fractions in municipal solid waste buried in sanitary landfills, consisting primarily of methane (CH₄) and carbon dioxide (CO₂), with traces of hydrogen sulphide (H₂S), ammonia (NH₃), non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOCs), and water vapour.

**Four Phases of Landfill Decomposition:**

1. **Phase I — Aerobic Phase (Days to weeks):** Oxygen trapped in waste is consumed by aerobic bacteria; CO₂ and heat are released. Temperature rises to 50–70 °C. Duration: few days to few weeks.

2. **Phase II — Transition (Acidogenic) Phase (Weeks to months):** Aerobic conditions deplete; facultative and obligate anaerobes dominate. Hydrolysis and acidogenesis produce volatile fatty acids (VFAs), H₂, CO₂. pH drops to 5–6. Duration: months to 1–2 years.

3. **Phase III — Acidogenic / Active Methanogenic Phase (1–5 years):** Methanogenic archaea establish population; VFAs are converted to CH₄ and CO₂. Methane yield peaks; LFG production is at maximum. LHV of LFG ≈ 16–20 MJ/m³.

4. **Phase IV — Stable Methanogenic Phase (5–50 years):** Substrate depletion; gas generation gradually declines. LFG composition stabilises (CH₄ ~ 45–55%, CO₂ ~ 40–50%).

5. **Phase V — Maturation Phase (50+ years):** Nutrient availability drops; aerobic conditions may return during cover soil diffusion; gas production minimal.

**Graph (qualitative):** Methane generation rate vs. time forms a **bell-shaped curve** peaking in Phase III (around 2–5 years post-deposit) and tailing off exponentially into Phases IV–V.

**Typical LFG Composition:**

| **Component** | **Volume %** |
|---|---|
| CH₄ (methane) | 40–60% |
| CO₂ (carbon dioxide) | 30–50% |
| N₂ (nitrogen) | 2–5% |
| O₂ (oxygen) | 0.1–1% |
| H₂S (hydrogen sulphide) | 0–1% (often 100–500 ppm) |
| NH₃ (ammonia) | 0.1–1% |
| NMVOCs (trace organics) | 0.01–0.6% |
| Water vapour | Saturated |

(7 marks)

**(b) Numerical — First-Order Decay (IPCC FOD) Method:**

**Given:**

- Total mass $M_{\text{total}} = 200{,}000$ tonnes over 8 years
- DOC = 0.20; DOC_f = 0.5; F = 0.5; k = 0.04/yr
- End of year 12 → waste ages 4 to 12 years (deposits from year 4 to year 12 inclusive)
- Annual deposit rate: $\dot{M} = 200{,}000/8 = 25{,}000$ tonnes/yr

**Step 1 — Methane generation potential $L_0$ per kg of waste.**

$$
L_0 = \text{DOC} \times \text{DOC}_f \times F \times \dfrac{16}{12} = 0.20 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 \times \dfrac{16}{12}
$$

$$
L_0 = 0.05 \times 1.3333 = 0.06667 \text{ kg CH}_4/\text{kg waste}
$$

**Step 2 — Methane generation from each year's deposit, at end of year 12.**

For deposit in year $j$, age at end of year 12 is $\tau = 12 - j$. The age of waste from year 4 to year 12 deposit ranges from $\tau = 0$ (deposited in year 12) to $\tau = 8$ (deposited in year 4).

Wait — re-reading: landfill operation years 1 to 8. At end of year 12, the **oldest** waste was deposited in year 1 → age = 11 years. The **youngest** waste was deposited in year 8 → age = 4 years.

So age range is $\tau = 4$ to $\tau = 11$ years.

**Step 3 — Sum methane generation over all 8 deposit years.**

For each deposit of 25,000 tonnes:
$$
G_j = 25{,}000 \times 10^3 \text{ kg} \times L_0 \times e^{-k\tau_j} \text{ kg CH}_4/\text{yr (from year } j \text{ deposit)}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical summation (kg CH₄/yr from each deposit year):**

| **Deposit Year (j)** | **Age $\tau = 12 - j$ (yr)** | **$e^{-0.04\tau}$** | **CH₄ from year j (tonnes/yr)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 8 | 0.7261 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.7261 = 1210.3$ |
| 5 | 7 | 0.7558 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.7558 = 1259.6$ |
| 6 | 6 | 0.7866 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.7866 = 1310.9$ |
| 7 | 5 | 0.8187 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.8187 = 1364.4$ |
| 8 | 4 | 0.8521 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.8521 = 1420.0$ |

But we need to check: the problem says waste operation was years 1 to 8, and at end of year 12, waste is 4 to 12 years old. So the deposit years corresponding are years 1 through 8.

| **Deposit Year (j)** | **Age $\tau = 12 - j$ (yr)** | **$e^{-0.04\tau}$** | **CH₄ from year j (tonnes/yr)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 11 | 0.6412 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.6412 = 1068.7$ |
| 2 | 10 | 0.6703 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.6703 = 1117.1$ |
| 3 | 9 | 0.6977 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.6977 = 1162.7$ |
| 4 | 8 | 0.7261 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.7261 = 1210.3$ |
| 5 | 7 | 0.7558 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.7558 = 1259.6$ |
| 6 | 6 | 0.7866 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.7866 = 1310.9$ |
| 7 | 5 | 0.8187 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.8187 = 1364.4$ |
| 8 | 4 | 0.8521 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.8521 = 1420.0$ |

**Step 5 — Sum total CH₄ generation rate.**

$$
G_{\text{total}} = 1068.7 + 1117.1 + 1162.7 + 1210.3 + 1259.6 + 1310.9 + 1364.4 + 1420.0
$$

$$
G_{\text{total}} = 9913.7 \text{ tonnes CH}_4/\text{yr}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to volumetric flow at STP.**

$$
V_{\text{CH}_4} = 9913.7 \times 10^3 \text{ kg} \times \dfrac{22.414 \text{ m}^3/\text{kmol}}{16 \text{ kg/kmol}}
$$

$$
V_{\text{CH}_4} = 9{,}913{,}700 \times 1.4009 = 1.388 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}
$$

**Result:** Total methane generation rate at end of year 12 ≈ **1.39 × 10⁷ m³ CH₄/yr** (≈ 13.9 million m³/yr).

**Bonus check — Energy potential:**

$$
E_{\text{CH}_4} = 9913.7 \times 10^3 \times 50 \text{ MJ/kg} = 4.957 \times 10^8 \text{ MJ/yr}
$$

With 35% electrical conversion:
$$
P_{\text{electric}} = \dfrac{4.957 \times 10^8}{3.6 \times 8760} = \dfrac{1.377 \times 10^8 \text{ kWh}}{8760 \text{ h}} = 15{,}718 \text{ kW} \approx 15.7 \text{ MW}_{\text{electric}}
$$

(7 marks — full marks for stepwise derivation and substitution)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1 (3 marks lost):** Writing $L_0$ without the factor $16/12$ (stoichiometric ratio of CH₄ molecular weight to carbon atomic weight). This factor converts *degradable carbon* to *methane mass*.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2 (2 marks lost):** Confusing "end of year 12" with "year 12 of operation." The age is always calculated as $t_{\text{now}} - t_{\text{deposit}}$, not the deposit year itself.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3 (2 marks lost):** Forgetting to convert kg CH₄ to m³ CH₄ at STP (multiply by $22.414/16 = 1.4009$). Examiners explicitly check for unit conversion.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4 (1 mark lost):** In Buswell equation problems, omitting the **NH₃** term. Always show the nitrogen balance.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **MSW Composition Rule of Thumb:** ~40% biodegradable, ~30% paper, ~10–15% plastic, ~10% inerts.
- **LHV of MSW** (as-received): **8–12 MJ/kg**; RDF: **15–20 MJ/kg**.
- **Four Routes:** Incineration, Anaerobic Digestion, Landfill Gas, RDF.
- **Incineration Plant Efficiencies:** Boiler 80–85%, Turbine 30–35%, Net Electrical 18–28%, CHP Total 60–80%.
- **AD Operating Parameters:** Mesophilic 35–40 °C, HRT 20 days, TS 10–15%, Biogas yield 0.4–0.5 m³/kg VS.
- **LFG Composition:** CH₄ 40–60%, CO₂ 30–50%, traces of H₂S, NH₃.
- **Buswell Stoichiometry:** For substrate $C_n H_a O_b N_c$, methane yield depends only on $n$, $a$, $b$, $c$ — not on microbial kinetics.
- **Dulong's Formula:** $\text{CV} = 0.338C + 1.428(H - O/8) + 0.095S$ (use elemental weight %).
- **IPCC FOD Model:** $G_{\text{CH}_4} = M \cdot L_0 \cdot (e^{-kt_c} - e^{-kt})/k$ for continuous deposition; or summation of discrete year deposits.
- **LHV vs HHV:** LHV is used in plant calculations (water leaves as vapour in flue gas).
- **Methane LHV:** **50 MJ/kg** (pure); biogas energy = 50 × %CH₄ / 100 × mass flow.
- **Plant Availability:** 330 days/yr (90.4%) is the KTU standard assumption for biomass plants.
- **Power Plant Capacity from 1000 TPD MSW** (rule of thumb): **20–30 MW** gross, **18–28 MW** net.
- **Methane GWP₁₀₀:** 28–34 (IPCC AR5/AR6) — use 28 unless otherwise specified.
- **Auxiliary Consumption:** Typically 8–12% of gross electrical output for waste-to-energy plants.
- **Mermaid Diagram Keywords to AVOID in node IDs:** `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style` — use prefixed alphanumeric IDs only.
- **Always convert MJ → kWh** in numerical answers: divide by **3.6** (or multiply by $0.2778$).
- **Always show the chain:** Daily thermal energy → Gross electrical → Net electrical (after aux) → Annual energy (with availability).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Urban Waste to Energy Conversion.** All formulas in this document are derived from first principles and IPCC 2006 Guidelines; all numerical examples are within the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus scope. Practice the Part A and Part B questions twice and verify unit consistency at every step.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Routes — The Four Pillars

#### **Pillar 1: Incineration (Direct Combustion with Energy Recovery)**

- MSW is burned at **850 °C to 1100 °C** in a controlled furnace (typically a **mass-burn grate** or **fluidised bed combustor**).
- Flue gases pass over a **water-tube boiler**, generating **steam at 40–60 bar and 400–450 °C**.
- Steam drives a **Rankine-cycle turbine** coupled to a generator, producing **electricity at 18–28% net efficiency**.
- **Combined Heat and Power (CHP)** boosts total energy utilisation to **60–80%**.

**Why this route?** Simplest, fastest volume reduction (90% by volume, 70% by mass), and suitable for waste with **LHV > 6 MJ/kg**.

**How it works — Step Logic:**

1. Waste receipt and tipping floor storage (2–5 day buffer).
2. Crane grabs feed the combustion grate.
3. Primary air from below (40–60% stoichiometric) sustains combustion; secondary air above completes burnout of CO and VOCs.
4. Combustion heat is transferred to boiler tubes.
5. Flue gas treatment removes particulates (electrostatic precipitator), acid gases (wet scrubber), NOₓ (SNCR), and dioxins (activated carbon + bag filter).
6. Bottom ash is quenched and sent for metal recovery; fly ash is vitrified or landfilled as hazardous waste.

#### **Pillar 2: Anaerobic Digestion (AD)**

- Biochemical breakdown of organic matter by **four microbial consortia**: hydrolytic, acidogenic, acetogenic, and methanogenic bacteria.
- Operates in the **mesophilic range (35–40 °C)** or **thermophilic range (50–55 °C)**.
- Residence time: **15–30 days (mesophilic)** or **10–20 days (thermophilic)**.
- Outputs: **biogas (50–65% CH₄, 30–45% CO₂, trace H₂S)** and **digestate (liquid/solid fertiliser)**.

**Why this route?** Handles wet organic waste (>50% moisture), generates two revenue streams (electricity + fertiliser), and operates at near-atmospheric pressure with low emissions.

**How it works — Step Logic:**

1. Pre-treatment: removal of plastics, metals, glass (recycling stream).
2. Size reduction and slurry formation (total solids **TS = 10–15%**).
3. Sealed airtight digester with controlled pH (**6.8–7.5**) and alkalinity.
4. Continuous or batch feeding.
5. Biogas collection in a **gas holder**; H₂S scrubbing with iron oxide or activated carbon.
6. Biogas utilisation in **CHP engine (electrical efficiency 35–42%)** or upgraded to **biomethane** (CH₄ > 95%) for grid injection.

#### **Pillar 3: Landfill Gas (LFG) Recovery**

- Anaerobic decomposition of waste *in situ* within a sanitary landfill.
- Gas generation follows a **four-phase model**: aerobic (Phase I), transition (Phase II), acidogenic (Phase III — peak CH₄), methanogenic steady (Phase IV), and decline (Phase V).
- **LFG collection system**: vertical extraction wells + horizontal trenches connected to a **blower/flare station**.

**Why this route?** Passive energy recovery from legacy landfills, with global warming mitigation potential of **21× CO₂ equivalent** (methane GWP₁₀₀ = 28–34).

#### **Pillar 4: Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF) and Solid Recovered Fuel (SRF)**

- **Mechanical Biological Treatment (MBT)** of MSW to produce a homogenised, dried, high-CV fuel pellet.
- Process: shredding → magnetic separation (ferrous) → eddy current (non-ferrous) → ballistic/air classifier (organic/inorganic) → drying to moisture **<15%** → pelletisation.
- **RDF-CV** typically **15–20 MJ/kg**, suitable for co-firing in **cement kilns**, **coal-fired power plants**, and **dedicated fluidised bed combustors**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Symbol / Definition** | **Units** | **Applicable Route** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Q = m \times \text{CV}_{\text{LHV}} \times \eta_{\text{thermal}} \times \eta_{\text{electrical}}$ | Mass-based power output | kWh/day | Incineration |
| $\text{CV}_{\text{Dulong}} = 0.338C + 1.428\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 0.095S$ | Dulong's empirical CV | MJ/kg | RDF, MSW |
| $G_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.4 \times B_o \times M \times e^{-k(t-t_c)}$ | First-order decay methane yield (IPCC) | m³ | Landfill |
| $V_{\text{CH}_4} = \dfrac{B_o \cdot M_{\text{degradable}}}{1000}$ | Buswell stoichiometric yield | m³ | AD, LFG |
| $C_n H_a O_b N_c + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2} + \dfrac{3c}{4}\right)H_2O \rightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} + \dfrac{3c}{8}\right)CO_2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{3c}{8}\right)CH_4 + c\,NH_3$ | Buswell generalised equation | mol | AD |
| $\eta_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{electrical,grid}}}{m_{\text{waste}} \times \text{CV}_{\text{LHV}}}$ | Net electrical efficiency | dimensionless | All routes |
| $E_{\text{daily}} = \dfrac{W \times \text{CV}_{\text{LHV}} \times \eta_{\text{overall}}}{365}$ | Annual energy potential | kWh/yr | Planning |
| $\text{Stoichiometric air} = \dfrac{11.6C + 34.8(H_2 - O_2/8) + 4.35S}{100}$ | Theoretical O₂ demand | kg air/kg fuel | Combustion |
| $H_{\text{CH}_4, \text{LHV}} = 50.0 \text{ MJ/kg}$ (pure); biogas 50–65% CH₄ | Methane energy density | MJ/kg | AD, LFG |
| $Y_{\text{biogas}} = 0.4 \text{ to } 0.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg VS}_{\text{added}}$ | Volatile solids yield | m³/kg VS | AD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Table Symbols:** All absolute values (such as $\vert C \vert$) are rendered using `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. In your exam answer scripts, simply write the absolute value bars normally.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban planning:** Estimating power generation potential of a city (e.g., 1000 tonnes/day MSW ≈ 8–14 MW electrical from incineration).
- **Carbon credit accounting:** Each MWh of landfill gas-derived electricity displaces approximately **0.8–1.0 tonnes of CO₂** compared to grid coal.
- **Cement industry co-processing:** RDF replaces fossil coal at substitution rates of **20–60%** by thermal input.
- **Smart grid integration:** Anaerobic digestion provides **dispatchable renewable baseload** (unlike solar/wind), giving 7000+ hours/yr capacity factor.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Biogas Yield from Buswell's Generalised Stoichiometric Equation

**Statement:** A model organic substrate with empirical formula $C_n H_a O_b N_c$ undergoes complete anaerobic digestion. Derive the theoretical methane yield per kilogram of substrate.

**Starting Reaction (Buswell Equation):**

$$
C_n H_a O_b N_c + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2} + \dfrac{3c}{4}\right)H_2O \longrightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} + \dfrac{3c}{8}\right)CO_2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{3c}{8}\right)CH_4 + c\,NH_3
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the substrate.** For a generic carbohydrate-lipid-protein blend approximating urban food waste, take the empirical formula as **$C_{10}H_{19}O_3N$** (representing an average organic constituent with $n=10$, $a=19$, $b=3$, $c=1$).

**Step 2 — Compute stoichiometric coefficients.**

Moles of water required:
$$
n_{H_2O} = n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2} + \dfrac{3c}{4} = 10 - \dfrac{19}{4} - \dfrac{3}{2} + \dfrac{3}{4} = 10 - 4.75 - 1.5 + 0.75 = 4.5
$$

Moles of CO₂ produced:
$$
n_{CO_2} = \dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} + \dfrac{3c}{8} = 5 - \dfrac{19}{8} + \dfrac{3}{4} + \dfrac{3}{8} = 5 - 2.375 + 0.75 + 0.375 = 3.75
$$

Moles of CH₄ produced:
$$
n_{CH_4} = \dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{3c}{8} = 5 + 2.375 - 0.75 - 0.375 = 6.25
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of substrate per mole.**

Molecular weight of $C_{10}H_{19}O_3N$:
$$
M = 10(12) + 19(1) + 3(16) + 1(14) = 120 + 19 + 48 + 14 = 201 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric methane yield at STP.**

Volume per mole of substrate:
$$
V_{CH_4} = 6.25 \times 22.414 \text{ L} = 140.09 \text{ L}
$$

Yield per kg:
$$
Y_{CH_4} = \dfrac{140.09 \text{ L}}{0.201 \text{ kg}} = 696.96 \text{ L/kg substrate} \approx 0.697 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Energy content of methane produced.**

Mass of CH₄ per kg substrate:
$$
m_{CH_4} = 6.25 \times 16 \text{ g/mol} = 100 \text{ g} = 0.1 \text{ kg}
$$

Energy content (using LHV = 50 MJ/kg):
$$
E_{CH_4} = 0.1 \times 50 = 5.0 \text{ MJ/kg substrate}
$$

**Step 6 — Final balanced equation for this specific case:**

$$
C_{10}H_{19}O_3N + 4.5\,H_2O \longrightarrow 3.75\,CO_2 + 6.25\,CH_4 + NH_3
$$

**Result:** The theoretical maximum methane yield is **0.697 m³ CH₄ per kg of substrate**, equivalent to **5.0 MJ/kg** of substrate as chemical energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Yield Correction:** In real digesters, only **60–85%** of this theoretical maximum is achieved due to incomplete hydrolysis, microbial cell synthesis (≈5% of carbon retained as biomass), and process losses. The KTU examiner expects you to state a **realistic operating yield of 0.25–0.45 m³ CH₄/kg VS** for food waste and **0.15–0.25 m³ CH₄/kg VS** for mixed MSW.

### 3.2 Derivation: Power Generation from MSW Incineration

**Given:**

- Daily MSW generation: $W = 1000$ tonnes/day
- LHV of as-received MSW: $\text{CV}_{\text{LHV}} = 9.5$ MJ/kg
- Boiler thermal efficiency: $\eta_{\text{boiler}} = 0.82$
- Turbine-generator electrical efficiency: $\eta_{\text{turbine}} = 0.32$
- Auxiliary consumption (parasitic load): $\eta_{\text{aux}} = 0.10$ of gross power
- Plant availability: $A = 0.85$

**Step 1 — Daily thermal energy released in furnace.**

$$
Q_{\text{daily}} = W \times \text{CV}_{\text{LHV}} = 1000 \times 10^3 \text{ kg/day} \times 9.5 \text{ MJ/kg} = 9.5 \times 10^6 \text{ MJ/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Gross electrical energy per day (before auxiliaries).**

$$
E_{\text{gross}} = Q_{\text{daily}} \times \eta_{\text{boiler}} \times \eta_{\text{turbine}} = 9.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.82 \times 0.32 = 2.4928 \times 10^6 \text{ MJ/day}
$$

Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ):
$$
E_{\text{gross}} = \dfrac{2.4928 \times 10^6}{3.6} = 6.924 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Net electrical energy after auxiliary consumption.**

$$
E_{\text{net}} = E_{\text{gross}} \times (1 - \eta_{\text{aux}}) = 6.924 \times 10^5 \times 0.90 = 6.232 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Net electrical power output.**

$$
P_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{net}}}{24 \text{ h}} = \dfrac{6.232 \times 10^5}{24} = 25{,}966 \text{ kW} \approx 26 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual energy generation considering plant availability.**

$$
E_{\text{annual}} = E_{\text{net}} \times 365 \times A = 6.232 \times 10^5 \times 365 \times 0.85 = 1.933 \times 10^8 \text{ kWh/yr} \approx 193.3 \text{ GWh/yr}
$$

**Step 6 — Overall net electrical efficiency.**

$$
\eta_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{net, kWh/day}} \times 3.6}{Q_{\text{daily, MJ/day}}} = \dfrac{6.232 \times 10^5 \times 3.6}{9.5 \times 10^6} = 0.2361 \approx 23.6\%
$$

**Result Summary:**

| **Output Metric** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| Net electrical power | **26 MW** |
| Annual energy generation | **193.3 GWh** |
| Overall net efficiency | **23.6%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to convert MJ to kWh, OR they apply auxiliary consumption *after* annualising the result instead of *before*. The correct order is: **daily thermal → gross electrical (daily) → net electrical (daily) → annualise with availability**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Landfill Gas Generation Using First-Order Decay Model (IPCC FOD)

**Statement:** Estimate methane generation from a landfill receiving $M = 500{,}000$ tonnes of waste over 10 years ($t_c = 10$ yr), with degradable organic carbon (DOC) content $DOC = 0.15$ (fraction), fraction of DOC that decomposes $DOC_f = 0.5$, methane fraction in LFG $F = 0.5$, and decay rate $k = 0.05$ /yr. The landfill is currently at $t = 15$ years after closure.

**Step 1 — Methane generation potential $L_0$ (per kg of waste).**

$$
L_0 = \text{DOC} \times \text{DOC}_f \times F \times \dfrac{16}{12} = 0.15 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 \times \dfrac{16}{12}
$$

$$
L_0 = 0.0375 \times 1.3333 = 0.050 \text{ kg CH}_4/\text{kg waste}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual waste deposition.**

$$
\dot{M} = \dfrac{500{,}000}{10} = 50{,}000 \text{ tonnes/yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Methane generated in year $t$ (since deposit).**

For waste deposited in year $j$ and now at age $\tau = t - j$:

$$
G_{\tau} = \dot{M} \times L_0 \times e^{-k\tau}
$$

**Step 4 — Total methane generation at $t = 15$ yr (sum over deposits from year 5 to year 15, since waste at $t=15$ is 5–15 years old).**

$$
G_{\text{total}} = \sum_{j=5}^{15} 50{,}000 \times 0.050 \times e^{-0.05(15-j)}
$$

In tonnes/year:
$$
G_{\text{total}} = 2500 \sum_{\tau=0}^{10} e^{-0.05\tau}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the geometric series.**

Sum of geometric series:
$$
S = \sum_{\tau=0}^{10} e^{-0.05\tau} = \dfrac{1 - e^{-0.55}}{1 - e^{-0.55}}
$$

Wait — re-evaluate: numerator $= 1 - e^{-0.55} = 1 - 0.5769 = 0.4231$; denominator $= 1 - 0.9512 = 0.0488$.

$$
S = \dfrac{0.4231}{0.0488} = 8.670
$$

**Step 6 — Compute total methane generation.**

$$
G_{\text{total}} = 2500 \times 8.670 = 21{,}675 \text{ tonnes CH}_4/\text{yr}
$$

Convert to volume at STP:
$$
V_{\text{CH}_4} = 21{,}675 \times 10^3 \text{ kg} \times \dfrac{22.414 \text{ m}^3/\text{kmol}}{16 \text{ kg/kmol}} = 21{,}675 \times 10^3 \times 1.4009 = 3.036 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}
$$

Convert to energy (LHV of CH₄ = 50 MJ/kg):
$$
E_{\text{annual}} = 21{,}675 \times 10^3 \times 50 = 1.084 \times 10^9 \text{ MJ/yr} = 301{,}000 \text{ MWh/yr}
$$

With 35% electrical conversion efficiency:
$$
P_{\text{electric}} = \dfrac{301{,}000 \text{ MWh}}{8760 \text{ h}} \approx 34.4 \text{ MW}_{\text{electric}} \text{ (continuous equivalent)}
$$

**Result:** This landfill can support a **~30 MW electrical** LFG-to-power plant (with capacity factor 0.85).
