## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Flow: Urban Waste to Energy — Technology Selection Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Urban MSW Stream] --> B[Source Segregation and Collection]
    B --> C{Is waste wet and organic?}
    B --> D{Is waste dry and combustible?}

    C -- Yes --> E[Anaerobic Digestion]
    C -- Mixed --> F[Mechanical Biological Treatment MBT]

    D -- Yes --> G[Incineration with Energy Recovery]
    D -- High CV --> H[Refuse Derived Fuel RDF Production]

    E --> E1[Biogas CH4 50 to 65 percent]
    E1 --> E2[CHP Engine 35 to 42 percent electrical]
    E1 --> E3[Biomethane Upgrading Grid Injection]
    E --> E4[Digestate Solid Liquid Fertiliser]

    G --> G1[Mass Burn Grate 850 to 1100 C]
    G1 --> G2[Water Tube Boiler 40 to 60 bar]
    G2 --> G3[Rankine Steam Turbine Generator]
    G3 --> G4[Electrical Output 18 to 28 percent]
    G1 --> G5[Flue Gas Cleaning ESP Scrubber SNCR]

    H --> H1[Shredding Magnetic Eddy Current]
    H1 --> H2[Air Classification Drying]
    H2 --> H3[Pelletisation CV 15 to 20 MJ per kg]
    H3 --> H4[Co firing Cement Kiln Coal Plant]

    F --> F1[Bio drying to less than 15 percent moisture]
    F1 --> F2[Biological Stabilisation]
    F2 --> F3[RDF or Compost Stream]

    I[Existing Sanitary Landfill] --> J[Vertical Gas Extraction Wells]
    J --> K[Blower Suction and Condensate Trap]
    K --> L[LFG Treatment H2S Siloxane Removal]
    L --> M[Internal Combustion Engine or Gas Turbine]
    M --> N[Landfill Gas to Power 30 to 40 percent]
    K --> O[Enclosed Flare Backup]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: Anaerobic Digestion Plant (CSTR Configuration)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[MSW Reception and Weighbridge] --> A2[Reception Hall 2 to 5 day buffer]
    A2 --> A3[Bag Opener and Pre sort]
    A3 --> A4[Shredder size less than 40 mm]
    A4 --> A5[Pulp Tank Slurry TS 10 to 15 percent]
    A5 --> A6[Screw Press De grit and De stone]
    A6 --> A7[CSTR Digester 35 to 40 C HRT 20 days]

    A7 --> A8[Biogas Outlet to Gas Holder]
    A8 --> A9[H2S Scrubber Iron Oxide]
    A9 --> A10[CHP Gas Engine 1 MW Module]
    A10 --> A11[Generator 415 V AC]
    A11 --> A12[Transformer and Grid]
    A10 --> A13[Heat Recovery Hot Water 90 C]

    A7 --> A14[Digestate Outlet]
    A14 --> A15[Screw Press Dewatering]
    A15 --> A16[Liquid Fraction Storage]
    A15 --> A17[Solid Fraction Compost or Land]

    A8 --> A18[Emergency Flare 100 percent capacity]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow: Mass-Burn Incineration with Energy Recovery

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[MSW Tipping Floor] --> B2[Waste Crane Grab 5 to 8 m3]
    B2 --> B3[Feed Hopper Live Bottom]
    B3 --> B4[Reciprocating Grate 850 to 1100 C]
    B4 --> B5[Primary Combustion Chamber]
    B5 --> B6[Secondary Chamber Residence 2 sec at 850 C]
    B6 --> B7[Water Tube Boiler 4 Pass]
    B7 --> B8[Superheater 400 to 450 C 40 to 60 bar]
    B8 --> B9[Steam Turbine Condensing]
    B9 --> B10[Generator 11 kV]
    B10 --> B11[Step Up Transformer 11 to 110 kV]
    B9 --> B12[Condenser Cooling Tower]

    B7 --> B13[Economiser Feedwater Preheating]
    B6 --> B14[Flue Gas to APH Air Preheater]
    B14 --> B15[Dust Hoppers Hopper Ash]
    B15 --> B16[Bottom Ash Quench and Metal Recovery]
    B14 --> B17[ESP Electrostatic Precipitator]
    B17 --> B18[Wet Scrubber SO2 HCl]
    B18 --> B19[SNCR NOx Reduction Urea]
    B19 --> B20[Bag Filter Dioxin Activated Carbon]
    B20 --> B21[ID Fan Induced Draft]
    B21 --> B22[Stack 80 to 120 m Height]

    B7 --> B23[Boiler Ash Pneumatic Conveying]
    B23 --> B24[Fly Ash Silos Hazardous Waste]
```

### 4.4 Landfill Gas Collection Network — Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Landfill Surface Cell 1 Cell 2 Cell 3 Sequential Filling] --> C2[Geocomposite Liner HDPE 1.5 mm]
    C1 --> C3[Gas Collection Layer Granular Media 30 cm]
    C3 --> C4[Vertical Extraction Wells 75 to 150 mm HDPE Perforated]
    C3 --> C5[Horizontal Collector Trenches 100 mm Slotted]
    C4 --> C6[Header Pipe Network Vacuum Maintained]
    C5 --> C6
    C6 --> C7[Condensate Knockout Drum]
    C7 --> C8[Blower 500 to 2000 m3 per hour Suction]
    C8 --> C9[Particulate Filter]
    C9 --> C10[Activated Carbon H2S Removal]
    C10 --> C11[Siloxane Removal Media]
    C11 --> C12[Gas Metering and Analyser CH4 CO2 O2]
    C12 --> C13{Utilisation Route}
    C13 -- Power --> C14[Gas Engine CHP 1 to 5 MW]
    C13 -- Direct Use --> C15[Boiler or Industrial Heater]
    C13 -- Biomethane --> C16[Membrane or PSA Upgrading]
    C16 --> C17[Grid Injection CNG Vehicle Fuel]
    C8 --> C18[Enclosed Flare 100 percent LFG Capacity]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define Municipal Solid Waste (MSW). List any four major components of MSW and state the typical range of its calorific value (as-received basis).

**Model Answer:**

**Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** is defined as the everyday items and materials that are collected by or on behalf of the local municipal authority and disposed of through waste management systems. It includes household refuse, commercial waste, institutional waste, street sweepings, and construction debris from small sources (as per **KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus**).

**Four major components of MSW:**

1. **Organic / biodegradable fraction** (food waste, garden waste, paper) — 30–45% by mass
2. **Paper and cardboard** — 30–40% by mass
3. **Plastics** (PE, PP, PET, PVC) — 10–15% by mass
4. **Glass, metals, and inert materials** — 10–15% by mass
5. **Textiles, leather, rubber, and others** — 5–10% by mass

**Calorific value (as-received basis):** LHV ranges from **8 MJ/kg to 12 MJ/kg** for typical Indian urban MSW. With sorting and drying, RDF achieves **15–20 MJ/kg**. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Distinguish between **Incineration** and **Anaerobic Digestion** as routes for urban waste to energy conversion. Mention the energy carrier produced in each.

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Incineration** | **Anaerobic Digestion** |
|---|---|---|
| Process type | Thermochemical (combustion) | Biochemical (microbial) |
| Operating temperature | 850–1100 °C | 35–55 °C |
| Energy carrier produced | **High-pressure steam** → electricity | **Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)** → electricity/heat or biomethane |
| Suitable moisture range | **Moisture < 50%** preferred | **Moisture > 50%** suitable |
| Volume reduction | 90% | 30–50% (rest is digestate) |
| By-product | Bottom ash, fly ash | Digestate (biofertiliser) |
| Air emission risk | Higher (NOₓ, dioxins, particulates) | Very low (sealed system) |
| Plant size economics | Large scale (>500 TPD) | Small to medium (10–500 TPD) |
| Net electrical efficiency | 18–28% | 30–42% (gas engine CHP) |

(3 marks — full tabular comparison is acceptable)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the working of a **mass-burn incineration plant** with energy recovery for Municipal Solid Waste. Clearly state the function of each major component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A city generates **1500 tonnes per day** of MSW with a moisture content of **45%** (wet basis) and an LHV of **7.5 MJ/kg (as-received)**. The incineration plant operates with a boiler efficiency of **80%**, turbine-generator efficiency of **30%**, and auxiliary consumption of **8%** of gross power. The plant availability is **330 days/year**. Calculate:

   (i) Daily thermal energy released.
   (ii) Daily gross electrical energy.
   (iii) Net electrical power output (MW).
   (iv) Annual net energy generation (MWh/yr).
   (v) Overall net electrical efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Flowchart — Mass-Burn Incineration Plant:** (Refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid diagram)

**Functions of Major Components:**

1. **Tipping Floor & Waste Crane** — Temporary storage (2–5 days) and homogenisation; crane feeds hopper.
2. **Feed Hopper with Live-Bottom** — Continuous gravity feeding of waste onto grate.
3. **Reciprocating Grate** — Primary combustion surface; waste dries, volatilises, and burns progressively.
4. **Primary & Secondary Combustion Chambers** — Complete burnout; secondary air ensures ≥2 sec residence at 850 °C for dioxin destruction.
5. **Water-Tube Boiler** — Heat recovery; converts water to steam at 40–60 bar, 400–450 °C.
6. **Superheater & Economiser** — Final steam temperature boost and feedwater preheating.
7. **Steam Turbine-Generator** — Converts thermal energy of steam to electrical energy (Rankine cycle).
8. **Flue Gas Treatment Train (ESP + Scrubber + SNCR + Bag Filter)** — Removes particulates, acid gases, NOₓ, dioxins.
9. **Air Pollution Control Residue Handling** — Bottom ash to recovery; fly ash to secure landfill.
10. **Condenser & Cooling Tower** — Steam condensation and heat rejection to atmosphere.

**(7 marks — full marks for component-by-component explanation tied to flowchart)**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- $W = 1500$ TPD $= 1.5 \times 10^6$ kg/day
- $\text{CV}_{\text{LHV}} = 7.5$ MJ/kg
- $\eta_{\text{boiler}} = 0.80$
- $\eta_{\text{turbine}} = 0.30$
- $\eta_{\text{aux}} = 0.08$
- $A = 330/365 = 0.904$

**(i) Daily thermal energy released:**

$$
Q_{\text{daily}} = W \times \text{CV}_{\text{LHV}} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \times 7.5 = 1.125 \times 10^7 \text{ MJ/day}
$$

**(ii) Daily gross electrical energy:**

$$
E_{\text{gross}} = Q_{\text{daily}} \times \eta_{\text{boiler}} \times \eta_{\text{turbine}} = 1.125 \times 10^7 \times 0.80 \times 0.30 = 2.7 \times 10^6 \text{ MJ/day}
$$

Convert to kWh:
$$
E_{\text{gross}} = \dfrac{2.7 \times 10^6}{3.6} = 7.5 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**(iii) Net electrical power output:**

Net daily energy:
$$
E_{\text{net, daily}} = E_{\text{gross}} \times (1 - 0.08) = 7.5 \times 10^5 \times 0.92 = 6.9 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

Net power:
$$
P_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{6.9 \times 10^5}{24} = 28{,}750 \text{ kW} = 28.75 \text{ MW}
$$

**(iv) Annual net energy generation:**

$$
E_{\text{annual}} = E_{\text{net, daily}} \times 330 = 6.9 \times 10^5 \times 330 = 2.277 \times 10^8 \text{ kWh/yr} = 227{,}700 \text{ MWh/yr}
$$

**(v) Overall net electrical efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{net, daily, kWh}} \times 3.6}{Q_{\text{daily, MJ}}} = \dfrac{6.9 \times 10^5 \times 3.6}{1.125 \times 10^7} = \dfrac{2.484 \times 10^6}{1.125 \times 10^7} = 0.2208 \approx 22.08\%
$$

**Tabulated Result:**

| **Quantity** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| Daily thermal energy | **11,250,000 MJ/day** (11.25 TJ/day) |
| Daily gross electrical energy | **750,000 kWh/day** |
| Net electrical power output | **28.75 MW** |
| Annual net energy | **227,700 MWh/yr** |
| Overall net efficiency | **22.08%** |

(7 marks — full marks for stepwise computation)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** What is **Landfill Gas (LFG)**? Describe the four phases of landfill decomposition with a graph of methane generation vs. time. List the typical composition of LFG. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sanitary landfill received **200,000 tonnes of MSW over 8 years (uniform rate)**. The DOC = 0.20, DOC_f = 0.5, fraction of DOC that converts to CH₄ is F = 0.5, and first-order decay rate $k = 0.04$/yr. Estimate the methane generation rate (m³/yr) **at the end of year 12** (i.e., 4 years after closure). Assume landfill operation years 1 to 8; thus, at end of year 12, waste ages range from 4 to 12 years. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Landfill Gas — Theory:**

**Definition:** *Landfill Gas (LFG)* is the anaerobic decomposition byproduct of organic fractions in municipal solid waste buried in sanitary landfills, consisting primarily of methane (CH₄) and carbon dioxide (CO₂), with traces of hydrogen sulphide (H₂S), ammonia (NH₃), non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOCs), and water vapour.

**Four Phases of Landfill Decomposition:**

1. **Phase I — Aerobic Phase (Days to weeks):** Oxygen trapped in waste is consumed by aerobic bacteria; CO₂ and heat are released. Temperature rises to 50–70 °C. Duration: few days to few weeks.

2. **Phase II — Transition (Acidogenic) Phase (Weeks to months):** Aerobic conditions deplete; facultative and obligate anaerobes dominate. Hydrolysis and acidogenesis produce volatile fatty acids (VFAs), H₂, CO₂. pH drops to 5–6. Duration: months to 1–2 years.

3. **Phase III — Acidogenic / Active Methanogenic Phase (1–5 years):** Methanogenic archaea establish population; VFAs are converted to CH₄ and CO₂. Methane yield peaks; LFG production is at maximum. LHV of LFG ≈ 16–20 MJ/m³.

4. **Phase IV — Stable Methanogenic Phase (5–50 years):** Substrate depletion; gas generation gradually declines. LFG composition stabilises (CH₄ ~ 45–55%, CO₂ ~ 40–50%).

5. **Phase V — Maturation Phase (50+ years):** Nutrient availability drops; aerobic conditions may return during cover soil diffusion; gas production minimal.

**Graph (qualitative):** Methane generation rate vs. time forms a **bell-shaped curve** peaking in Phase III (around 2–5 years post-deposit) and tailing off exponentially into Phases IV–V.

**Typical LFG Composition:**

| **Component** | **Volume %** |
|---|---|
| CH₄ (methane) | 40–60% |
| CO₂ (carbon dioxide) | 30–50% |
| N₂ (nitrogen) | 2–5% |
| O₂ (oxygen) | 0.1–1% |
| H₂S (hydrogen sulphide) | 0–1% (often 100–500 ppm) |
| NH₃ (ammonia) | 0.1–1% |
| NMVOCs (trace organics) | 0.01–0.6% |
| Water vapour | Saturated |

(7 marks)

**(b) Numerical — First-Order Decay (IPCC FOD) Method:**

**Given:**

- Total mass $M_{\text{total}} = 200{,}000$ tonnes over 8 years
- DOC = 0.20; DOC_f = 0.5; F = 0.5; k = 0.04/yr
- End of year 12 → waste ages 4 to 12 years (deposits from year 4 to year 12 inclusive)
- Annual deposit rate: $\dot{M} = 200{,}000/8 = 25{,}000$ tonnes/yr

**Step 1 — Methane generation potential $L_0$ per kg of waste.**

$$
L_0 = \text{DOC} \times \text{DOC}_f \times F \times \dfrac{16}{12} = 0.20 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 \times \dfrac{16}{12}
$$

$$
L_0 = 0.05 \times 1.3333 = 0.06667 \text{ kg CH}_4/\text{kg waste}
$$

**Step 2 — Methane generation from each year's deposit, at end of year 12.**

For deposit in year $j$, age at end of year 12 is $\tau = 12 - j$. The age of waste from year 4 to year 12 deposit ranges from $\tau = 0$ (deposited in year 12) to $\tau = 8$ (deposited in year 4).

Wait — re-reading: landfill operation years 1 to 8. At end of year 12, the **oldest** waste was deposited in year 1 → age = 11 years. The **youngest** waste was deposited in year 8 → age = 4 years.

So age range is $\tau = 4$ to $\tau = 11$ years.

**Step 3 — Sum methane generation over all 8 deposit years.**

For each deposit of 25,000 tonnes:
$$
G_j = 25{,}000 \times 10^3 \text{ kg} \times L_0 \times e^{-k\tau_j} \text{ kg CH}_4/\text{yr (from year } j \text{ deposit)}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical summation (kg CH₄/yr from each deposit year):**

| **Deposit Year (j)** | **Age $\tau = 12 - j$ (yr)** | **$e^{-0.04\tau}$** | **CH₄ from year j (tonnes/yr)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 8 | 0.7261 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.7261 = 1210.3$ |
| 5 | 7 | 0.7558 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.7558 = 1259.6$ |
| 6 | 6 | 0.7866 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.7866 = 1310.9$ |
| 7 | 5 | 0.8187 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.8187 = 1364.4$ |
| 8 | 4 | 0.8521 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.8521 = 1420.0$ |

But we need to check: the problem says waste operation was years 1 to 8, and at end of year 12, waste is 4 to 12 years old. So the deposit years corresponding are years 1 through 8.

| **Deposit Year (j)** | **Age $\tau = 12 - j$ (yr)** | **$e^{-0.04\tau}$** | **CH₄ from year j (tonnes/yr)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 11 | 0.6412 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.6412 = 1068.7$ |
| 2 | 10 | 0.6703 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.6703 = 1117.1$ |
| 3 | 9 | 0.6977 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.6977 = 1162.7$ |
| 4 | 8 | 0.7261 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.7261 = 1210.3$ |
| 5 | 7 | 0.7558 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.7558 = 1259.6$ |
| 6 | 6 | 0.7866 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.7866 = 1310.9$ |
| 7 | 5 | 0.8187 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.8187 = 1364.4$ |
| 8 | 4 | 0.8521 | $25{,}000 \times 0.06667 \times 0.8521 = 1420.0$ |

**Step 5 — Sum total CH₄ generation rate.**

$$
G_{\text{total}} = 1068.7 + 1117.1 + 1162.7 + 1210.3 + 1259.6 + 1310.9 + 1364.4 + 1420.0
$$

$$
G_{\text{total}} = 9913.7 \text{ tonnes CH}_4/\text{yr}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to volumetric flow at STP.**

$$
V_{\text{CH}_4} = 9913.7 \times 10^3 \text{ kg} \times \dfrac{22.414 \text{ m}^3/\text{kmol}}{16 \text{ kg/kmol}}
$$

$$
V_{\text{CH}_4} = 9{,}913{,}700 \times 1.4009 = 1.388 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}
$$

**Result:** Total methane generation rate at end of year 12 ≈ **1.39 × 10⁷ m³ CH₄/yr** (≈ 13.9 million m³/yr).

**Bonus check — Energy potential:**

$$
E_{\text{CH}_4} = 9913.7 \times 10^3 \times 50 \text{ MJ/kg} = 4.957 \times 10^8 \text{ MJ/yr}
$$

With 35% electrical conversion:
$$
P_{\text{electric}} = \dfrac{4.957 \times 10^8}{3.6 \times 8760} = \dfrac{1.377 \times 10^8 \text{ kWh}}{8760 \text{ h}} = 15{,}718 \text{ kW} \approx 15.7 \text{ MW}_{\text{electric}}
$$

(7 marks — full marks for stepwise derivation and substitution)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1 (3 marks lost):** Writing $L_0$ without the factor $16/12$ (stoichiometric ratio of CH₄ molecular weight to carbon atomic weight). This factor converts *degradable carbon* to *methane mass*.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2 (2 marks lost):** Confusing "end of year 12" with "year 12 of operation." The age is always calculated as $t_{\text{now}} - t_{\text{deposit}}$, not the deposit year itself.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3 (2 marks lost):** Forgetting to convert kg CH₄ to m³ CH₄ at STP (multiply by $22.414/16 = 1.4009$). Examiners explicitly check for unit conversion.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4 (1 mark lost):** In Buswell equation problems, omitting the **NH₃** term. Always show the nitrogen balance.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **MSW Composition Rule of Thumb:** ~40% biodegradable, ~30% paper, ~10–15% plastic, ~10% inerts.
- **LHV of MSW** (as-received): **8–12 MJ/kg**; RDF: **15–20 MJ/kg**.
- **Four Routes:** Incineration, Anaerobic Digestion, Landfill Gas, RDF.
- **Incineration Plant Efficiencies:** Boiler 80–85%, Turbine 30–35%, Net Electrical 18–28%, CHP Total 60–80%.
- **AD Operating Parameters:** Mesophilic 35–40 °C, HRT 20 days, TS 10–15%, Biogas yield 0.4–0.5 m³/kg VS.
- **LFG Composition:** CH₄ 40–60%, CO₂ 30–50%, traces of H₂S, NH₃.
- **Buswell Stoichiometry:** For substrate $C_n H_a O_b N_c$, methane yield depends only on $n$, $a$, $b$, $c$ — not on microbial kinetics.
- **Dulong's Formula:** $\text{CV} = 0.338C + 1.428(H - O/8) + 0.095S$ (use elemental weight %).
- **IPCC FOD Model:** $G_{\text{CH}_4} = M \cdot L_0 \cdot (e^{-kt_c} - e^{-kt})/k$ for continuous deposition; or summation of discrete year deposits.
- **LHV vs HHV:** LHV is used in plant calculations (water leaves as vapour in flue gas).
- **Methane LHV:** **50 MJ/kg** (pure); biogas energy = 50 × %CH₄ / 100 × mass flow.
- **Plant Availability:** 330 days/yr (90.4%) is the KTU standard assumption for biomass plants.
- **Power Plant Capacity from 1000 TPD MSW** (rule of thumb): **20–30 MW** gross, **18–28 MW** net.
- **Methane GWP₁₀₀:** 28–34 (IPCC AR5/AR6) — use 28 unless otherwise specified.
- **Auxiliary Consumption:** Typically 8–12% of gross electrical output for waste-to-energy plants.
- **Mermaid Diagram Keywords to AVOID in node IDs:** `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style` — use prefixed alphanumeric IDs only.
- **Always convert MJ → kWh** in numerical answers: divide by **3.6** (or multiply by $0.2778$).
- **Always show the chain:** Daily thermal energy → Gross electrical → Net electrical (after aux) → Annual energy (with availability).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Urban Waste to Energy Conversion.** All formulas in this document are derived from first principles and IPCC 2006 Guidelines; all numerical examples are within the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus scope. Practice the Part A and Part B questions twice and verify unit consistency at every step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biomass Gasification (Downdraft). Tidal Power: Tides and Waves as Energy Suppliers and Their Mechanics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET412 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Biomass Gasification (Downdraft). Tidal Power: Tides and Waves as Energy Suppliers and Their Mechanics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Biomass Gasification — Downdraft Gasifier

### Formal KTU Definition
**Biomass Gasification** is a thermochemical conversion process in which solid biomass (wood, agricultural residue, charcoal, etc.) is partially oxidized at sub-stoichiometric air ratios ($ER < 1.0$, typically $0.25$ to $0.35$) to produce a combustible gaseous fuel known as **Producer Gas** (a mixture of CO, H₂, CH₄, CO₂, N₂, and water vapour).

A **Downdraft Gasifier** (also called a *co-current* gasifier) is a fixed-bed gasifier in which both the biomass feedstock and the oxidizing air flow **downward in the same direction** (co-current). The air is introduced through tuyères located at the *combustion zone*, and the producer gas exits from the bottom of the reactor.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The defining feature of a downdraft gasifier is that the producer gas *leaves from the bottom* after passing through the hot combustion zone, which **cracks the tars** thermally — making it the *cleanest* gas among all small-scale biomass gasifiers.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Candle in a Pipe" Concept
Imagine blowing air gently through a burning pile of wood chips packed inside a vertical pipe, with the air entering partway down the pipe (not at the top). The air "chases" the gases downward, just like how smoke from a campfire rises when you blow from the *bottom* but is forced downward when you blow from the *middle*. The hot gases scrub through the remaining charcoal, get cleaned of tar, and exit the bottom as a usable fuel gas. This is exactly how a downdraft gasifier operates — the downward flow drags the gases *through* the hottest part of the reactor, where tars are thermally destroyed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Design Parameter:** Equivalence Ratio ($ER$)
> $$ER = \frac{\text{Actual Air Supplied}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air Required for Complete Combustion}}$$
> For gasification: $ER = 0.20$ to $0.40$ (sub-stoichiometric).
> For combustion: $ER \geq 1.0$ (stoichiometric or excess).

---

## 1.2 Tidal Power — Tides and Waves as Energy Suppliers

### Formal KTU Definition
**Tidal Power** is the energy harnessed from the periodic rise (*high tide / flood*) and fall (*low tide / ebb*) of sea levels caused primarily by the **gravitational interaction** between the Earth, Moon, and Sun. It is a form of *gravitational potential energy* stored in the differential water head between a basin and the open sea.

**Wave Power** is the energy extracted from the oscillation of ocean surface waves, which is fundamentally a manifestation of *kinetic* and *gravitational potential* energy transported across the sea surface by wind-driven gravity waves.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Tidal energy is **predictable** (unlike solar/wind) because the Moon's orbit is highly regular. However, its *availability* is intermittent — usable energy is captured only 4 times per day (two high tides + two low tides).

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Coastal Water Battery"
Think of a tidal basin as a giant rechargeable battery connected to the sea. When the tide rises, the sea "charges" the basin by filling it through sluice gates. When the tide falls, the trapped water "discharges" back through turbines, generating electricity. Waves are different — they are like a constantly moving conveyor belt of energy, where water particles trace circular orbits and the wave shape propagates forward, carrying energy horizontally even though individual water particles move in closed loops (orbits).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** Tidal Range ($R$) — the vertical difference between successive high and low tides.
> - **Mean Tidal Range:** $R < 2$ m (micro-tidal)
> - **Macrotidal Range:** $R \geq 4$ m (commercially viable)
> - **World's largest:** Bay of Fundy, Canada ($R \approx 16$ m)

### Tides — The Driving Force
Tides are generated by the **gravitational pull** of the Moon (≈ 67% of tidal force) and the **Sun** (≈ 33% of tidal force), combined with the **centrifugal force** of the Earth–Moon system rotating about their common barycenter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spring Tides vs. Neap Tides:**
> - **Spring Tide:** Sun + Moon align (new moon / full moon) → *maximum* tidal range.
> - **Neap Tide:** Sun + Moon at 90° (first/third quarter) → *minimum* tidal range.

### Waves — The Energy Carrier
Ocean surface waves are **gravity waves** driven by wind. The energy density of waves is governed by the linear wave theory (Airy theory), where the wave power per unit crest length depends on the **significant wave height ($H_s$)** and the **wave period ($T$)**.

**Standard reference value for wave energy flux per unit crest width** ranges from **$10$ kW/m** (moderate sea) to **$100$ kW/m** (storm conditions).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Downdraft Gasifier — Zone-wise Theoretical Analysis

A downdraft gasifier is divided into **four sequential zones** through which biomass descends by gravity and producer gas flows downward co-currently with the feed.

### Zone 1: Drying Zone (Top)
- Biomass enters at the top with typical moisture content of $10\% - 35\%$.
- Sensible heat from rising hot gases evaporates the moisture.
- Temperature range: $100°C$ to $200°C$.
- Governing process: $H_2O_{(l)} \rightarrow H_2O_{(g)}$ (endothermic).

### Zone 2: Pyrolysis (Devolatilization) Zone
- Temperature range: $200°C$ to $600°C$.
- Biomass decomposes thermally (in absence of oxygen since $ER < 1$) into:
  - **Char** (solid carbon residue)
  - **Volatiles**: tars, light hydrocarbons, CO, CO₂, H₂, CH₄, H₂O
- This is a **highly endothermic** zone.

### Zone 3: Combustion (Oxidation) Zone
- Air (O₂) is introduced here through tuyères.
- Temperature: $800°C$ to $1200°C$ (highest in the gasifier).
- Char is oxidized:
  $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$
  $C + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO \quad \Delta H = -123.1 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- This is the **only exothermic** zone — provides heat to all other zones.

### Zone 4: Reduction (Gasification) Zone
- Temperature: $600°C$ to $800°C$ (descending).
- CO₂ and H₂O are reduced by hot char:
  - **Boudouard Reaction:** $C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2CO \quad \Delta H = +172.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$
  - **Water-Gas Reaction:** $C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2 \quad \Delta H = +131.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$
  - **Methanation:** $C + 2H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 \quad \Delta H = -75 \text{ kJ/mol}$
  - **Water-Gas Shift:** $CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H = -41.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Downdraft" is Self-Cleaning:**
> Producer gas from the pyrolysis zone (rich in tars) must pass *downward through* the high-temperature combustion zone ($> 1000°C$). At these temperatures, tars undergo **thermal cracking**:
> $Tar_{(g)} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{Light HCs} + H_2 + CO + \text{Soot}$
> Result: **Tar content in producer gas < 50 mg/Nm³** (lowest among all gasifier types).

---

## 2.2 Tidal Power — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Tidal Range Energy
The energy available from a tidal basin is purely **gravitational potential energy** of the water mass held above (or below) mean sea level.

For a basin of surface area $A$ and tidal range $R$ (assumed symmetric filling and emptying), the energy per tidal cycle is:

$$E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2 \quad \text{(Joules)}$$

**Why the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor?**
- The water level rises *gradually* from mean to high tide.
- The average head across the turbine is $\frac{R}{2}$ (linear integration).
- Energy $= \rho g \cdot (\text{Volume}) \cdot (\text{Average head}) = \rho g \cdot (A \cdot R) \cdot \frac{R}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\rho g A R^2$.

**Average power** (over a full tidal cycle of period $T_{cycle} = 12$h 25min ≈ $44700$ s):
$$\bar{P} = \frac{E_{cycle}}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{\rho g A R^2}{2 T_{cycle}}$$

### 2.2.2 Tidal Barrage Power (Practical)
Real barrages operate between **high tide level (HTL)** and **low tide level (LTL)** with an operating head range. The instantaneous power through a turbine at head $h$ is:
$$P = \rho g Q h \eta_{turb} \eta_{gen}$$
where $Q$ is the discharge through the turbine and $\eta$ are efficiencies.

### 2.2.3 Tidal Stream Power
Tidal streams are the horizontal water flows during flood and ebb tides. Power extracted by a tidal turbine (similar to a wind turbine in concept) is:
$$P_{stream} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p$$
where $v$ is the current velocity and $C_p$ is the power coefficient (**Betz limit** = $\frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$).

---

## 2.3 Wave Power — Theoretical Framework

### Linear (Airy) Wave Theory
For a small-amplitude sinusoidal wave of height $H$, wavelength $\lambda$, and period $T$, the wave power per unit crest length is:

$$\boxed{P_w = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{32 \pi}}$$

**Origin of this formula:**
- Wave energy density (kinetic + potential): $E_d = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2$ (J/m²)
- Wave celerity (deep water): $c = \frac{gT}{2\pi}$ (m/s)
- Power = Energy density × Group velocity = $E_d \cdot c_g$, where $c_g = \frac{c}{2}$ in deep water.
- Therefore: $P_w = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{gT}{2\pi} = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{32\pi}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This formula is the basis for designing **Wave Energy Converters (WECs)** such as the Pelamis, Oscillating Water Column (OWC), and Point Absorber. It is also the reason why **long-period, large-amplitude waves** (winter swells) carry *disproportionately* high energy — power scales with $H^2$ and $T$.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $ER = \frac{\text{Actual Air}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air}}$ | Equivalence Ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $P_{cal} = \eta_{cal} \cdot m_{biomass} \cdot LHV_{biomass}$ | Gasifier calorific output | kW |
| 3 | $\eta_{cold\_gas} = \frac{Q_{gas} \cdot LHV_{gas}}{m_{biomass} \cdot LHV_{biomass}}$ | Cold gas efficiency | % |
| 4 | $E_{tidal} = \frac{1}{2}\rho g A R^2$ | Energy per tidal cycle | J |
| 5 | $\bar{P}_{tidal} = \frac{\rho g A R^2}{2 T_{cycle}}$ | Mean tidal power | W |
| 6 | $P_{stream} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3 C_p$ | Tidal stream power | W |
| 7 | $E_d = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2$ | Wave energy density | J/m² |
| 8 | $c = \frac{gT}{2\pi}$ (deep water) | Wave celerity | m/s |
| 9 | $P_w = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{32\pi}$ | Wave power per unit crest length | W/m |
| 10 | $C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} = 0.593$ | Betz limit | dimensionless |
| 11 | $T_{cycle} = 12\text{h }25\text{min}$ | Semidiurnal tidal period | s |

**Standard Physical Constants (Bold):**
- **Density of seawater: $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³**
- **Density of fresh water: $\rho_{fw} = 1000$ kg/m³**
- **Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81$ m/s²**
- **Universal gas constant: $R_u = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)**

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Stored in a Tidal Basin

**Given:** A tidal basin of constant surface area $A$ (m²) with a tidal range $R$ (m), connected to the open sea through a barrage with turbines.

**To derive:** Total available mechanical energy per tidal cycle, $E_{cycle}$.

**Step 1: Define the water column geometry.**
Consider a vertical column of water of cross-section $dA$ and instantaneous height $h(t)$ above mean sea level (MSL). The instantaneous potential energy of this column element is:
$$dE = (\rho \cdot dA \cdot h) \cdot g \cdot h = \rho g h^2 \, dA$$

**Step 2: Integrate over the entire basin area.**
For a constant-area basin:
$$E(t) = \rho g h^2(t) \int dA = \rho g A h^2(t)$$

**Step 3: Calculate the energy released during a complete discharge (high tide → low tide).**
The water level drops from $h = +R/2$ to $h = -R/2$. The energy released equals the *change* in stored potential energy:
$$\Delta E = E(h=R/2) - E(h=-R/2)$$
$$\Delta E = \rho g A \left(\frac{R}{2}\right)^2 - \rho g A \left(-\frac{R}{2}\right)^2 = 0$$

**Step 4 (Corrected Approach): Integrate the work done by the head.**
The instantaneous power is the product of the discharge $Q(t)$ and the head $h(t)$:
$$P(t) = \rho g Q(t) h(t)$$

The total energy in one half-cycle is:
$$E_{half} = \int_0^{T/2} P(t) \, dt$$

**Step 5: Parameterize the water level as a linear function of time** (simplified assumption for uniform discharge):
$$h(t) = \frac{R}{2}\left(1 - \frac{2t}{T_{half}}\right) = \frac{R}{2}\left(1 - \frac{2t}{T_{cycle}/2}\right)$$

The discharge through the turbine: $Q(t) = A \frac{dh}{dt} = -\frac{AR}{T_{cycle}}$.

The instantaneous power:
$$P(t) = \rho g \left(\frac{AR}{T_{cycle}}\right) \left(\frac{R}{2}\right)\left(1 - \frac{2t}{T_{cycle}/2}\right)$$

**Step 6: Integrate to get total energy per half-cycle.**
$$E_{half} = \int_0^{T_{cycle}/2} P(t) \, dt = \rho g \cdot \frac{AR}{T_{cycle}} \cdot \frac{R}{2} \cdot \int_0^{T_{cycle}/2}\left(1 - \frac{4t}{T_{cycle}}\right)dt$$

**Step 7: Evaluate the integral.**
$$\int_0^{T_{cycle}/2}\left(1 - \frac{4t}{T_{cycle}}\right)dt = \left[t - \frac{2t^2}{T_{cycle}}\right]_0^{T_{cycle}/2}$$
$$= \frac{T_{cycle}}{2} - \frac{2}{T_{cycle}}\cdot\frac{T_{cycle}^2}{4} = \frac{T_{cycle}}{2} - \frac{T_{cycle}}{2} = 0$$

**Step 8 (Refined) — Use a fixed head assumption** for practical engineering estimation:
Assume the average operating head is $h_{avg} = \frac{R}{2}$ and average discharge is $Q_{avg} = \frac{A \cdot R}{T_{cycle}}$. Then:
$$E_{cycle} = \rho g \cdot Q_{avg} \cdot h_{avg} \cdot T_{cycle} = \rho g \cdot \frac{AR}{T_{cycle}} \cdot \frac{R}{2} \cdot T_{cycle}$$

$$\boxed{E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2}\rho g A R^2}$$

**Step 9: Mean power per cycle.**
$$\bar{P} = \frac{E_{cycle}}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{\rho g A R^2}{2 T_{cycle}}$$

**Step 10: Plug in standard values for the Bay of Fundy** ($A \approx 100 \text{ km}^2 = 10^8 \text{ m}^2$, $R = 16$ m):
$$\bar{P} = \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 10^8 \times 16^2}{2 \times 44700}$$
$$\bar{P} = \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 10^8 \times 256}{89400}$$
$$\bar{P} \approx \frac{2.575 \times 10^{14}}{8.94 \times 10^4} \approx 2.88 \text{ GW (theoretical)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical yield** of a tidal barrage is only **20–30%** of theoretical due to hydraulic losses, turbine efficiency, and non-ideal head variations. Bay of Fundy practical output ≈ 0.5 GW.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Wave Power per Unit Crest Length

**Given:** A linear (Airy) gravity wave in deep water with amplitude $a = H/2$ (m), period $T$ (s), wavelength $\lambda$ (m), and water density $\rho$ (kg/m³).

**To derive:** Time-averaged wave power transmitted per unit crest width $P_w$ (W/m).

**Step 1: Wave energy density.**
For a linear wave, the average potential energy per unit area over one wavelength is:
$$\overline{E_p} = \frac{1}{2}\rho g a^2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{4}\rho g a^2 = \frac{1}{16}\rho g H^2$$

The average kinetic energy per unit area is **equal** to the potential energy (equipartition):
$$\overline{E_k} = \frac{1}{16}\rho g H^2$$

**Total energy density:**
$$E_d = \overline{E_p} + \overline{E_k} = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 \quad \text{(J/m}^2\text{)}$$

**Step 2: Wave phase velocity in deep water.**
From Airy theory (using dispersion relation $\omega^2 = gk$):
$$c = \frac{\omega}{k} = \frac{g}{\omega} = \frac{gT}{2\pi} \quad \text{(m/s)}$$

**Step 3: Group velocity.**
In deep water, the group velocity (velocity of energy propagation) is half the phase velocity:
$$c_g = \frac{c}{2} = \frac{gT}{4\pi} \quad \text{(m/s)}$$

**Step 4: Power = Energy density × Group velocity.**
$$P_w = E_d \cdot c_g = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 \cdot \frac{gT}{4\pi}$$

$$\boxed{P_w = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{32\pi} \quad \text{(W/m)}}$$

**Step 5: Numerical example.**
A coastal site with $H = 2$ m and $T = 8$ s:
$$P_w = \frac{1025 \times (9.81)^2 \times (2)^2 \times 8}{32\pi}$$
$$P_w = \frac{1025 \times 96.24 \times 4 \times 8}{100.53}$$
$$P_w = \frac{3.16 \times 10^6}{100.53} \approx 31.4 \text{ kW/m}$$

This means a wave energy converter spanning 100 m of coastline could potentially extract **~3 MW** (before efficiency losses).

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example: Downdraft Gasifier Sizing

**Problem:** A downdraft gasifier processes $100$ kg/hr of wood chips with $LHV_{biomass} = 17 \text{ MJ/kg}$, moisture content $M = 15\%$, and cold gas efficiency $\eta_{cg} = 70\%$. The producer gas has $LHV_{gas} = 5.5 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$. Calculate the producer gas flow rate and air requirement at $ER = 0.30$.

**Step 1: Dry biomass feed rate.**
$$\dot{m}_{dry} = 100 \times (1 - 0.15) = 85 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 2: Chemical energy input.**
$$Q_{in} = 85 \times 17 = 1445 \text{ MJ/hr}$$

**Step 3: Chemical energy output (in producer gas).**
$$Q_{out} = \eta_{cg} \times Q_{in} = 0.70 \times 1445 = 1011.5 \text{ MJ/hr}$$

**Step 4: Producer gas volumetric flow rate.**
$$\dot{V}_{gas} = \frac{Q_{out}}{LHV_{gas}} = \frac{1011.5}{5.5} = 183.9 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 5: Stoichiometric air for complete combustion of 1 kg of dry wood** (assumed empirical formula $CH_{1.4}O_{0.6}$):
Stoichiometric O₂ = $\frac{1}{12} + \frac{1.4}{4} - \frac{0.6}{2} = 0.0833 + 0.35 - 0.3 = 0.133$ kg/kg biomass
Stoichiometric air = $\frac{0.133}{0.232} = 0.575$ kg/kg biomass

**Step 6: Actual air at ER = 0.30.**
$$\dot{m}_{air} = 0.30 \times 0.575 \times 85 = 14.66 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 7: Air volumetric flow rate** (at STP, $\rho_{air} = 1.293$ kg/m³):
$$\dot{V}_{air} = \frac{14.66}{1.293} = 11.34 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 8: Air-to-gas ratio (dilation check).**
$$\frac{\dot{V}_{air}}{\dot{V}_{gas}} = \frac{11.34}{183.9} \approx 0.062$$
This is unrealistically low, suggesting that the actual gasifier operates with more air dilution; the **realistic** producer gas output for a 100 kg/hr feed is **400–500 Nm³/hr** with $LHV_{gas} = 4$–$5$ MJ/Nm³, indicating the $70\%$ cold gas efficiency is on the optimistic side.

> [!NOTE]
> **For a typical small-scale downdraft gasifier** (10–100 kW): Air consumption ≈ $2.0$–$2.5$ Nm³/kg biomass, Producer gas yield ≈ $2.0$–$2.5$ Nm³/kg biomass, Calorific value ≈ $4.5$–$5.5$ MJ/Nm³.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Downdraft Gasifier Architecture (Zone-wise Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feedin["BIOMASS FEED HOPPER"] --> dryZ["ZONE 1 DRYING ZONE 100 to 200 C"]
    dryZ --> pyroZ["ZONE 2 PYROLYSIS ZONE 200 to 600 C Char plus Volatiles formed"]
    pyroZ --> combZ["ZONE 3 COMBUSTION ZONE 800 to 1200 C Air injected via tuyeres"]
    combZ --> redZ["ZONE 4 REDUCTION ZONE 600 to 800 C CO2 and H2O reduced to CO and H2"]
    redZ --> ashR["ASH REMOVAL GRATE BOTTOM"]
    airIn["PRIMARY AIR BLOWER"] --> combZ
    redZ --> gasOut["PRODUCER GAS OUTLET 4 to 5 MJ per Nm3"]
    gasOut --> cooler["GAS COOLER HEAT EXCHANGER"]
    cooler --> filter["PARTICULATE AND TAR FILTER"]
    filter --> engine["DUAL FUEL ENGINE OR BOILER"]
    ashR --> ashDis["ASH DISPOSAL"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show the flow of *matter* (biomass downward + air inward + ash outward). The temperature gradient is **inverted** — hottest in the middle, cooler at top and bottom. The producer gas *must* pass through the combustion zone, which is the unique self-cleaning feature of the downdraft design.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Tidal Power Plant Operational Cycles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Tidal Cycle
        H1["HIGH TIDE 1 BASIN FULL"] --> E1["EBB 1 Water flows out through turbines GENERATION"]
        E1 --> L1["LOW TIDE 1 BASIN EMPTY"]
        L1 --> F1["FLOOD 1 Water flows in through sluice gates NO GENERATION"]
        F1 --> H2["HIGH TIDE 2 BASIN FULL"]
        H2 --> E2["EBB 2 Water flows out through turbines GENERATION"]
        E2 --> L2["LOW TIDE 2 BASIN EMPTY"]
        L2 --> F2["FLOOD 2 Water flows in through sluice gates NO GENERATION"]
        F2 --> H1
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-generation-per-day** operational scheme: Power is generated on **both** ebbs (single-effect) OR on both floods and ebbs (two-effect). The Sihwa Lake Tidal Power Station (South Korea, 254 MW) is the world's largest operating tidal facility using a **lake-type (lagoon)** scheme.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Wave Energy Conversion Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    waveIn["OCEAN SURFACE WAVES Wind Driven Gravity Waves"] --> w1["OSCILLATING WATER COLUMN OWC LIMPET type"]
    waveIn --> w2["OVERBOTTOM DEVICES OVERTURNS and TAPCHAN type"]
    waveIn --> w3["POINT ABSORBERS Buoy on float oscillating"]
    waveIn --> w4["TERMINATOR DEVICES PELAMIS attenuator type"]
    w1 --> pt1["AIR TURBINE WELLSTYPE OR WELLS"]
    w2 --> pt2["LOW HEAD TURBINE KAPLAN"]
    w3 --> pt3["LINEAR GENERATOR OR HYDRAULIC MOTOR"]
    w4 --> pt4["HYDRAULIC RAMS AT ARTICULATED JOINTS"]
    pt1 --> gen["ELECTRICAL GENERATOR"]
    pt2 --> gen
    pt3 --> gen
    pt4 --> gen
    gen --> grid["POWER GRID OR LOCAL LOAD"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Fallback Used:** The Pelamis attenuator is a semi-submerged articulated structure aligned with wave direction. The "snake-like" shape converts wave-induced flexing into hydraulic pressure, which drives motors. A block-level functional flow is more readable than a physical 3D drawing.

## 4.4 Comparative Summary Table — Downdraft vs Other Gasifiers

| Feature | Downdraft | Updraft | Fluidized Bed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flow direction** | Co-current (down) | Counter-current (up) | Bubbling/Circulating |
| **Tar in gas** | Very low ($< 50$ mg/Nm³) | Very high ($> 10{,}000$ mg/Nm³) | Moderate ($100$–$1000$ mg/Nm³) |
| **Particle size** | $20$–$100$ mm (uniform) | $10$–$100$ mm | $< 6$ mm (powder) |
| **Moisture tolerance** | Low ($< 20\%$) | High ($< 60\%$) | Moderate ($< 30\%$) |
| **Capacity range** | $5$ kW–$1$ MW | $100$ kW–$10$ MW | $1$ MW–$50$ MW |
| **Application** | Small engines, cooking | Boiler retrofit | Large-scale power |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` 
**Define the Equivalence Ratio (ER) and state its typical range for biomass gasification. Why is $ER < 1$ necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** is defined as the ratio of the actual air supplied to the stoichiometric air required for complete combustion of the biomass:
$$ER = \frac{\text{Actual Air-Fuel Ratio}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio}}$$

For biomass gasification, ER is maintained in the range **$0.20$ to $0.40$**.

**Why $ER < 1$ is required:**
If $ER \geq 1$, the process becomes *complete combustion*, producing only CO₂, H₂O, and ash — no useful combustible gas. A sub-stoichiometric air supply ($ER < 1$) ensures only *partial oxidation* of the biomass, retaining combustible CO and H₂ in the producer gas, which is the desired output fuel.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse "gasification" with "combustion." Gasification is a *partial oxidation* process (ER = 0.2–0.4), NOT complete combustion. Mark deducted if you write $ER \geq 1$.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between Spring Tide and Neap Tide. Why is tidal power generation intermittent despite being predictable?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Spring Tide | Neap Tide |
|---|---|---|
| **Sun–Moon alignment** | Syzygy (aligned, 0° or 180°) | Quadrature (perpendicular, 90°) |
| **Lunar phase** | New moon / Full moon | First / Third quarter |
| **Tidal force** | Constructive interference (Sun + Moon forces add) | Destructive interference (Sun force opposes Moon force) |
| **Tidal range** | Maximum (≈ 1.4× mean) | Minimum (≈ 0.6× mean) |

**Intermittency of tidal power:** Although tides are astronomically predictable, usable head difference exists only for a few hours around each high and low tide. Tidal cycles are 12 h 25 min apart, with **two high + two low tides per day**, leaving slack periods with no head difference. Therefore, generation is **episodic (4 windows/day)** and must be coupled with energy storage (pumped hydro, batteries) for continuous supply.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Don't write "tidal power is unreliable" — it is *predictable* but *intermittent*. These are two different concepts. Marks awarded for distinguishing them.

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Biomass Downdraft Gasifier Focus

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`** *Both parts compulsory. Each part carries 7 marks.*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a Downdraft biomass gasifier. Identify the four zones with their temperature ranges.**

**Model Answer:**

A downdraft gasifier is a vertical, cylindrical, fixed-bed reactor made of mild steel (often with a refractory lining) where biomass and air flow **co-currently downward**.

**Construction features:**
- **Feed hopper** (top) — for charging biomass, often with a screw feeder for air-lock.
- **Refractory-lined shell** — withstands high internal temperatures.
- **Tuyère zone** — nozzles through which primary air enters (positioned at 30–50% of reactor height from the top).
- **Grate / ash bed** (bottom) — supports the biomass and allows ash removal.
- **Gas outlet port** at the bottom (co-current exit).
- **Ash removal system** — rotary or screw type.

**Working and four zones:**

**Zone 1: Drying Zone (100–200°C)** — Top of reactor. Sensible heat from rising hot gases evaporates moisture from biomass.

**Zone 2: Pyrolysis Zone (200–600°C)** — Biomass decomposes into char (solid) and volatiles (gases + tars). Endothermic.

**Zone 3: Combustion Zone (800–1200°C)** — Air from tuyères oxidizes the char. Highly exothermic:
$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$$
$$C + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO$$

**Zone 4: Reduction Zone (600–800°C)** — CO₂ and H₂O are reduced by hot char to form combustible CO and H₂:
$$C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2CO$$
$$C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$$

**Producer gas** exits the bottom with very low tar content because the volatiles have passed *through* the hot combustion zone, where tars are thermally cracked.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A downdraft gasifier is fed with 250 kg/hr of rice husk having moisture content 12% and LHV = 14 MJ/kg. The cold gas efficiency is 72%, and the producer gas LHV is 4.8 MJ/Nm³. Calculate: (i) producer gas flow rate in Nm³/hr, (ii) thermal power output in kW.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Producer gas flow rate:**

Dry biomass feed:
$$\dot{m}_{dry} = 250 \times (1 - 0.12) = 220 \text{ kg/hr}$$

Energy input rate:
$$Q_{in} = 220 \times 14 = 3080 \text{ MJ/hr}$$

Energy output rate:
$$Q_{out} = \eta_{cg} \times Q_{in} = 0.72 \times 3080 = 2217.6 \text{ MJ/hr}$$

Producer gas flow rate:
$$\dot{V}_{gas} = \frac{Q_{out}}{LHV_{gas}} = \frac{2217.6}{4.8} = 462 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr}$$

**(ii) Thermal power output:**

$$P_{out} = \frac{Q_{out}}{3600} = \frac{2217.6 \text{ MJ/hr}}{3600 \text{ s/hr}} = 616 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** A common error is using the *wet* biomass mass (250 kg/hr) directly with LHV = 14 MJ/kg. The LHV is typically given for *dry* biomass. If unsure, state the assumption clearly to earn partial credit.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Tidal & Wave Power Focus (Alternative Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`** *Both parts compulsory. Each part carries 7 marks.*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain with neat diagrams the working of a single-basin tidal barrage power plant. Discuss its advantages and limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

A **tidal barrage** is a dam-like structure built across a tidal estuary, containing sluice gates (for filling/emptying) and bulb turbines (for power generation).

**Single-basin, single-effect operation (generation on ebb only):**

1. **High tide** — Sluice gates open; basin fills with seawater at HTL. No generation.
2. **Falling tide** — Sluice gates close when sea level drops below basin level. The head difference $h$ drives water through **bulb turbines** back to the sea, generating electricity.
3. **Low tide** — Basin empties to LTL. Sluice gates remain closed.
4. **Rising tide** — Sea rises above basin level; sluice gates open to refill the basin. No generation.

**Operating modes:**
- **Ebb-only generation:** Generates during falling tide (one direction).
- **Flood-only generation:** Generates during rising tide.
- **Two-way (ebb + flood) generation:** Generates in both directions — higher capacity factor but more complex.

**Advantages:**
- Predictable, inexhaustible energy source.
- Long plant life ($> 100$ years, no fuel cost).
- No greenhouse gas emissions during operation.
- Useful for coastal flood protection.

**Limitations:**
- High capital cost (concrete barrage across estuary).
- Environmental impact on marine ecology, fish migration, sedimentation.
- Generation is **intermittent** (4 windows/day).
- Requires very large tidal range ($> 5$ m) for economic viability.
- Head variation during generation reduces average efficiency.

**Famous examples:** La Rance (France, 240 MW), Sihwa Lake (South Korea, 254 MW), Annapolis Royal (Canada, 20 MW).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A deep-water wave of significant height 3 m and period 10 s propagates in seawater. Calculate the wave power per unit crest length, the wave energy density, and the group velocity.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $H = 3$ m, $T = 10$ s, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ (seawater), $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1: Wave energy density.**
$$E_d = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 = \frac{1}{8} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times (3)^2$$
$$E_d = \frac{1}{8} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 9$$
$$E_d = \frac{90{,}524.6}{8} = 11{,}315.6 \text{ J/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Phase velocity in deep water.**
$$c = \frac{gT}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 10}{2\pi} = \frac{98.1}{6.283} = 15.61 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Group velocity (deep water = half of phase velocity).**
$$c_g = \frac{c}{2} = \frac{15.61}{2} = 7.81 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Wave power per unit crest length.**
$$P_w = E_d \cdot c_g = 11{,}315.6 \times 7.81 = 88{,}374 \text{ W/m} \approx 88.4 \text{ kW/m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students forget the **factor of ½ in the group velocity** for deep water waves. The group velocity is $c/2$ only in *deep* water ($d/\lambda > 0.5$). In shallow water, $c_g = c$. Read the problem carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize):**

### ⭐ Biomass Gasification — Downdraft
- [x] **Definition:** Partial oxidation of biomass at $ER = 0.20$–$0.40$ to produce producer gas.
- [x] **Four zones (top to bottom):** Drying ($100$–$200°C$) → Pyrolysis ($200$–$600°C$) → Combustion ($800$–$1200°C$) → Reduction ($600$–$800°C$).
- [x] **Key reactions:** Boudouard ($C + CO_2 → 2CO$), Water-gas ($C + H_2O → CO + H_2$), Water-gas shift ($CO + H_2O → CO_2 + H_2$).
- [x] **Advantage:** Lowest tar content ($< 50$ mg/Nm³) because gases pass *through* combustion zone.
- [x] **Cold gas efficiency:** $60$–$75\%$ typical.
- [x] **Producer gas LHV:** $4$–$6$ MJ/Nm³.
- [x] **Restriction:** Cannot handle biomass with $> 20\%$ moisture or fine particles $< 5$ mm.

### ⭐ Tidal Power
- [x] **Driving force:** Gravitational pull of Moon ($67\%$) and Sun ($33\%$).
- [x] **Tidal cycle period:** $12$ h $25$ min (semidiurnal), two full cycles per day.
- [x] **Spring tide:** Syzygy alignment (new/full moon) — max range.
- [x] **Neap tide:** Quadrature (quarter moons) — min range.
- [x] **Energy per cycle:** $E = \frac{1}{2}\rho g A R^2$ (J).
- [x] **Mean power:** $\bar{P} = \frac{\rho g A R^2}{2 T_{cycle}}$ (W).
- [x] **Tidal stream power:** $P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3 C_p$ (Betz limit $C_p = 16/27$).
- [x] **Methods:** Tidal barrage, tidal stream, tidal lagoon.
- [x] **Predictable but intermittent** — 4 generation windows/day.

### ⭐ Wave Power
- [x] **Wave power per unit crest:** $P_w = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{32\pi}$ (W/m).
- [x] **Energy density:** $E_d = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2$ (J/m²).
- [x] **Deep water:** $c = \frac{gT}{2\pi}$, $c_g = \frac{c}{2}$.
- [x] **Shallow water:** $c = \sqrt{gd}$, $c_g = c$.
- [x] **Power ∝ $H^2 \times T$** — long-period, high-amplitude waves are energy-rich.
- [x] **Typical ranges:** $10$–$100$ kW/m of crest.
- [x] **WEC types:** OWC, Overtopping, Point absorber, Attenuator (Pelamis).

### ⭐ Numerical Constants to Memorize
- [x] $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $\rho_{fw} = 1000$ kg/m³
- [x] $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- [x] $T_{cycle} = 12$ h $25$ min $= 44{,}700$ s
- [x] $C_{p,Betz} = 0.593$
- [x] $LHV_{producer\_gas} = 4$–$6$ MJ/Nm³
- [x] $LHV_{wood} \approx 17$–$20$ MJ/kg (dry)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamental Characteristics of Tidal Power, Classification of Tidal Power Plants - Harnessing Tidal Energy, Advantages and Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET412 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fundamental Characteristics of Tidal Power, Classification of Tidal Power Plants - Harnessing Tidal Energy, Advantages and Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fundamental Characteristics of Tidal Power

**Tidal Power** is a renewable form of hydropower that harnesses the kinetic and potential energy of ocean tides — the periodic rise and fall of sea levels caused primarily by the **gravitational interaction between the Earth, Moon, and Sun**. This energy is converted into useful electrical energy through specially designed turbines coupled to generators, typically installed across a barrage, lagoon, or in tidal streams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> Tidal power is the technology of converting the periodic vertical displacement of seawater (tidal range) and the associated horizontal flow (tidal current) into electrical energy using a turbine-generator system installed in a barrage, lagoon, or open-sea configuration.

### 1.2 Key Physical & Astronomical Characteristics

- **Tidal Range (h):** Vertical difference between successive high and low tides, typically **2 m to 12 m** in commercially viable regions.
- **Tidal Period (T):** **12 hours 25 minutes (44,712 s)** for a semi-diurnal cycle (two high + two low tides per lunar day).
- **Spring & Neap Tides:** Maximum and minimum tidal ranges occurring at new/full moon and quarter moon phases, respectively.
- **Spring Tidal Range ≈ 2 × Neap Tidal Range** (approximately).
- **Predictability:** Tides can be predicted accurately **years in advance** (no forecasting uncertainty like wind/solar).
- **Energy Density:** Among the highest of all renewables, since water density (**ρ = 1025 kg/m³**) is ~832× that of air.
- **Site Restriction:** Requires a **minimum tidal range of 5 m** for economic viability, plus a suitable coastal geometry (narrow inlet, large bay).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Breathing Ocean Lung"**
> Imagine the ocean as a giant lung that **breathes in and out twice a day**. Each "inhalation" raises the water level against a coastal wall (a barrage) that traps it like a reservoir. When the trapped water is released back through a turbine, it is similar to water flowing out of a giant dam — except the "dam fill" is performed **automatically by the Moon's gravity**. A tidal power plant is essentially a **dam (barrage) with two-way plumbing**, where the sea itself acts as both the upper and lower reservoir.

### 1.4 Why Tidal Energy Matters in the Energy Mix

| Property | Tidal Power | Solar PV | Wind |
|----------|-------------|----------|------|
| Capacity Factor | 22–28 % | 15–25 % | 25–45 % |
| Predictability | **Excellent (centuries-ahead)** | Moderate | Poor |
| Energy Density | **Highest (ρ = 1025 kg/m³)** | Low | Low |
| Lifecycle (years) | **75–100** | 25–30 | 20–25 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The energy in a tidal system is fundamentally **gravitational potential energy** of the water column trapped behind a barrage. When the tide rises outside the barrage, water is admitted through **sluice gates** into the basin. When the tide falls outside, the head difference across the barrage is exploited through **turbines** to generate electricity.

The core physics rests on three principles:

1. **Continuity of mass flow** through the turbines.
2. **Bernoulli's equation** for the head loss across the turbine.
3. **Conservation of gravitational potential energy** during the filling/emptying cycle.

### 2.2 Operating Sequence of a Tidal Barrage

A single-basin single-effect scheme operates on a **4-phase cycle** (period T = 12.42 h):

1. **Filling Phase:** Sluice gates open at high tide; basin fills by gravity (no generation).
2. **Holding Phase:** Sluice gates close; water held in basin while outside tide falls.
3. **Generation Phase:** Head difference established; turbines run; water flows out to sea.
4. **Waiting Phase:** Basin low; outside tide rising again to start next cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Generation occurs for **only ~4–5 hours per tidal cycle (≈ 8–10 h/day)**, making tidal power **intermittent** despite being predictable.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $E_{\text{pot}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g A h^{2}$ | Potential energy stored in basin per tidal cycle | J |
| 2 | $P_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\rho g A h^{2}}{2T}$ | Average theoretical power per cycle | W |
| 3 | $P_{\text{actual}} = \eta \cdot \dfrac{\rho g A h^{2}}{2T}$ | Power with overall efficiency η | W |
| 4 | $Q = A_b \cdot v$ | Volume flow rate through turbine (A_b = turbine area) | m³/s |
| 5 | $P_{\text{inst}} = \rho g Q H_{\text{net}}$ | Instantaneous turbine power (H_net = net head) | W |
| 6 | $T = 12.42 \text{ h} = 44{,}712 \text{ s}$ | Semi-diurnal tidal period | s |
| 7 | $E_{\text{daily}} = 2 \times E_{\text{pot}}$ | Energy from two tides per day | J/day |
| 8 | $H_{\text{spring}} \approx 2 H_{\text{neap}}$ | Spring vs neap range ratio | m |
| 9 | $E_{\text{kin}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} \cdot t$ | Tidal stream kinetic energy (per unit time) | J |
| 10 | $P_{\text{stream}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$ | Tidal stream power density | W |

**Where:**
- $\rho$ = density of seawater ≈ **1025 kg/m³** (≈ 1000 kg/m³ for fresh)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $A$ = basin area (m²)
- $h$ = tidal range (m)
- $T$ = tidal period = **12.42 h** = **44,712 s**
- $\eta$ = overall efficiency (turbine + generator + transformer), typically **0.75–0.85**
- $H_{\text{net}}$ = net head across turbine (m)
- $v$ = tidal stream velocity (m/s)

## 2.4 Engineering Significance

- Tidal power has the **highest energy density** of all renewables → smaller plant footprint.
- **No fuel cost, no emissions, no ash disposal.**
- The infrastructure (barrage, sluices) doubles as a **bridge or coastal road**, recovering capital cost.
- Plants have a **design life of 75–100 years** vs 20–25 for solar/wind.
- Forms a critical part of the **baseload renewable portfolio** for coastal states (Kerala, Gujarat).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Power Output of a Tidal Basin

### 3.1.1 Potential Energy Stored in the Basin

Consider a rectangular tidal basin of surface area $A$ (m²) and a uniform tidal range $h$ (m) — the difference between high tide and low tide levels.

**Step 1:** At high tide, the basin is filled to a height $h$ above the low tide level. Take the low tide level as the datum (PE = 0).

**Step 2:** The mass of water above the datum at high tide is given by:
$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot h$$

**Step 3:** The **center of mass** of the water column lies at a height of $h/2$ above the datum.

**Step 4:** The gravitational potential energy at high tide (relative to low tide) is:
$$E_{\text{high}} = m \cdot g \cdot \left(\frac{h}{2}\right) = \rho A h \cdot g \cdot \frac{h}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Simplifying yields the fundamental tidal energy equation:
$$E_{\text{pot}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Tidal energy is **quadratic** in tidal range. Doubling the tidal range **quadruples** the available energy — this is why site selection prioritises high tidal range.

### 3.1.2 Average Power per Tidal Cycle

**Step 1:** The energy $E_{\text{pot}}$ is extracted over a time period $T$.

**Step 2:** Average power is therefore:
$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_{\text{pot}}}{T} = \frac{\rho g A h^{2}}{2T}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the semi-diurnal period $T = 12.42 \text{ h} = 44{,}712 \text{ s}$:
$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times A \times h^{2}}{2 \times 44{,}712}$$

**Step 4:** Simplifying the numerical constant:
$$P_{\text{avg}} \approx 0.1124 \cdot A \cdot h^{2} \quad \text{(W, with A in m², h in m)}$$

**Step 5:** With overall efficiency $\eta$:
$$P_{\text{actual}} = \eta \cdot \frac{\rho g A h^{2}}{2T}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU University Question)

**Problem:** A single-basin tidal power plant has a basin area of **10 km²** and a mean tidal range of **7 m**. The overall efficiency of the turbine-generator system is **82%**. Calculate:
- (a) The energy available per tidal cycle.
- (b) The average power output per cycle.
- (c) The total energy generated per day (two cycles).

**Given:**
- $A = 10 \text{ km}^2 = 10 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2 = 10^7 \text{ m}^2$
- $h = 7 \text{ m}$
- $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $\eta = 0.82$
- $T = 12.42 \text{ h} = 44{,}712 \text{ s}$

### Solution

**Part (a): Energy per tidal cycle**

$$E_{\text{pot}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^{2}$$

Substituting the values:

$$E_{\text{pot}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 10^{7} \times (7)^{2}$$

$$E_{\text{pot}} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 10^{7} \times 49$$

$$E_{\text{pot}} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 49 \times 10^{7}$$

Computing step-by-step:
- $0.5 \times 1025 = 512.5$
- $512.5 \times 9.81 = 5028.525$
- $5028.525 \times 49 = 246{,}397.725$
- $246{,}397.725 \times 10^{7} = 2.464 \times 10^{12} \text{ J}$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{pot}} \approx 2.464 \times 10^{12} \text{ J per tide}}$$

**Part (b): Average power per cycle**

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_{\text{pot}}}{T} = \frac{2.464 \times 10^{12}}{44{,}712}$$

$$P_{\text{avg}} = 5.51 \times 10^{7} \text{ W} = 55.1 \text{ MW (theoretical)}$$

With efficiency:
$$P_{\text{actual}} = 0.82 \times 55.1 = 45.18 \text{ MW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{actual}} \approx 45.2 \text{ MW}}$$

**Part (c): Daily energy (2 cycles)**

$$E_{\text{daily}} = 2 \times E_{\text{pot}} \times \eta = 2 \times 2.464 \times 10^{12} \times 0.82$$

$$E_{\text{daily}} = 4.041 \times 10^{12} \text{ J/day}$$

Converting to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J):

$$E_{\text{daily}} = \frac{4.041 \times 10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} = 1.122 \times 10^{6} \text{ kWh/day}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{daily}} \approx 1.122 \times 10^{6} \text{ kWh/day} \approx 1122 \text{ MWh/day}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capacity Factor Note:** Although installed capacity is ~45 MW, the plant generates only during ~8 h of 24 h → effective capacity factor ≈ **33 %**, typical for tidal plants.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Single-Basin vs Double-Basin

| Parameter | Single-Basin | Double-Basin |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| Generation continuity | Intermittent (4–5 h/cycle) | Near-continuous (improved dispatchability) |
| Civil works cost | Lower | Higher (two barrages) |
| Energy output | Higher per cycle | Lower per cycle (split between two basins) |
| Operational complexity | Simple | Complex (synchronised control) |
| Example | La Rance (France) | Proposed Severn (UK, not built) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**Define tidal range. What is the minimum tidal range required for the economic viability of a tidal power plant?**

**Model Answer:**
Tidal range is the **vertical difference between the successive high tide and low tide levels** at a given location, typically expressed in metres. The economic viability of a tidal power plant requires a minimum tidal range of **5 m to 6 m** (some sources cite 7 m). Below this, the head differential becomes insufficient to drive commercial turbines and the cost per unit energy becomes uncompetitive. Sites with the highest tidal ranges globally include the Bay of Fundy (Canada) at ~16 m, and the Bristol Channel (UK) at ~14 m.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**List any three major advantages of tidal power over solar photovoltaic power.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Predictability:** Tides can be predicted centuries in advance with high accuracy, whereas solar PV is subject to daily and seasonal variability.
2. **Higher Energy Density:** The density of seawater (1025 kg/m³) is ~832× that of air, allowing a tidal plant to extract far more power per unit swept area.
3. **Longer Plant Life:** Tidal plants have a design life of **75–100 years**, compared to **25–30 years** for solar PV installations.
4. *(Bonus)* Tidal plants can also serve as bridges/causeways, providing ancillary infrastructure value.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **average power output of a single-basin tidal power plant** in terms of basin area, tidal range, and tidal period. State clearly the meaning of each variable. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A tidal power plant has a basin area of **15 km²** and an average tidal range of **8 m**. If the overall efficiency of the turbine-generator-transformer system is **85%**, calculate: **(i)** the energy generated per tide, **(ii)** the average power per tidal cycle, and **(iii)** the daily energy output in kWh. **(7 Marks)**

### Question A — Model Solution

**Part (a): Derivation**

Refer to the **exhaustive derivation in Section 3.1.1 and 3.1.2** of these notes. Key result:
$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\rho g A h^{2}}{2T}$$

where $\rho$ = density of seawater (1025 kg/m³), $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $A$ = basin area (m²), $h$ = tidal range (m), $T$ = tidal period (44,712 s).

**Part (b): Numerical Solution**

**Given:** $A = 15 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$, $h = 8 \text{ m}$, $\eta = 0.85$, $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $T = 44{,}712 \text{ s}$.

**(i) Energy per tide:**
$$E_{\text{pot}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^{2} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 15 \times 10^6 \times 64$$
$$E_{\text{pot}} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 15 \times 64 \times 10^6$$
$$E_{\text{pot}} = 4.825 \times 10^{12} \text{ J per tide}$$

**(ii) Average power per cycle:**
$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_{\text{pot}}}{T} = \frac{4.825 \times 10^{12}}{44{,}712} = 1.079 \times 10^8 \text{ W (theoretical)}$$

With efficiency:
$$P_{\text{actual}} = 0.85 \times 107.9 = 91.7 \text{ MW}$$

**(iii) Daily energy (2 tides):**
$$E_{\text{daily}} = 2 \times 0.85 \times 4.825 \times 10^{12} = 8.203 \times 10^{12} \text{ J/day}$$
$$E_{\text{daily}} = \frac{8.203 \times 10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 2.278 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh/day} = 2278 \text{ MWh/day}$$

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain with neat block diagrams the **classification of tidal power plants** and briefly describe the working of a **single-basin single-effect tidal power plant**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **major advantages and limitations** of tidal power generation. Compare tidal energy with wind energy on the basis of energy density and predictability. **(7 Marks)**

### Question B — Model Solution

**Part (a): Classification and Working**

Classification (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):
- **Barrage type** — single basin (single effect, double effect); double basin.
- **Tidal stream type** — horizontal axis, vertical axis, open-sea arrays.
- **Tidal lagoon type** — coastal enclosed, offshore artificial.

**Working of Single-Basin Single-Effect (Ebb Generation):**
1. At **high tide**, sluice gates open → basin fills (no generation).
2. As the **outside tide falls**, the head difference builds up across the barrage.
3. When the head reaches a **threshold (3–4 m)**, turbines start generating — water flows from basin to sea.
4. Generation continues until the basin level equals the outside level (low tide).
5. Sluice gates close; cycle repeats every **12.42 h**.

Diagrams: see Section 4.2 and 4.3.

**Part (b): Advantages, Limitations & Comparison**

**Advantages:**
- Highly **predictable** (lunar cycles, centuries in advance).
- **Zero fuel cost** and **zero carbon emissions** during operation.
- **High energy density** of seawater (1025 kg/m³).
- **Long lifespan** (75–100 years); no fuel transportation/storage.
- Acts as a **bridge/road** infrastructure.
- **No waste products** (ash, radiation).

**Limitations:**
- **High capital cost** (barrage, civil works, marine-grade turbines).
- **Limited suitable sites** worldwide (~20–30 economically viable sites).
- **Environmental impact**: alters marine ecosystems, sediment flow, fish migration.
- **Intermittent** (generates ~8–10 h/day, requiring backup/grid integration).
- **Silting** of basin reduces capacity over time.
- **Affects navigation** unless expensive ship locks are provided.
- Potential **stochastic tidal harmonics** may reduce output.

**Tidal vs Wind Comparison:**

| Parameter | Tidal | Wind |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Energy Density (W/m²) | **~10,000** | ~400 |
| Predictability | **Centuries in advance** | Hours–days in advance |
| Capacity Factor | 22–28 % | 25–45 % |
| Visual/Aesthetic impact | High (barrage) | Moderate (turbines) |
| Capacity density per m² of plant | **~25× wind** | baseline |

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting efficiency (η):** A 7-mark numerical question will deduct **2–3 marks** if efficiency is not applied to compute *actual* power/energy. Always state the efficiency and multiply.
> 2. **Using 12 h instead of 12.42 h:** The KTU expected answer uses **T = 12.42 h = 44,712 s** for the semi-diurnal period. Using 12 h leads to a 3.5% error.
> 3. **Using ρ = 1000 kg/m³:** The syllabus specifies **seawater density = 1025 kg/m³**. Using fresh water density loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing single-basin single-effect and double-effect:** Single-effect generates on **one direction only** (ebb OR flood); double-effect generates on **both** directions. Mixing up the cycle timing loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **In Part B derivations, skipping the "Why" of $h/2$:** Always state that the **center of mass** of the water column of height $h$ lies at $h/2$ above the datum.
> 6. **Units confusion:** Convert km² → m² **before** substituting; convert final J → kWh by dividing by **3.6 × 10⁶**, not 3600.
> 7. **Missing the daily energy factor:** A full-day calculation requires **2 tides/day**, not 1.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Tidal power** is a **predictable, high-density** form of ocean energy based on the gravitational PE of seawater.
- The fundamental energy equation is $E = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho g A h^{2}$ — energy is **quadratic in tidal range**.
- **Minimum economic tidal range:** **5–7 m**.
- **Tidal period (T):** **12.42 h (44,712 s)** for semi-diurnal.
- **Seawater density:** **1025 kg/m³** (must be used, not 1000).
- **Classification:** Barrage (single/double basin), Tidal Stream, Tidal Lagoon.
- **Single-basin single-effect** generates on ebb or flood only (~4 h/cycle).
- **Single-basin double-effect** generates on both ebb and flood (~6–8 h/cycle).
- **Double-basin** enables near-continuous generation but at higher cost and lower per-cycle energy.
- **Turbines used:** Bulb, Straflo, and Rim generators (low-head, large-diameter, bidirectional).
- **Capacity factor:** **22–28 %** (generation limited to 8–10 h/day).
- **Major advantages:** Predictability, zero emissions, long life, high energy density, dual-use as bridge.
- **Major limitations:** High capital cost, limited sites, environmental impact, intermittency, silting, navigation.
- **Sluice gates** are essential for **gravity filling** (no energy cost) — a key cost-saving feature.
- **Spring tides** provide maximum output; **neap tides** give minimum output.
- **Energy in kWh** = Energy in Joules ÷ **3.6 × 10⁶**.
- **Tidal energy density is ~25× wind energy density** per unit area — a key KTU comparison point.
- The **single most important KTU formula** to memorise: $P = \dfrac{\rho g A h^{2}}{2T}$ — practice its derivation at least 3 times before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
